name: Agentic DevOps research and slide deck interation
summary:
**Conversation overview**

This conversation is part of a multi-session project creating webinar collateral for KodeKloud's April 2026 webinar titled "Beyond Copilots: How Agentic AI is Rewriting the DevOps Playbook," targeting mid-to-senior DevOps and Platform Engineers in EU/US markets. The session focused on two main tasks: correcting the relationship between the webinar outline and the slide deck, then researching and integrating KodeKloud's current course offerings into the outline.

The person made an important clarification early in the session: the finished 31-slide PowerPoint deck at `/mnt/user-data/outputs/Agentic-DevOps-Playbook-KodeKloud.pptx` is the source of truth, not the webinar outline. The previous outline had been written independently and diverged from the slides in structure, section framing, and content. Claude rewrote the outline from scratch to match the deck's actual eight-part structure: intro hook (December 2025 cloud platform convergence), What Is Agentic DevOps, What's Real Right Now, The Role Shift, and the 90-Day Playbook. The person confirmed the deck should not be changed to match the outline. When asked at the end whether the outline was "best for the PowerPoint presentation," Claude clarified that the deck is already complete and the outline serves as a speaker companion document, not a build source.

The second task involved researching KodeKloud's current agentic DevOps course offerings to weave accurate course references and links into the outline. The person communicated via voice-to-text with phonetic approximations (e.g., "KH" for KAgent, "did a dog by migration" for Datadog, "get her Cobalt" for GitHub Copilot, "cod code judge EBT" for Claude Code). Claude searched kodekloud.com directly rather than guessing, confirming 15+ relevant courses including KAgent: Host Your AI Agents on Kubernetes (`/courses/kagents-host-your-ai-agents-on-kubernetes`), Crash Course: AI-Powered DevOps, MCP For Beginners, Building AI Agents with Claude Agent SDK, Claude Code For Beginners, GitHub Copilot Certification, GitOps with ArgoCD, Migrating to Datadog, Introduction to K8sGPT, LangChain, Cline, n8n, and others. The person's communication style throughout was direct and efficiency-focused, explicitly pushing back when Claude flagged uncertainty about K8sGPT rather than searching first. Course references were added in two locations in the outline: a callout block after the tool landscape table in Section 2, and a lookup table in the closing CTA section. The updated outline was saved to `/mnt/user-data/outputs/Webinar-Outline-Beyond-Copilots-Agentic-DevOps.md`.

**Tool knowledge**

For kodekloud.com course discovery, `site:kodekloud.com [tool-name] course` web searches reliably surfaced confirmed course URLs in `/courses/[slug]` format. The KAgent course URL pattern differed from the tool's common name: the course slug is `kagents-host-your-ai-agents-on-kubernetes` (plural "kagents"), not `kagent`. The KodeKloud recent courses page at `kodekloud.com/recent-courses` and the AI learning path at `kodekloud.com/learning-path/ai` were reliable discovery surfaces for new releases. Course notes at `notes.kodekloud.com/docs/[Course-Name]/` provided curriculum detail when course pages themselves were sparse. Google Drive searches for KodeKloud content were unproductive; direct web search against the domain was consistently more reliable for course catalog research.

{"language": "markdown", "code": "# Agentic DevOps: The Playbook for Working Engineers
## KodeKloud Live Webinar — Slide Content & Speaker Notes
### 50 Minutes Presentation + 10 Minutes Q&A

**Design Theme:** Midnight Executive — Navy `#1E2761` dominant, Ice Blue `#CADCFC` supporting, White `#FFFFFF` accent
**Typography:** Trebuchet MS headers / Calibri body
**Visual Motif:** Numbered section badges (colored circles) + stat callout boxes throughout

> **SPEAKER NOTE CONVENTION**
> - `[TIMING]` = cumulative clock from session start
> - `[PAUSE]` = intentional beat — wait for it to land
> - `[CLICK]` = advance animation or build
> - Bold text = emphasis in delivery
> - *Italic text* = tone/delivery direction

---

## PRE-SESSION (Before go-live)

**Slide 0 — Title Hold Screen**

**ON SCREEN:**
```
AGENTIC DEVOPS
The Playbook for Working Engineers

Live Session | April 2026 | KodeKloud

[KodeKloud logo]
```

**SPEAKER NOTES:**
*[Play holding music or ambient screen. This is the pre-roll while people join.]*

Have your outline open in a second window. Know your five section transitions cold — you should be able to state the transition sentence for each section without looking at notes.

Water nearby. Chat window open if you're monitoring it.

Check: mic levels, camera framing, slide advancer working, backup slides loaded.

---

## SECTION 1 — THE SIGNAL IN THE NOISE
*Target: 5 minutes | Cumulative: 0:00–5:00*

---

**Slide 1 — Title Slide**

**LAYOUT:** Dark full-bleed background (navy `#1E2761`). Large centered title. Subtitle below. Speaker name bottom left. KodeKloud branding bottom right.

**ON SCREEN:**
```
AGENTIC DEVOPS
The Playbook for Working Engineers

[Your Name] | Senior Principal Trainer & DevOps Advocate
KodeKloud | April 2026
```

**SPEAKER NOTES `[TIMING: 0:00]`**

*[Open with energy — this is the first 30 seconds and it sets the tone for 60 minutes]*

"Before we get into frameworks and tools, I want to start with something that happened two months ago that stopped me mid-scroll."

"Anthropic's Claude Code — their agentic coding tool — is now writing **100% of Anthropic's own code**. Not assisting. Writing."

*[PAUSE — let that register]*

"And in the same week that went public, Boris Cherny — the Head of Claude Code — replied to the obvious follow-up question: 'So why does Anthropic still have 100 open engineering positions?'"

"His answer was: **someone has to prompt the models, talk to customers, coordinate teams, and decide what to build next.**"

*[PAUSE]*

"That's not a reassurance. That's a job description. And it looks a lot like what strong DevOps engineers already do."

"That's what the next 50 minutes is about. Not 'is AI taking your job.' That question is already answered. The real question is: **what does your job look like on the other side of this shift — and what do you need to build to get there?**"

*[Advance slide]*

---

**Slide 2 — The Week Everything Changed**

**LAYOUT:** Dark background. Large headline. Three stat callout boxes in a row below — each with a big number/stat and a one-line description. Source attribution in small text.

**ON SCREEN:**
```
ONE WEEK. DECEMBER 2025.

[BOX 1]          [BOX 2]          [BOX 3]
AWS              MICROSOFT        GOOGLE
DevOps Agent     SRE Agent        ADK Expansion
Public Preview   Preview          +4 Major Tools

All three shipped autonomous DevOps agents in the same week at re:Invent and Azure/Google events.
```

**SPEAKER NOTES `[TIMING: 1:00]`**

"I want to give you a single data point to calibrate how fast this is moving."

"In the first week of December 2025, at AWS re:Invent, Microsoft Azure, and Google Cloud events — all three shipped autonomous DevOps agents. In the same week."

"Not AI assistants. Agents. Systems that can autonomously investigate incidents, analyze CI failures, propose infrastructure changes — and in some configurations, execute them."

"That's not a coincidence. That's a race. And every major cloud platform has decided that **agentic operations is the next platform layer** they're competing on."

"Which means your infrastructure — **your Kubernetes clusters, your CI/CD pipelines, your observability stack** — is about to become the runtime that these agents operate on."

*[PAUSE]*

"If you're a DevOps engineer who understands those systems, you are not obsolete. You are the foundation."

*[Advance slide]*

---

**Slide 3 — The Honest Numbers**

**LAYOUT:** Light section — off-white background. Five large stat callouts in a 2x3 grid (last one spanning full width). Each stat is 60pt number, label below in 14pt.

**ON SCREEN:**
```
WHAT'S ACTUALLY HAPPENING RIGHT NOW

1,000,000+          46%                4.6x
PRs created by      of active          longer AI-generated
GitHub Copilot      Copilot users'     PRs wait for review
autonomously        code is AI-gen     vs. human PRs
(May–Sept 2025)

2,000               90%
Production envs     of Fortune 100
where Datadog's     companies now
AI SRE Agent        using GitHub
is running          Copilot

Source: GitHub, Opsera 2026, Datadog
```

**SPEAKER NOTES `[TIMING: 2:00]`**

"Let me give you the numbers that matter — and I'm going to be specific about what they actually mean."

"GitHub Copilot's Coding Agent has created **over one million pull requests autonomously** since May 2025. That's not people using a code suggestion tool. That's an agent receiving an issue ticket and delivering a PR — without a human writing a single line."

*[CLICK to reveal the 46% stat]*

"**46% of active Copilot users' code is now AI-generated.** Not suggested — generated. That number will be higher by the time this webinar recording hits our library."

*[CLICK to reveal the 4.6x stat]*

"Here's the number that tells you the real story: those AI-generated PRs wait **4.6 times longer** to be reviewed than human-written PRs."

*[PAUSE]*

"Think about what that means. We solved the code-writing bottleneck with AI — and immediately created a new bottleneck in the **outer loop**. Review, testing, deployment, incident response."

"That outer loop — **that's where DevOps engineers live.** And that's exactly where agentic AI is moving next."

*[Advance slide]*

---

**Slide 4 — What This Webinar Is (and Isn't)**

**LAYOUT:** Two-column. Left: "What this IS" with three bullet points in icon + text rows. Right: "What this ISN'T" with three bullet points in icon + text rows. Clean dividing line between columns.

**ON SCREEN:**
```
THIS WEBINAR

WHAT THIS IS                    WHAT THIS ISN'T
✓ A decision framework          ✗ A tools demo
  for working engineers
                                ✗ Hype about what's
✓ Grounded in what's              possible in 2030
  confirmed in production
  right now                     ✗ A message that
                                  your skills are
✓ A 90-day plan you               becoming worthless
  can start Monday
```

**SPEAKER NOTES `[TIMING: 3:30]`**

"Before we go further, let me tell you exactly what you're going to get and what you're not."

"This is **a decision framework**. By the end you'll know what agentic DevOps actually is, where the real use cases are, what skills you need to build, and — critically — a 90-day plan that starts with one concrete action."

"This is **not a vendor demo**. I'm going to show you what's confirmed in production at companies like Elastic, Datadog, and Block — not what a vendor says is possible with their product."

< truncated lines 196-1174 >

"By day 90, here's what you've built: a proven agentic workflow in production, an eval suite, observability dashboards, documented IAM policies, and the data to make the case for going further."

"That's Level 2 on the maturity curve. IDC says most organizations are at Level 1. **Day 90, you're ahead of 85% of the industry.** And you got there without a breach and without a failed pilot."

*[Advance slide]*

---

## SECTION 6 — CLOSING
*Target: 5 minutes | Cumulative: 45:00–50:00*

---

**Slide 28 — The Real Opportunity**

**LAYOUT:** Dark background. Left column: "Your existing skills + the new layer." Right column: stacked callout boxes showing the connection. Large statement at bottom.

**ON SCREEN:**
```
YOU ARE ALREADY POSITIONED

Your Kubernetes knowledge      =  The runtime agents operate on
Your CI/CD expertise           =  The pipeline agents are embedded in
Your observability skills      =  The only way to govern agents
Your security knowledge        =  The boundary design language

The engineers defining what Agentic DevOps looks like
are working on it right now.
Not in 2028 when there's a certification for it.
Now.

The question isn't whether you're capable.
The question is whether you start in the next 90 days.
```

**SPEAKER NOTES `[TIMING: 45:00]`**

"I want to close with what I actually believe about this moment — and I'm going to be direct."

"The DORA data says: AI amplifies what's already there. If you're a DevOps engineer with strong foundations — Kubernetes, CI/CD, observability, security — you are exactly the person agentic systems need."

*[PAUSE]*

"Your Kubernetes knowledge is the runtime. Your CI/CD expertise is the deployment layer. Your observability skills are the governance mechanism. Your security knowledge is the boundary design language."

"None of this is obsolete. It's the foundation."

*[PAUSE]*

"But here's the uncomfortable truth about timing: the engineers who define what Agentic DevOps looks like — who write the patterns, set the governance standards, build the internal platforms — **are working on it right now.** Not in two years when there's a KodeKloud certification track for it. Now."

"You're not behind. But you don't have unlimited runway to think about it."

*[Advance slide]*

---

**Slide 29 — One Action Before You Leave**

**LAYOUT:** Dark background. Large centered text. Single action item in accent box. Bold and minimal — this is meant to be the last thing they see before Q&A.

**ON SCREEN:**
```
ONE ACTION BEFORE YOU LEAVE

Go to your CI pipeline.
Find the last 5 build failures.
Ask yourself:

Could an agent have analyzed these and recommended a fix —
with me approving the commit?

If yes — you have your first use case.
That's your day one.

[Registration/Resource CTA]
[KodeKloud logo]
```

**SPEAKER NOTES `[TIMING: 47:00]`**

"I want to give you one specific action — not a list of things to read, not a framework to implement. One thing."

*[Read the slide slowly]*

"Go to your CI pipeline. Find the last 5 build failures. Look at the error messages. Ask yourself: could an agent have analyzed these and recommended a fix, with me approving the commit?"

*[PAUSE]*

"If the answer is yes — you just found your first agentic use case. That's your day one of the 90-day plan."

"If the answer is no — look at why. Is it because the errors are ambiguous? Because the fix requires architectural knowledge? Those are the boundaries you need to design around. And that's still useful information."

*[PAUSE]*

"We have the resource list and the full 90-day playbook in the chat — I'll drop that in now. And I want to open up for questions. Let's talk."

*[Advance slide]*

---

## Q&A — 10 Minutes
*Cumulative: 50:00–60:00*

---

**Slide 30 — Q&A Slide**

**LAYOUT:** Dark background. "Q&A" large centered. Timer visual (optional). Resource links. Session wrap note.

**ON SCREEN:**
```
Q&A

10 Minutes

Resources dropped in chat:
• 90-Day Agentic DevOps Playbook (PDF)
• Webinar recording link (available within 48 hrs)
• KodeKloud Agentic DevOps learning path

[KodeKloud logo + social handles]
```

**ANTICIPATED QUESTIONS & HONEST ANSWERS:**

---

**Q: Isn't this going to eliminate junior DevOps roles?**

*Delivery: Acknowledge the real concern, give the honest answer, pivot to what to do about it.*

"Some entry-level tasks are being absorbed — yes. The same way automation absorbed some tasks in the first DevOps wave. What I see happening is that junior engineers who learn to supervise, evaluate, and tune agents grow faster than they would in a traditional role. The floor of the skill requirement is rising. That's real. The answer is building the skills we talked about today."

---

**Q: Which tools should I actually learn first?**

"Pick one and go deep. Don't try to learn all of them at once. If you're building custom workflows: LangGraph. If you want a tool-first approach: Claude Code or GitHub Copilot Coding Agent. If you want infrastructure focus: learn MCP and build a server for your most-used tool. One skill, one depth, one proof of concept. Then expand."

---

**Q: What about the security risks — our security team will never approve this.**

"Your security team is right to be skeptical. Use that. Go to them with the boundary design framework — show them the permission table with exactly what the agent can read, write, execute, and where humans must approve. That's a conversation they know how to have. The teams that are succeeding aren't asking permission to deploy autonomous agents. They're asking permission to deploy read-only advisory agents with documented IAM policies. Very different ask."

---

**Q: Our org isn't going to let agents touch production. Is this still relevant?**

"Completely relevant. Start in dev and staging. Build your eval suite. Run the agent in advisory mode against real CI failures but in a non-production environment. Generate the data. Then you have evidence — not a pitch — when you make the case to leadership. The teams that have the most success navigating organizational resistance are the ones who showed up with eval data, not enthusiasm."

---

**Q: How do I explain this to my manager / get buy-in?**

"Don't use the word 'agentic' in your first conversation. Say: 'I want to pilot an AI assistant that analyzes our CI failures and recommends fixes for engineer review.' That's a much easier conversation than 'I want to deploy autonomous agents to our pipeline.' Same technical outcome. Very different organizational reaction. Start with advisory mode, generate the data, and let the data make the case for autonomy."

---

## TIMING SUMMARY

| Section | Content | Target Time | Cumulative |
|---------|---------|-------------|------------|
| Pre | Title Hold | — | — |
| 1 | Signal in the Noise | 5:00 | 0:00–5:00 |
| 2 | What Is Agentic DevOps | 8:00 | 5:00–13:00 |
| 3 | What's Real Right Now | 10:00 | 13:00–23:00 |
| 4 | The Role Shift | 12:00 | 23:00–35:00 |
| 5 | 90-Day Playbook | 10:00 | 35:00–45:00 |
| 6 | Closing | 5:00 | 45:00–50:00 |
| Q&A | Questions | 10:00 | 50:00–60:00 |
| **Total** | | **60:00** | |

---

## SLIDE COUNT: 30 slides (including title hold and Q&A)
## AVERAGE: 1.7 minutes per slide — pacing is sustainable

---

## CRITICAL DELIVERY NOTES

1. **Section 3 (What's Real) is your credibility engine.** Do not rush the Elastic case study. Practitioners in the audience will know if you're hand-waving.

2. **The DORA slide (Slide 15) is the most important data credibility moment.** Say "DORA" not "a study" — the audience knows DORA.

3. **Never say timing numbers out loud.** All timing guidance lives in these notes only.

4. **The 70% decision gate (Slide 26) will generate Q&A.** Be ready to defend it. The number comes from general recommendation system quality thresholds — not a published agentic AI standard. If pushed, say "it's a starting benchmark; tune it to your organization's risk tolerance."

5. **If you're running long by Section 4:** compress Slides 20–22 (skills 3–5) to higher-level bullet summaries. The 5 skills can become "here are five skills, I'll highlight the two most important — context engineering and boundary design." The other three survive as reference material in the playbook handout.

6. **The closing (Slide 29) should feel personal.** Don't rush it. This is the moment the audience decides whether they act on this.

{"language": "markdown", "code": "
"And this is absolutely **not** a message that your Kubernetes skills, your CI/CD expertise, your observability knowledge are becoming worthless. They're the foundation agents run on. We're going to show you exactly what that means."

*[PAUSE]*

"Let's get into it."

*[Advance slide]*

---

## SECTION 2 — WHAT IS AGENTIC DEVOPS, ACTUALLY
*Target: 8 minutes | Cumulative: 5:00–13:00*

---

**Slide 5 — Section Header**

**LAYOUT:** Dark full-bleed background. Large section number badge (circle with "2"). Section title centered. One-line description below.

**ON SCREEN:**
```
  2

WHAT IS AGENTIC DEVOPS, ACTUALLY

Cutting through the noise with a definition that sticks
```

**SPEAKER NOTES `[TIMING: 5:00]`**

"The phrase 'agentic AI' is everywhere right now. Before we can talk about what to do about it, we need a definition that's actually useful — not marketing language."

*[Advance slide]*

---

**Slide 6 — The Three Waves**

**LAYOUT:** Three-column layout. Each column is a "wave card" with a wave number, title, dates, visual description, and status badge. Wave 3 card is slightly highlighted (brighter border or accent).

**ON SCREEN:**
```
THREE WAVES OF AI IN DEVOPS

WAVE 1              WAVE 2              WAVE 3
Pair Programmer     AI-Assisted         Agentic
2021–2023           2023–2025           2025–Present

Code completion     Chat help,          Autonomous agents
in your IDE.        code review,        operating for
You're present      vulnerability       hours without
for every           scanning.           oversight.
keystroke.          Reactive.

Where are           WHERE MOST          WHERE THIS
you now?            TEAMS ARE TODAY     IS GOING
```

**SPEAKER NOTES `[TIMING: 5:30]`**

"There are three waves. Most people conflate all three — which is why there's so much confusion about what 'AI in DevOps' actually means."

"**Wave 1** was the original GitHub Copilot. Code completion. You're sitting in your IDE, it's making suggestions, you decide what to keep. Every action still has a human in the loop."

*[CLICK]*

"**Wave 2** is where most teams are today. Chat-based assistance, code review suggestions, vulnerability scanning. Still reactive — you ask, it answers. Still human-present."

*[CLICK]*

"**Wave 3 is what we're talking about today.** Autonomous agents that receive a goal — 'fix this broken CI build,' 'investigate this incident,' 'update these dependencies' — and operate independently for hours. They plan, execute, iterate, and hand off to other agents."

*[PAUSE]*

"The reason this distinction matters: most of the panic about 'AI taking DevOps jobs' is people applying Wave 3 fears to Wave 2 reality. **Wave 2 doesn't restructure your job. Wave 3 does.** And Wave 3 is here — but it's early, and you have time to get positioned."

*[Advance slide]*

---

**Slide 7 — Inner Loop vs. Outer Loop**

**LAYOUT:** Full-width horizontal diagram. Left side: "Inner Loop" (smaller circle, lighter color) with items inside. Right side: "Outer Loop" (larger circle, darker/accent color) with items inside. Arrow connecting them with "AI moved here →" label.

**ON SCREEN:**
```
WHERE THE BOTTLENECK MOVED

INNER LOOP                      OUTER LOOP
(Where AI started)              (Where AI is going)

• Code writing                  • Code review
• Code completion               • PR approval
• Test generation               • CI/CD pipeline management
                                • Deployment
                                ← The new              • Monitoring & alerting
                                   bottleneck           • Incident response
                                                       • Runbook execution

AI-generated PRs created 48% faster.
Those same PRs waited 4.6x longer to be reviewed. (Opsera, 2026)
```

**SPEAKER NOTES `[TIMING: 7:00]`**

"This diagram is the most important thing I'll show you today. Hold it in your head."

"AI tools solved the **inner loop** — the time it takes to write code. GitHub Copilot, Claude Code, Cursor — all of these tools massively accelerated what happens inside the IDE."

"**And immediately created a bigger problem in the outer loop.**"

"Opsera measured this in February 2026: AI-assisted workflows produced PRs **48% faster**. But those PRs waited **4.6 times longer** to be reviewed. They also contained more code duplication and 15–18% more security vulnerabilities than human-written PRs."

*[PAUSE]*

"We optimized one end of the pipeline — and created a new constraint everywhere else."

"The outer loop is: code review, CI/CD management, deployment orchestration, monitoring, incident response, runbook execution. That is the **DevOps engineer's domain.** Every single item on that list."

*[PAUSE]*

"Agentic DevOps is the industry's response to this. Not 'let's replace DevOps engineers.' **Let's give them AI agents that handle the outer loop at scale.**"

*[Advance slide]*

---

**Slide 8 — The Honest Definition**

**LAYOUT:** Dark background. Large centered quote-style text box with the definition. Supporting context below in smaller text. Clean and minimal.

**ON SCREEN:**
```
WHAT AGENTIC DEVOPS ACTUALLY IS

"Autonomous, goal-driven AI systems operating within your
existing infrastructure — Kubernetes, CI/CD, observability
tooling — that can plan, execute, and iterate on multi-step
tasks without constant human direction."

The key words: WITHIN YOUR EXISTING INFRASTRUCTURE.
Agents don't replace your stack. They run on it.
```

**SPEAKER NOTES `[TIMING: 9:00]`**

"Here's the definition I want you to take from this webinar."

*[Read it slowly — let it land]*

"The phrase I want you to focus on: **within your existing infrastructure.** Not a new system you have to build. Not a separate AI environment."

"Agents run **on Kubernetes**. They deploy **through your CI/CD pipelines**. They're monitored **with your observability tools**. They get their context from **your logs, your metrics, your runbooks.**"

"What this means for you: the more deeply you understand your existing infrastructure — the more value you bring to designing and operating these systems."

*[PAUSE]*

"Now let me also tell you what it's not."

*[Advance slide]*

---

**Slide 9 — The Risk You Need to Name**

**LAYOUT:** Two-column. Left: "The opportunity" (3 brief points). Right: "The real risk" (3 brief points, with a Forrester callout box). Red/amber accent on the right side to visually signal risk.

"First: **build your eval suite before the agent is deployed.** Not after. Your 10 most common CI failure scenarios become your eval test cases."

"Second: **define permissions explicitly.** Create a separate service principal for the agent with read-only access. Document the IAM policy. Put it in your AGENTS.md file."

"Third: **form your Agentic AI Pod** — 2 or 3 engineers who own this. Someone needs to be accountable for the eval suite, the observability, and the governance documentation."

*[Advance slide]*

---

**Slide 26 — Days 31–60: Validation**

**LAYOUT:** Timeline-style. "Days 31-60" header. Three metric boxes with checkmarks. Decision gate graphic in center. "If ready" vs "If not ready" branching paths.

**ON SCREEN:**
```
DAYS 31–60: VALIDATION

AGENTS IN ADVISORY MODE ONLY. THEY RECOMMEND. HUMANS APPROVE.

ADD OBSERVABILITY FIRST:         TRACK THESE METRICS:
Datadog LLM Observability        • Agent recommendation accuracy
New Relic AI Monitoring          • False positive rate
Grafana (GA Oct 2025)            • Time saved vs. review time
Langfuse (open source)           • Human override frequency

             DECISION GATE AT DAY 60:

     ≥70% human agreement         < 70% human agreement
     with agent recommendations   with recommendations
              ↓                            ↓
       Ready to expand             Tune context (not prompts)
                                   Stay in advisory mode
```

**SPEAKER NOTES `[TIMING: 38:00]`**

"Days 31 through 60. Your goal: generate enough data to make a real decision about expanding."

"Agents are still in **advisory mode only** during this phase. They surface recommendations, humans decide."

"Your first action in this phase: **add observability before you add anything else.** Pick one of these tools — Datadog, New Relic, Grafana, or the open-source Langfuse. Get visibility into what the agent is doing, what it's costing, and where it's getting stuck."

*[PAUSE]*

"Track four metrics: how often do humans agree with the agent's recommendations? What's the false positive rate? Is it saving time net of review? And how often are humans overriding it?"

"At day 60, you have a decision gate: if humans are agreeing with the agent's recommendations 70% or more of the time — you're ready to cautiously expand scope."

"If you're below 70% — **do not increase autonomy.** Tune the context. Improve the eval suite. Look at what failure scenarios the agent is getting wrong and add them to your test cases. Stay in advisory mode until the numbers support moving forward."

*[Advance slide]*

---

**Slide 27 — Days 61–90: Controlled Expansion**

**LAYOUT:** Timeline-style. "Days 61-90" header. Two-column: "Add this" vs "Not yet." Progress indicator showing Level 1 → Level 2 movement. "What you will have built" summary at bottom.

**ON SCREEN:**
```
DAYS 61–90: CONTROLLED EXPANSION

ADD THIS:                             NOT YET:
✓ Second use case: AI-assisted        ✗ Write access to production
  incident triage (read-only          ✗ Autonomous remediation
  data surfacing + hypothesis)        ✗ Multi-agent systems
                                      ✗ Full pipeline automation
✓ Cross-team documentation
  sharing (story, not mandate)

✓ Cadence for prompt tuning
  and workflow updates

BY DAY 90, YOU WILL HAVE:
• One proven agentic workflow in production (advisory mode)
• An eval suite covering your top failure scenarios
• Agent observability dashboards you can show your leadership
• IAM documentation for agent permissions
• The data to make the case for expanding scope

That's Level 2. That's ahead of 85%+ of organizations right now.
```

**SPEAKER NOTES `[TIMING: 40:30]`**

"Days 61 through 90. This is where you expand — carefully."

"Add a second use case: **AI-assisted incident triage.** The agent surfaces relevant logs, metrics, traces, and produces a hypothesis about what caused the incident. Humans confirm before any remediation happens. Read-only data access only."

"Notice what's in the 'not yet' column: no write access to production, no autonomous remediation, no multi-agent systems. Not because these things don't work — but because you haven't generated enough trust data to justify them yet."

*[PAUSE]*

"Share your learnings across teams — as a story, not a mandate. Show people what the agent got right, what it got wrong, what you had to tune. That builds organizational trust."

content.text:
1	---
     2	name: pptx
     3	description: "Use this skill any time a .pptx file is involved in any way — as input, output, or both. This includes: creating slide decks, pitch decks, or presentations; reading, parsing, or extracting text from any .pptx file (even if the extracted content will be used elsewhere, like in an email or summary); editing, modifying, or updating existing presentations; combining or splitting slide files; working with templates, layouts, speaker notes, or comments. Trigger whenever the user mentions \"deck,\" \"slides,\" \"presentation,\" or references a .pptx filename, regardless of what they plan to do with the content afterward. If a .pptx file needs to be opened, created, or touched, use this skill."
     4	license: Proprietary. LICENSE.txt has complete terms
     5	---
     6	
     7	# PPTX Skill
     8	
     9	## Quick Reference
    10	
    11	| Task | Guide |
    12	|------|-------|
    13	| Read/analyze content | `python -m markitdown presentation.pptx` |
    14	| Edit or create from template | Read [editing.md](editing.md) |
    15	| Create from scratch | Read [pptxgenjs.md](pptxgenjs.md) |
    16	
    17	---
    18	
    19	## Reading Content
    20	
    21	```bash
    22	# Text extraction
    23	python -m markitdown presentation.pptx
    24	
    25	# Visual overview
    26	python scripts/thumbnail.py presentation.pptx
    27	
    28	# Raw XML
    29	python scripts/office/unpack.py presentation.pptx unpacked/
    30	```
    31	
    32	---
    33	
    34	## Editing Workflow
    35	
    36	**Read [editing.md](editing.md) for full details.**
    37	
    38	1. Analyze template with `thumbnail.py`
    39	2. Unpack → manipulate slides → edit content → clean → pack
    40	
    41	---
    42	
    43	## Creating from Scratch
    44	
    45	**Read [pptxgenjs.md](pptxgenjs.md) for full details.**
    46	
    47	Use when no template or reference presentation is available.
    48	
    49	---
    50	
    51	## Design Ideas
    52	
    53	**Don't create boring slides.** Plain bullets on a white background won't impress anyone. Consider ideas from this list for each slide.
    54	
    55	### Before Starting
    56	
    57	- **Pick a bold, content-informed color palette**: The palette should feel designed for THIS topic. If swapping your colors into a completely different presentation would still "work," you haven't made specific enough choices.
    58	- **Dominance over equality**: One color should dominate (60-70% visual weight), with 1-2 supporting tones and one sharp accent. Never give all colors equal weight.
    59	- **Dark/light contrast**: Dark backgrounds for title + conclusion slides, light for content ("sandwich" structure). Or commit to dark throughout for a premium feel.
    60	- **Commit to a visual motif**: Pick ONE distinctive element and repeat it — rounded image frames, icons in colored circles, thick single-side borders. Carry it across every slide.
    61	
    62	### Color Palettes
    63	
    64	Choose colors that match your topic — don't default to generic blue. Use these palettes as inspiration:
    65	
    66	| Theme | Primary | Secondary | Accent |
    67	|-------|---------|-----------|--------|
    68	| **Midnight Executive** | `1E2761` (navy) | `CADCFC` (ice blue) | `FFFFFF` (white) |
    69	| **Forest & Moss** | `2C5F2D` (forest) | `97BC62` (moss) | `F5F5F5` (cream) |
    70	| **Coral Energy** | `F96167` (coral) | `F9E795` (gold) | `2F3C7E` (navy) |
    71	| **Warm Terracotta** | `B85042` (terracotta) | `E7E8D1` (sand) | `A7BEAE` (sage) |
    72	| **Ocean Gradient** | `065A82` (deep blue) | `1C7293` (teal) | `21295C` (midnight) |
    73	| **Charcoal Minimal** | `36454F` (charcoal) | `F2F2F2` (off-white) | `212121` (black) |
    74	| **Teal Trust** | `028090` (teal) | `00A896` (seafoam) | `02C39A` (mint) |
    75	| **Berry & Cream** | `6D2E46` (berry) | `A26769` (dusty rose) | `ECE2D0` (cream) |
    76	| **Sage Calm** | `84B59F` (sage) | `69A297` (eucalyptus) | `50808E` (slate) |
    77	| **Cherry Bold** | `990011` (cherry) | `FCF6F5` (off-white) | `2F3C7E` (navy) |
    78	
    79	### For Each Slide
    80	
    81	**Every slide needs a visual element** — image, chart, icon, or shape. Text-only slides are forgettable.
    82	
    83	**Layout options:**
    84	- Two-column (text left, illustration on right)
    85	- Icon + text rows (icon in colored circle, bold header, description below)
    86	- 2x2 or 2x3 grid (image on one side, grid of content blocks on other)
    87	- Half-bleed image (full left or right side) with content overlay
    88	
    89	**Data display:**
    90	- Large stat callouts (big numbers 60-72pt with small labels below)
    91	- Comparison columns (before/after, pros/cons, side-by-side options)
    92	- Timeline or process flow (numbered steps, arrows)
    93	
    94	**Visual polish:**
    95	- Icons in small colored circles next to section headers
    96	- Italic accent text for key stats or taglines
    97	
    98	### Typography
    99	
   100	**Choose an interesting font pairing** — don't default to Arial. Pick a header font with personality and pair it with a clean body font.
   101	
   102	| Header Font | Body Font |
   103	|-------------|-----------|
   104	| Georgia | Calibri |
   105	| Arial Black | Arial |
   106	| Calibri | Calibri Light |
   107	| Cambria | Calibri |
   108	| Trebuchet MS | Calibri |
   109	| Impact | Arial |
   110	| Palatino | Garamond |
   111	| Consolas | Calibri |
   112	
   113	| Element | Size |
   114	|---------|------|
   115	| Slide title | 36-44pt bold |
   116	| Section header | 20-24pt bold |
   117	| Body text | 14-16pt |
   118	| Captions | 10-12pt muted |
   119	
   120	### Spacing
   121	
   122	- 0.5" minimum margins
   123	- 0.3-0.5" between content blocks
   124	- Leave breathing room—don't fill every inch
   125	
   126	### Avoid (Common Mistakes)
   127	
   128	- **Don't repeat the same layout** — vary columns, cards, and callouts across slides
   129	- **Don't center body text** — left-align paragraphs and lists; center only titles
   130	- **Don't skimp on size contrast** — titles need 36pt+ to stand out from 14-16pt body
   131	- **Don't default to blue** — pick colors that reflect the specific topic
   132	- **Don't mix spacing randomly** — choose 0.3" or 0.5" gaps and use consistently
   133	- **Don't style one slide and leave the rest plain** — commit fully or keep it simple throughout
   134	- **Don't create text-only slides** — add images, icons, charts, or visual elements; avoid plain title + bullets
   135	- **Don't forget text box padding** — when aligning lines or shapes with text edges, set `margin: 0` on the text box or offset the shape to account for padding
   136	- **Don't use low-contrast elements** — icons AND text need strong contrast against the background; avoid light text on light backgrounds or dark text on dark backgrounds
   137	- **NEVER use accent lines under titles** — these are a hallmark of AI-generated slides; use whitespace or background color instead
   138	
   139	---
   140	
   141	## QA (Required)
   142	
   143	**Assume there are problems. Your job is to find them.**
   144	
   145	Your first render is almost never correct. Approach QA as a bug hunt, not a confirmation step. If you found zero issues on first inspection, you weren't looking hard enough.
   146	
   147	### Content QA
   148	
   149	```bash
   150	python -m markitdown output.pptx
   151	```
   152	
   153	Check for missing content, typos, wrong order.
   154	
   155	**When using templates, check for leftover placeholder text:**
   156	
   157	```bash
   158	python -m markitdown output.pptx | grep -iE "\bx{3,}\b|lorem|ipsum|\bTODO|\[insert|this.*(page|slide).*layout"
   159	```
   160	
   161	If grep returns results, fix them before declaring success.
   162	
   163	### Visual QA
   164	
   165	**⚠️ USE SUBAGENTS** — even for 2-3 slides. You've been staring at the code and will see what you expect, not what's there. Subagents have fresh eyes.
   166	
   167	Convert slides to images (see [Converting to Images](#converting-to-images)), then use this prompt:
   168	
   169	```
   170	Visually inspect these slides. Assume there are issues — find them.
   171	
   172	Look for:
   173	- Overlapping elements (text through shapes, lines through words, stacked elements)
   174	- Text overflow or cut off at edges/box boundaries
   175	- Decorative lines positioned for single-line text but title wrapped to two lines
   176	- Source citations or footers colliding with content above
   177	- Elements too close (< 0.3" gaps) or cards/sections nearly touching
   178	- Uneven gaps (large empty area in one place, cramped in another)
   179	- Insufficient margin from slide edges (< 0.5")
   180	- Columns or similar elements not aligned consistently
   181	- Low-contrast text (e.g., light gray text on cream-colored background)
   182	- Low-contrast icons (e.g., dark icons on dark backgrounds without a contrasting circle)
   183	- Text boxes too narrow causing excessive wrapping
   184	- Leftover placeholder content
   185	
   186	For each slide, list issues or areas of concern, even if minor.
   187	
   188	Read and analyze these images — run `ls -1 "$PWD"/slide-*.jpg` and use the exact absolute paths it prints:
   189	1. <absolute-path>/slide-N.jpg — (Expected: [brief description])
   190	2. <absolute-path>/slide-N.jpg — (Expected: [brief description])
   191	...
   192	
   193	Report ALL issues found, including minor ones.
   194	```
   195	
   196	### Verification Loop
   197	
   198	1. Generate slides → Convert to images → Inspect
   199	2. **List issues found** (if none found, look again more critically)
   200	3. Fix issues
   201	4. **Re-verify affected slides** — one fix often creates another problem
   202	5. Repeat until a full pass reveals no new issues
   203	
   204	**Do not declare success until you've completed at least one fix-and-verify cycle.**
   205	
   206	---
   207	
   208	## Converting to Images
   209	
   210	Convert presentations to individual slide images for visual inspection:
   211	
   212	```bash
   213	python scripts/office/soffice.py --headless --convert-to pdf output.pptx
   214	rm -f slide-*.jpg
   215	pdftoppm -jpeg -r 150 output.pdf slide
   216	ls -1 "$PWD"/slide-*.jpg
   217	```
   218	
   219	**Pass the absolute paths printed above directly to the view tool.** The `rm` clears stale images from prior runs. `pdftoppm` zero-pads based on page count: `slide-1.jpg` for decks under 10 pages, `slide-01.jpg` for 10-99, `slide-001.jpg` for 100+.
   220	
   221	**After fixes, rerun all four commands above** — the PDF must be regenerated from the edited `.pptx` before `pdftoppm` can reflect your changes.
   222	
   223	---
   224	
   225	## Dependencies
   226	
   227	- `pip install "markitdown[pptx]"` - text extraction
   228	- `pip install Pillow` - thumbnail grids
   229	- `npm install -g pptxgenjs` - creating from scratch
   230	- LibreOffice (`soffice`) - PDF conversion (auto-configured for sandboxed environments via `scripts/office/soffice.py`)
   231	- Poppler (`pdftoppm`) - PDF to images
   232

input.description: Read pptxgenjs guide

content.text:
1	# PptxGenJS Tutorial
     2	
     3	## Setup & Basic Structure
     4	
     5	```javascript
     6	const pptxgen = require("pptxgenjs");
     7	
     8	let pres = new pptxgen();
     9	pres.layout = 'LAYOUT_16x9';  // or 'LAYOUT_16x10', 'LAYOUT_4x3', 'LAYOUT_WIDE'
    10	pres.author = 'Your Name';
    11	pres.title = 'Presentation Title';
    12	
    13	let slide = pres.addSlide();
    14	slide.addText("Hello World!", { x: 0.5, y: 0.5, fontSize: 36, color: "363636" });
    15	
    16	pres.writeFile({ fileName: "Presentation.pptx" });
    17	```
    18	
    19	## Layout Dimensions
    20	
    21	Slide dimensions (coordinates in inches):
    22	- `LAYOUT_16x9`: 10" × 5.625" (default)
    23	- `LAYOUT_16x10`: 10" × 6.25"
    24	- `LAYOUT_4x3`: 10" × 7.5"
    25	- `LAYOUT_WIDE`: 13.3" × 7.5"
    26	
    27	---
    28	
    29	## Text & Formatting
    30	
    31	```javascript
    32	// Basic text
    33	slide.addText("Simple Text", {
    34	  x: 1, y: 1, w: 8, h: 2, fontSize: 24, fontFace: "Arial",
    35	  color: "363636", bold: true, align: "center", valign: "middle"
    36	});
    37	
    38	// Character spacing (use charSpacing, not letterSpacing which is silently ignored)
    39	slide.addText("SPACED TEXT", { x: 1, y: 1, w: 8, h: 1, charSpacing: 6 });
    40	
    41	// Rich text arrays
    42	slide.addText([
    43	  { text: "Bold ", options: { bold: true } },
    44	  { text: "Italic ", options: { italic: true } }
    45	], { x: 1, y: 3, w: 8, h: 1 });
    46	
    47	// Multi-line text (requires breakLine: true)
    48	slide.addText([
    49	  { text: "Line 1", options: { breakLine: true } },
    50	  { text: "Line 2", options: { breakLine: true } },
    51	  { text: "Line 3" }  // Last item doesn't need breakLine
    52	], { x: 0.5, y: 0.5, w: 8, h: 2 });
    53	
    54	// Text box margin (internal padding)
    55	slide.addText("Title", {
    56	  x: 0.5, y: 0.3, w: 9, h: 0.6,
    57	  margin: 0  // Use 0 when aligning text with other elements like shapes or icons
    58	});
    59	```
    60	
    61	**Tip:** Text boxes have internal margin by default. Set `margin: 0` when you need text to align precisely with shapes, lines, or icons at the same x-position.
    62	
    63	---
    64	
    65	## Lists & Bullets
    66	
    67	```javascript
    68	// ✅ CORRECT: Multiple bullets
    69	slide.addText([
    70	  { text: "First item", options: { bullet: true, breakLine: true } },
    71	  { text: "Second item", options: { bullet: true, breakLine: true } },
    72	  { text: "Third item", options: { bullet: true } }
    73	], { x: 0.5, y: 0.5, w: 8, h: 3 });
    74	
    75	// ❌ WRONG: Never use unicode bullets
    76	slide.addText("• First item", { ... });  // Creates double bullets
    77	
    78	// Sub-items and numbered lists
    79	{ text: "Sub-item", options: { bullet: true, indentLevel: 1 } }
    80	{ text: "First", options: { bullet: { type: "number" }, breakLine: true } }
    81	```
    82	
    83	---
    84	
    85	## Shapes
    86	
    87	```javascript
    88	slide.addShape(pres.shapes.RECTANGLE, {
    89	  x: 0.5, y: 0.8, w: 1.5, h: 3.0,
    90	  fill: { color: "FF0000" }, line: { color: "000000", width: 2 }
    91	});
    92	
    93	slide.addShape(pres.shapes.OVAL, { x: 4, y: 1, w: 2, h: 2, fill: { color: "0000FF" } });
    94	
    95	slide.addShape(pres.shapes.LINE, {
    96	  x: 1, y: 3, w: 5, h: 0, line: { color: "FF0000", width: 3, dashType: "dash" }
    97	});
    98	
    99	// With transparency
   100	slide.addShape(pres.shapes.RECTANGLE, {
   101	  x: 1, y: 1, w: 3, h: 2,
   102	  fill: { color: "0088CC", transparency: 50 }
   103	});
   104	
   105	// Rounded rectangle (rectRadius only works with ROUNDED_RECTANGLE, not RECTANGLE)
   106	// ⚠️ Don't pair with rectangular accent overlays — they won't cover rounded corners. Use RECTANGLE instead.
   107	slide.addShape(pres.shapes.ROUNDED_RECTANGLE, {
   108	  x: 1, y: 1, w: 3, h: 2,
   109	  fill: { color: "FFFFFF" }, rectRadius: 0.1
   110	});
   111	
   112	// With shadow
   113	slide.addShape(pres.shapes.RECTANGLE, {
   114	  x: 1, y: 1, w: 3, h: 2,
   115	  fill: { color: "FFFFFF" },
   116	  shadow: { type: "outer", color: "000000", blur: 6, offset: 2, angle: 135, opacity: 0.15 }
   117	});
   118	```
   119	
   120	Shadow options:
   121	
   122	| Property | Type | Range | Notes |
   123	|----------|------|-------|-------|
   124	| `type` | string | `"outer"`, `"inner"` | |
   125	| `color` | string | 6-char hex (e.g. `"000000"`) | No `#` prefix, no 8-char hex — see Common Pitfalls |
   126	| `blur` | number | 0-100 pt | |
   127	| `offset` | number | 0-200 pt | **Must be non-negative** — negative values corrupt the file |
   128	| `angle` | number | 0-359 degrees | Direction the shadow falls (135 = bottom-right, 270 = upward) |
   129	| `opacity` | number | 0.0-1.0 | Use this for transparency, never encode in color string |
   130	
   131	To cast a shadow upward (e.g. on a footer bar), use `angle: 270` with a positive offset — do **not** use a negative offset.
   132	
   133	**Note**: Gradient fills are not natively supported. Use a gradient image as a background instead.
   134	
   135	---
   136	
   137	## Images
   138	
   139	### Image Sources
   140	
   141	```javascript
   142	// From file path
   143	slide.addImage({ path: "images/chart.png", x: 1, y: 1, w: 5, h: 3 });
   144	
   145	// From URL
   146	slide.addImage({ path: "https://example.com/image.jpg", x: 1, y: 1, w: 5, h: 3 });
   147	
   148	// From base64 (faster, no file I/O)
   149	slide.addImage({ data: "image/png;base64,iVBORw0KGgo...", x: 1, y: 1, w: 5, h: 3 });
   150	```
   151	
   152	### Image Options
   153	
   154	```javascript
   155	slide.addImage({
   156	  path: "image.png",
   157	  x: 1, y: 1, w: 5, h: 3,
   158	  rotate: 45,              // 0-359 degrees
   159	  rounding: true,          // Circular crop
   160	  transparency: 50,        // 0-100
   161	  flipH: true,             // Horizontal flip
   162	  flipV: false,            // Vertical flip
   163	  altText: "Description",  // Accessibility
   164	  hyperlink: { url: "https://example.com" }
   165	});
   166	```
   167	
   168	### Image Sizing Modes
   169	
   170	```javascript
   171	// Contain - fit inside, preserve ratio
   172	{ sizing: { type: 'contain', w: 4, h: 3 } }
   173	
   174	// Cover - fill area, preserve ratio (may crop)
   175	{ sizing: { type: 'cover', w: 4, h: 3 } }
   176	
   177	// Crop - cut specific portion
   178	{ sizing: { type: 'crop', x: 0.5, y: 0.5, w: 2, h: 2 } }
   179	```
   180	
   181	### Calculate Dimensions (preserve aspect ratio)
   182	
   183	```javascript
   184	const origWidth = 1978, origHeight = 923, maxHeight = 3.0;
   185	const calcWidth = maxHeight * (origWidth / origHeight);
   186	const centerX = (10 - calcWidth) / 2;
   187	
   188	slide.addImage({ path: "image.png", x: centerX, y: 1.2, w: calcWidth, h: maxHeight });
   189	```
   190	
   191	### Supported Formats
   192	
   193	- **Standard**: PNG, JPG, GIF (animated GIFs work in Microsoft 365)
   194	- **SVG**: Works in modern PowerPoint/Microsoft 365
   195	
   196	---
   197	
   198	## Icons
   199	
   200	Use react-icons to generate SVG icons, then rasterize to PNG for universal compatibility.
   201	
   202	### Setup
   203	
   204	```javascript
   205	const React = require("react");
   206	const ReactDOMServer = require("react-dom/server");
   207	const sharp = require("sharp");
   208	const { FaCheckCircle, FaChartLine } = require("react-icons/fa");
   209	
   210	function renderIconSvg(IconComponent, color = "#000000", size = 256) {
   211	  return ReactDOMServer.renderToStaticMarkup(
   212	    React.createElement(IconComponent, { color, size: String(size) })
   213	  );
   214	}
   215	
   216	async function iconToBase64Png(IconComponent, color, size = 256) {
   217	  const svg = renderIconSvg(IconComponent, color, size);
   218	  const pngBuffer = await sharp(Buffer.from(svg)).png().toBuffer();
   219	  return "image/png;base64," + pngBuffer.toString("base64");
   220	}
   221	```
   222	
   223	### Add Icon to Slide
   224	
   225	```javascript
   226	const iconData = await iconToBase64Png(FaCheckCircle, "#4472C4", 256);
   227	
   228	slide.addImage({
   229	  data: iconData,
   230	  x: 1, y: 1, w: 0.5, h: 0.5  // Size in inches
   231	});
   232	```
   233	
   234	**Note**: Use size 256 or higher for crisp icons. The size parameter controls the rasterization resolution, not the display size on the slide (which is set by `w` and `h` in inches).
   235	
   236	### Icon Libraries
   237	
   238	Install: `npm install -g react-icons react react-dom sharp`
   239	
   240	Popular icon sets in react-icons:
   241	- `react-icons/fa` - Font Awesome
   242	- `react-icons/md` - Material Design
   243	- `react-icons/hi` - Heroicons
   244	- `react-icons/bi` - Bootstrap Icons
   245	
   246	---
   247	
   248	## Slide Backgrounds
   249	
   250	```javascript
   251	// Solid color
   252	slide.background = { color: "F1F1F1" };
   253	
   254	// Color with transparency
   255	slide.background = { color: "FF3399", transparency: 50 };
   256	
   257	// Image from URL
   258	slide.background = { path: "https://example.com/bg.jpg" };
   259	
   260	// Image from base64
   261	slide.background = { data: "image/png;base64,iVBORw0KGgo..." };
   262	```
   263	
   264	---
   265	
   266	## Tables
   267	
   268	```javascript
   269	slide.addTable([
   270	  ["Header 1", "Header 2"],
   271	  ["Cell 1", "Cell 2"]
   272	], {
   273	  x: 1, y: 1, w: 8, h: 2,
   274	  border: { pt: 1, color: "999999" }, fill: { color: "F1F1F1" }
   275	});
   276	
   277	// Advanced with merged cells
   278	let tableData = [
   279	  [{ text: "Header", options: { fill: { color: "6699CC" }, color: "FFFFFF", bold: true } }, "Cell"],
   280	  [{ text: "Merged", options: { colspan: 2 } }]
   281	];
   282	slide.addTable(tableData, { x: 1, y: 3.5, w: 8, colW: [4, 4] });
   283	```
   284	
   285	---
   286	
   287	## Charts
   288	
   289	```javascript
   290	// Bar chart
   291	slide.addChart(pres.charts.BAR, [{
   292	  name: "Sales", labels: ["Q1", "Q2", "Q3", "Q4"], values: [4500, 5500, 6200, 7100]
   293	}], {
   294	  x: 0.5, y: 0.6, w: 6, h: 3, barDir: 'col',
   295	  showTitle: true, title: 'Quarterly Sales'
   296	});
   297	
   298	// Line chart
   299	slide.addChart(pres.charts.LINE, [{
   300	  name: "Temp", labels: ["Jan", "Feb", "Mar"], values: [32, 35, 42]
   301	}], { x: 0.5, y: 4, w: 6, h: 3, lineSize: 3, lineSmooth: true });
   302	
   303	// Pie chart
   304	slide.addChart(pres.charts.PIE, [{
   305	  name: "Share", labels: ["A", "B", "Other"], values: [35, 45, 20]
   306	}], { x: 7, y: 1, w: 5, h: 4, showPercent: true });
   307	```
   308	
   309	### Better-Looking Charts
   310	
   311	Default charts look dated. Apply these options for a modern, clean appearance:
   312	
   313	```javascript
   314	slide.addChart(pres.charts.BAR, chartData, {
   315	  x: 0.5, y: 1, w: 9, h: 4, barDir: "col",
   316	
   317	  // Custom colors (match your presentation palette)
   318	  chartColors: ["0D9488", "14B8A6", "5EEAD4"],
   319	
   320	  // Clean background
   321	  chartArea: { fill: { color: "FFFFFF" }, roundedCorners: true },
   322	
   323	  // Muted axis labels
   324	  catAxisLabelColor: "64748B",
   325	  valAxisLabelColor: "64748B",
   326	
   327	  // Subtle grid (value axis only)
   328	  valGridLine: { color: "E2E8F0", size: 0.5 },
   329	  catGridLine: { style: "none" },
   330	
   331	  // Data labels on bars
   332	  showValue: true,
   333	  dataLabelPosition: "outEnd",
   334	  dataLabelColor: "1E293B",
   335	
   336	  // Hide legend for single series
   337	  showLegend: false,
   338	});
   339	```
   340	
   341	**Key styling options:**
   342	- `chartColors: [...]` - hex colors for series/segments
   343	- `chartArea: { fill, border, roundedCorners }` - chart background
   344	- `catGridLine/valGridLine: { color, style, size }` - grid lines (`style: "none"` to hide)
   345	- `lineSmooth: true` - curved lines (line charts)
   346	- `legendPos: "r"` - legend position: "b", "t", "l", "r", "tr"
   347	
   348	---
   349	
   350	## Slide Masters
   351	
   352	```javascript
   353	pres.defineSlideMaster({
   354	  title: 'TITLE_SLIDE', background: { color: '283A5E' },
   355	  objects: [{
   356	    placeholder: { options: { name: 'title', type: 'title', x: 1, y: 2, w: 8, h: 2 } }
   357	  }]
   358	});
   359	
   360	let titleSlide = pres.addSlide({ masterName: "TITLE_SLIDE" });
   361	titleSlide.addText("My Title", { placeholder: "title" });
   362	```
   363	
   364	---
   365	
   366	## Common Pitfalls
   367	
   368	⚠️ These issues cause file corruption, visual bugs, or broken output. Avoid them.
   369	
   370	1. **NEVER use "#" with hex colors** - causes file corruption
   371	   ```javascript
   372	   color: "FF0000"      // ✅ CORRECT
   373	   color: "#FF0000"     // ❌ WRONG
   374	   ```
   375	
   376	2. **NEVER encode opacity in hex color strings** - 8-char colors (e.g., `"00000020"`) corrupt the file. Use the `opacity` property instead.
   377	   ```javascript
   378	   shadow: { type: "outer", blur: 6, offset: 2, color: "00000020" }          // ❌ CORRUPTS FILE
   379	   shadow: { type: "outer", blur: 6, offset: 2, color: "000000", opacity: 0.12 }  // ✅ CORRECT
   380	   ```
   381	
   382	3. **Use `bullet: true`** - NEVER unicode symbols like "•" (creates double bullets)
   383	
   384	4. **Use `breakLine: true`** between array items or text runs together
   385	
   386	5. **Avoid `lineSpacing` with bullets** - causes excessive gaps; use `paraSpaceAfter` instead
   387	
   388	6. **Each presentation needs fresh instance** - don't reuse `pptxgen()` objects
   389	
   390	7. **NEVER reuse option objects across calls** - PptxGenJS mutates objects in-place (e.g. converting shadow values to EMU). Sharing one object between multiple calls corrupts the second shape.
   391	   ```javascript
   392	   const shadow = { type: "outer", blur: 6, offset: 2, color: "000000", opacity: 0.15 };
   393	   slide.addShape(pres.shapes.RECTANGLE, { shadow, ... });  // ❌ second call gets already-converted values
   394	   slide.addShape(pres.shapes.RECTANGLE, { shadow, ... });
   395	
   396	   const makeShadow = () => ({ type: "outer", blur: 6, offset: 2, color: "000000", opacity: 0.15 });
   397	   slide.addShape(pres.shapes.RECTANGLE, { shadow: makeShadow(), ... });  // ✅ fresh object each time
   398	   slide.addShape(pres.shapes.RECTANGLE, { shadow: makeShadow(), ... });
   399	   ```
   400	
   401	8. **Don't use `ROUNDED_RECTANGLE` with accent borders** - rectangular overlay bars won't cover rounded corners. Use `RECTANGLE` instead.
   402	   ```javascript
   403	   // ❌ WRONG: Accent bar doesn't cover rounded corners
   404	   slide.addShape(pres.shapes.ROUNDED_RECTANGLE, { x: 1, y: 1, w: 3, h: 1.5, fill: { color: "FFFFFF" } });
   405	   slide.addShape(pres.shapes.RECTANGLE, { x: 1, y: 1, w: 0.08, h: 1.5, fill: { color: "0891B2" } });
   406	
   407	   // ✅ CORRECT: Use RECTANGLE for clean alignment
   408	   slide.addShape(pres.shapes.RECTANGLE, { x: 1, y: 1, w: 3, h: 1.5, fill: { color: "FFFFFF" } });
   409	   slide.addShape(pres.shapes.RECTANGLE, { x: 1, y: 1, w: 0.08, h: 1.5, fill: { color: "0891B2" } });
   410	   ```
   411	
   412	---
   413	
   414	## Quick Reference
   415	
   416	- **Shapes**: RECTANGLE, OVAL, LINE, ROUNDED_RECTANGLE
   417	- **Charts**: BAR, LINE, PIE, DOUGHNUT, SCATTER, BUBBLE, RADAR
   418	- **Layouts**: LAYOUT_16x9 (10"×5.625"), LAYOUT_16x10, LAYOUT_4x3, LAYOUT_WIDE
   419	- **Alignment**: "left", "center", "right"
   420	- **Chart data labels**: "outEnd", "inEnd", "center"
   421

876	
   877	---
   878	
   879	**Slide 21 — Skill 4: Agent Observability**
   880	
   881	**LAYOUT:** Icon + text. Large "4" in accent circle. Two-column comparison: "Traditional Metrics" vs "Agent-Specific Metrics." Tool callout boxes below. Action item.
   882	
   883	**ON SCREEN:**
   884	```
   885	  4   AGENT OBSERVABILITY
   886	
   887	"You can't govern what you can't see."
   888	
   889	TRADITIONAL METRICS          AGENT-SPECIFIC METRICS
   890	• Request latency             • Reasoning loop count
   891	• Error rate                  • Tool call success/failure rate
   892	• Throughput                  • Token usage + cost per invocation
   893	                              • Loop/stall detection
   894	                              • Human override frequency
   895	
   896	THE TOOLING IS HERE NOW:
   897	Datadog LLM Observability (GA June 2025) — interactive agent execution flow charts
   898	New Relic Agentic AI Monitoring (expanded Feb 2026) — inter-agent interaction maps
   899	Grafana Assistant (GA Oct 2025) — AI-native observability inside Grafana Cloud
   900	OpenTelemetry GenAI SIG — standardizing semantic conventions (watch this space)
   901	
   902	RULE: Add agent observability BEFORE you increase agent autonomy.
   903	```
   904	
   905	**SPEAKER NOTES `[TIMING: 30:30]`**
   906	
   907	"Skill four: **agent observability.** This is the DevOps skill you already have, applied to a new class of system."
   908	
   909	"Traditional observability: latency, error rate, throughput. You know these cold."
   910	
   911	"Agent-specific observability asks different questions: **how many reasoning loops did the agent run before it took action?** Is it stalling on a problem it can't solve? What's the token cost of this incident investigation compared to the last one? How often are humans overriding its recommendations?"
   912	
   913	"These are your leading indicators for agent health — and they're different from anything your current dashboards show."
   914	
   915	*[PAUSE]*
   916	
   917	"The good news: the tooling is here. Datadog, New Relic, Grafana all shipped agent-specific observability products in 2025. OpenTelemetry's GenAI SIG is standardizing the semantic conventions — keep an eye on that."
   918	
   919	"The rule I want you to take from this: **add agent observability before you add agent autonomy.** In that order. Always."
   920	
   921	*[Advance slide]*
   922	
   923	---
   924	
   925	**Slide 22 — Skill 5: Multi-Agent Architecture**
   926	
   927	**LAYOUT:** Icon + text. Large "5" in accent circle. Diagram showing lead agent → sub-agents pattern. Warning callout. Action item.
   928	
   929	**ON SCREEN:**
   930	```
   931	  5   MULTI-AGENT ARCHITECTURE DESIGN
   932	
   933	"Single-agent first. Multi-agent when you have a specific reason."
   934

attachments.extracted_content:
Webinar Title: Beyond Copilots: How Agentic AI is Rewriting the DevOps Playbook 


Target Audience: Mid-to-senior DevOps engineers, Platform Engineers, Engineering Leaders (50–500 eng teams, mostly EU/US) 
Format: Live webinar (60–75 min), interactive, with downloadable assets
Live date: April 2026 

INTRO 

Your team ships code faster than ever. GitHub Copilot writes the code. CI runs the tests. But then... the PR sits in review for 3 days. The staging environment is broken. Nobody knows which compliance checks apply. A security scan flags something, and the ticket gets lost.
The real bottleneck in DevOps isn't writing code anymore, it's everything that happens after code leaves your laptop. That's the "outer loop," and that's exactly what Agentic DevOps targets.

Key stat: Gartner predicts 40% of enterprise apps will include task-specific AI agents by 2026, up from <5% in 2025.. But most teams are stalled at the pilot stage because they can't operationalize agents safely.
Section 1: What Is Agentic DevOps, Really? (10 min)
Goal: Cut through the marketing fog. Give the audience a clear mental model.
The Evolution Path: Scripts → CI/CD Pipelines → GitOps → Copilots → Agents
Each step removed a different kind of toil. Agents tackle the coordination overhead between tools  
Automation vs. Autonomy:
Automation: If CPU > 80%, scale up (predefined rule)
Agent: Notices CPU trending up, checks if there's a deployment in progress, correlates with traffic patterns, decides whether to scale or wait, and logs its reasoning
What agents actually do in DevOps today (pulled from Bret Fisher's podcast guests):
Create and manage Kubernetes clusters via natural language 
Isolate coding environments in containers so multiple agents work in parallel 
Run on Kubernetes itself, with proper RBAC and gateway controls 
Triage incidents, execute runbooks, document timelines automatically
What agents DON'T do (yet):
Replace system architects or make strategic decisions
Handle novel incidents without human oversight
Understand your specific business context without being taught
According to Solomon Hykes, the father of Docker, Untrusted text injection into agents is the security challenge of this era. Just like we learned to sanitize database inputs, we need to learn to secure agent inputs.
Interactive moment: Quick poll : "Which part of your workflow would you trust an AI agent with today?" (Code review / Environment setup / Incident triage / Deployment approvals / None of them)
Section 2: The Agentic DevOps Stack - Tools & Skills (15 min)
Goal: Make it tangible. Map tools to KodeKloud courses.
Present the 4-layer framework from the Curriculum Mapping section above, but from the audience's perspective:
Layer 1: Your Existing Foundation (You Already Have This)
Kubernetes, Docker, Terraform, CI/CD, cloud platforms
"If you're here, you probably have this layer. If not, this is where to start."
KodeKloud courses: All existing DevOps and cloud courses
Layer 2: Cloud & Platform Foundation (Leveling Up)
Kubernetes Gateway API (how agents communicate across services)
Grafana/Prometheus (monitoring what agents are doing)
CNPE certification (the platform engineering career path)
Linux Advanced Networking (the infrastructure agents run on)
"Platform engineers are the people who BUILD the systems agents operate in. This role pays 20% more than traditional DevOps."
Layer 3: Agent Infrastructure (The New Frontier)
KAgent — run AI agents ON Kubernetes with proper controls
KServe — serve ML models natively on K8s
MCP (Model Context Protocol) — the "USB port" connecting agents to your tools
Container Use — isolate agent environments (Dagger/Solomon Hykes)
"This is where most teams need to build competency NOW."
KodeKloud courses: KAgent (launching), KServe (roadmap)
Layer 4: Agent Intelligence (Building the Brains)
Google ADK — build agents with Google's framework (LIVE on KodeKloud)
LangGraph — orchestrate multi-step agent workflows
AWS Bedrock — enterprise agent infrastructure
Vector Databases — give agents long-term memory and context
KubeFlow — ML pipeline orchestration
"This is where you build and train the agents themselves."
KodeKloud courses: Google ADK (LIVE), LangGraph (launching), AWS Bedrock (in prod), Vector DBs (in prod), KubeFlow (launching)
Live demo idea: Show how Google ADK (already live on KodeKloud) lets you build a basic agent, then show how KAgent deploys it to Kubernetes. Concrete, teachable, and drives course signups. 
Section 3: Where the industry is heading? 
MCP - The standard that matters 
MCP's trajectory to how containers evolved from dev tools to infrastructure platforms.
Learn MCP now, the same way you learned Docker Compose or Helm charts early. First-mover advantage is real. 
Kubernetes is the Agent runtime 
Kubernetes is becoming the platform where agents live and operate. If you know K8s, you're already ahead.
Our KAgent course teaches exactly this — deploying and managing AI agents on Kubernetes."
Security is the unsolved problem 
MCP lacks built-in security standards. Agent-to-agent communication (A2A) is still immature. Every expert on the podcast flags security as the biggest gap.
What this means for engineers: Security-aware agent practitioners will be the most in-demand people in DevOps. This maps to the Microsoft Agentic AI Solutions Architect certification on KodeKloud's roadmap.
4. Cost Matters More Than Capabilities
Token costs, API calls, tool invocations, retries — agents aren't free. The AWS episode specifically covered cost estimation for serverless MCP and agentic systems. FinOps for agents is going to be a real discipline.
KodeKloud angle: FinOps Engineer certification is on the roadmap. Grafana/Prometheus course can be positioned as "monitoring agent costs and performance."
5. Traditional CI/CD Is Being Rewritten
Solomon Hykes argues CI/CD as we know it will disappear because agents can't tolerate slow, unrepeatable build processes. Agents develop 100x faster than humans and need 100x faster feedback loops. This doesn't mean Jenkins dies tomorrow, but the architecture is shifting.
What this means for engineers: Invest in understanding how agentic workflows interact with CI/CD. GitHub Actions + AI is the bridge 
Section 4: Practical Playbook: Your 30-Day Start (10 min)
Goal: Send people home with a plan and a reason to sign up for KodeKloud Business 
Week 1: Assess & Learn the Fundamentals
Audit your team's DevOps foundation (K8s, Docker, CI/CD, cloud). Any gaps? Fix those first.
Take the Google ADK course on KodeKloud (LIVE now) to understand how agents are built
Listen to 2-3 episodes of the Agentic DevOps podcast for industry context
Week 2: Identify Your First Agent Use Case
Best starting points (high volume, low risk, repetitive):
Automated PR labeling and routing
Environment promotion with policy checks
Incident triage: classify severity, assign team, start runbook
Cost anomaly detection and alerting
Worst starting points:
Production deployments without rollback
Anything touching customer data without governance
Week 3: Get Hands-On
Deploy KAgent on a dev cluster (KAgent course launching imminently)
Build a multi-step workflow with LangGraph (course launching)
Set up monitoring with Grafana/Prometheus to track agent behavior
Week 4: Build the Business Case
Document time saved, error reduction, DORA metrics impact
Frame it for leadership: "We reduced incident triage time by X% using AI agents running on our existing Kubernetes infrastructure"
Use the Agentic DevOps Readiness Assessment (downloadable) to benchmark your team
Downloadable asset: "The Agentic DevOps Readiness Assessment" scorecard teams can use to evaluate where they are, maps directly to KodeKloud learning paths at each level.
The certification angle:
Microsoft just released Agentic AI Solutions Architect (beta): KodeKloud has it on the roadmap (approved)
CNPE (Cloud Native Platform Engineer):  KodeKloud is building this now
AWS DevOps Engineer Professional: KodeKloud has this at 80% production
FinOps Engineer : on the acquisition roadmap
Platform Engineering roles (where agent skills live) pay 20% more than traditional DevOps.
B2C2B conversion hook: "Take the readiness assessment. If your team has gaps, bring this to your engineering leader. We have learning paths that map to every level of the Agentic DevOps stack."
Closing & CTA (5 min)
The Big Takeaway: Agentic DevOps isn't about replacing engineers with AI. It's a new layer on top of the DevOps foundation you already have. Your Kubernetes skills, your Docker expertise, your CI/CD knowledge are not obsolete. It's the foundation agents need to operate.
The engineers who add agent skills to their existing DevOps expertise will own the next decade.
To do: 
Start the Google ADK course on KodeKloud (live now: build your first agent)
Run the Agentic DevOps Readiness Assessment with your team

In this episode, Solomon Hykes, founder of Dagger and the original Docker project, sat down with Daniel Bryant to discuss the state of DevOps and how the new open source Dagger project aims to improve continuous delivery practices. Topics covered included the challenges of building applications, modernizing CI/CD tooling and practices, and building an effective community. Engineers still experience significant challenges when building continuous delivery pipelines. Gluing build steps together with platform-specific tooling, YAML, and shell scripts does not scale effectively and is difficult and expensive to maintain. content.title: Solomon Hykes: Dagger Brings the Promise of Docker to CI/CD - The New Stack
content.text:
Now, the team has rejoined to build Dagger, which according to its introductory blog post “allows DevOps engineers to build powerful CI/CD pipelines quickly, then run them anywhere.” That summary immediately calls to mind the team’s prior ...

Mar 12th 2025 1:00pm, by Darryl K. Taft and Loraine Lawson · Oracle Won’t Release ‘JavaScript’ Without a Fight ... The team behind Docker has rejoined to build Dagger, allowing devops engineers to build powerful CI/CD pipelines quickly and run anywhere. ... For the team behind Dagger, the “portable dev kit for CI/CD pipelines” that was launched into public beta last month, the open source startup is a bit of a second act. The trio of co-founders — Solomon Hykes, Andrea Luzzardi, and Samuel Alba — all spent nearly a decade working together at Docker, the company Hykes founded before leaving in 2018. Now, the team has rejoined to build Dagger, which according to its introductory blog post “allows DevOps engineers to build powerful CI/CD pipelines quickly, then run them anywhere.” That summary immediately calls to mind the team’s prior endeavor.

Dec 16th 2025 6:00am, by Darryl K. Taft ... Dec 16th 2025 5:00am, by Steven J. Vaughan-Nichols · Meet Whamm: The WebAssembly Instrumentation Framework · Dec 8th 2025 3:00pm, by B. Taft · Java 25: Oracle Makes Java Easier To Learn, Ready for AI Development · Sep 18th 2025 4:00pm, by Darryl K. Taft ... Sep 10th 2025 4:00pm, by Darryl K. Taft ... Williams · Is Using Command Line AI an Option for Helping You Code? ... Nov 28th 2025 8:00am, by Alexander T. Williams ... Oct 26th 2025 11:00am, by B. Cameron Gain · Rust vs. C++: a Modern Take on Performance and Safety ... Dec 10th 2025 12:00pm, by Steven J. Vaughan-Nichols · Combining Rust and Python for High-Performance AI Systems ... Nov 11th 2025 12:00pm, by Steven J. Vaughan-Nichols ·

Dagger, explained Hykes in an interview with The New Stack, solves the problems that DevOps engineers face from having to keep up with the growth of software teams, the scale of the software now being shipped, and the need to automate it all with increasingly complicated pipelines. “We identified the main pain point, the main bottleneck, which is the glue,” he said. “DevOps engineers spend most of their time gluing together a bunch of specialized tools and the glue itself is the problem. It’s basically scripts that they throw together; they’re hard to test, they’re hard to adapt to new requirements, they’re hard to debug, and usually, you need to jump between tools and languages to kind of tie it all together. We let them get rid of that glue. Instead, they get to play Legos to get a standard set of components, the standard interface between them, and a unified model for how to combine them.” ·

content.title: Dagger | Dagger Love
content.text:
It's kind of a container-based swiss army knife that can be used for CI operations (reproducible builds, testing, publishing), creating LLM agents, and more. Tons of integrations and open-source modules on the "Daggerverse".

My latest issue on why I believe @dagger_io could be the future of CI/CD for Engineering and Platform Engineering teams https://romaricphilogene.substack.com/p/platform-tips-18-dagger-the-future #platformengineering #cicd #devops #dagger · When @solomonstre drops an open-source Claude Code... ya run with it. "Toy Programmer" + added MCP & cursor-rules + an updated version of @zbeyens phenom "dotai" repo · So im finally coming around on this whole AI thing is the future...Solomon Hykes demo of an agent writing a curl clone in 5 minutes using Dagger is still lingering in my mind...if any of you are curmudgeons like me you might want to watch... Few understand the implications of this. This will be absolutely defining · @dagger_io experimental AI tooling is really interesting. I just leveraged two separate modules (Strava and Notify). It took me 20 minutes to build the prompt and less than 5 to write the code.

It's kind of a container-based swiss army knife that can be used for CI operations (reproducible builds, testing, publishing), creating LLM agents, and more. Tons of integrations and open-source modules on the "Daggerverse". So im finally coming around on this whole AI thing is the future...Solomon Hykes demo of an agent writing a curl clone in 5 minutes using Dagger is still lingering in my mind...if any of you are curmudgeons like me you might want to watch... I'm feeling a "docker moment" in real time. Even though I didn't know the CI tool, it seems so promising for multiple areas. all your dagger functions are now available for agents to use. built-in function registry. ... That's why tools like @dagger_io are going to be so powerful! (standardized text interface, multi-language modules and a neatly integrated web GUI over the CLI) Great AI assistant experiences largely depend on well-designed interfaces they can easily leverage.

So im finally coming around on this whole AI thing is the future...Solomon Hykes demo of an agent writing a curl clone in 5 minutes using Dagger is still lingering in my mind...if any of you are curmudgeons like me you might want to watch... If you don’t know where to start to create AI agents, you should have a look at Dagger agents. That makes it so easy to build tools that will benefit from AI but as real developer tool. Few understand the implications of this. This will be absolutely defining · I'm feeling a "docker moment" in real time. Even though I didn't know the CI tool, it seems so promising for multiple areas. @dagger_io experimental AI tooling is really interesting. I just leveraged two separate modules (Strava and Notify). It took me 20 minutes to build the prompt and less than 5 to write the code. This is crazy cool! If you have worked with LLMs this is amazing!

Join me at the first @dagger_io meetup in Raleigh, NC this week. The meetup will start by teaching the basics of Dagger with real-world examples. I'm still in KubeCon mode, looking forward to meeting the DevOps community in person! Check out the event details at https://meetu.ps/e/MWc2l/3Fcx2/i ... I built my first Dagger function yesterday with Solomon Hykes, and just got it published up on the daggerverse! Dagger is awesome, and I plan on continuing to find ways to use it. At a high level, dagger lets you use containers as a first-class object in code, and stitch together complex orchestration pipelines using real programming languages instead of bash. Primitives like mounting in files/directories, running commands, building and publishing containers, environment variables and secrets are all available, type-safe, and programmable in Go, Python, or Typescript.

Solomon Hykes: Sort of. But this time, we’re opinionated. Dagger is narrowly focused on CI/CD. That lets us provide APIs, SDKs, and a deeper abstraction stack. DotCloud was about deploying and hosting people's apps—platform as a service—competing with Heroku and many clones. ... Solomon Hykes: I became aware of it in 2009. Just as I was struggling in France with container tech. When we joined YC in 2010, we packaged that tech into dotCloud, our hosting platform. Our differentiator was using containers under the hood when others didn’t. That let us support many language stacks and even run databases in containers—which was unheard of at the time.
