content.title: Docker founder Solomon Hykes launches Dagger, a new DevOps startup
content.text:
It’s been almost exactly four years since Docker founder Solomon Hykes left the company that kickstarted the container revolution. Docker has gone through its share of ups and downs since then, including selling its enterprise business to Mirantis in 2019, but Hykes, who was long the public face of Docker, mostly stayed on the periphery, with the exception of his participation in a few funding rounds. For a while now, though, he’s been quietly working on his next startup, Dagger, which is launching into public beta today and announcing a $20 million Series A funding round. The round was led by Redpoint Ventures, with participation from Y Combinator, Nat Friedman (former CEO, GitHub), Brian Stevens (former CTO, Google Cloud and former CTO, Red Hat), Idit Levine (founder and CEO, solo.io), Julius Volz (creator of Prometheus), Ellen Pao (former CEO, Reddit) and Daniel Lopez (co-founder, Bitnami).

agile software development, bitnami, Cloud Computing, computing, continuous integration, Dagger, developers, devops, Docker, Ellen Pao, Enterprise, erica brescia, GitHub, idit levine, mirantis, operating system, red hat, Reddit, redpoint ventures, software development, solomon hykes, Startups, Startups, technology, Y Combinator ... Frederic was with TechCrunch from 2012 through 2025. He also founded SiliconFilter and wrote for ReadWriteWeb (now ReadWrite). Frederic covers enterprise, cloud, developer tools, Google, Microsoft, gadgets, transportation and anything else he finds interesting. ... Tickets are live at the lowest rates of the year. Save up to $680 on your pass now. Meet investors. Discover your next portfolio company. Hear from 250+ tech leaders, dive into 200+ sessions, and explore 300+ startups building what’s next.

It’s been almost exactly four years since Docker founder Solomon Hykes left the company that kickstarted the container revolution. Docker has gone through its share of ups and downs since then, including selling its enterprise business to Mirantis in 2019, but Hykes, who was long the public face of Docker, mostly stayed on the periphery, with the exception of his participation in a few funding rounds. For a while now, though, he’s been quietly working on his next startup, Dagger, which is launching into public beta today and announcing a $20 million Series A funding round. The round was led by Redpoint Ventures, with participation from Y Combinator, Nat Friedman (former CEO, GitHub), Brian Stevens (former CTO, Google Cloud and former CTO, Red Hat), Idit Levine (founder and CEO, solo.io), Julius Volz (creator of Prometheus), Ellen Pao (former CEO, Reddit) and Daniel Lopez (co-founder, Bitnami).

Dagger, which was co-founded by Hykes and his fellow Docker alums Sam Alba and Andrea Luzzardi, aims to build what the team calls a “devops operating system.” Hykes noted how starting this new venture began with the team and not necessarily a product idea. The co-founders went looking for problems they could solve for the developer community and it quickly became clear to them that the DevOps process remains a bottleneck. “We decided to start from zero and not assume we know anything,” Hykes told me about the process the team used to develop its ideas. “We began this long discovery process to just be a blank slate and listen to people’s problems. And they pulled us very quickly in the direction of CI/CD and automation pipelines. You know, you’ve got dev — and developers are happy and productive. You’ve got ops — things scale, there’s all this cool cloud stuff — and the glue in the middle, the DevOps part, that’s just really complicated.

The team argues that there are a lot of very powerful DevOps tools, but they tend to be very specialized — and as these applications expand in scope, so does the DevOps stack. “There’s no shortage of specialized tools, but then [developers] have to glue them all together — and the glue is the bottleneck. So we’re focusing on replacing the glue with something better,” said Hykes. Specifically, that means Dagger lets DevOps engineers write their pipelines as declarative models in CUE (which stands for “configure, unify, execute”). With this, engineers can describe their pipelines and connect the different pieces to each other, all in code. Dagger calls these individual pieces “actions” and they, too, are described declaratively. “The main difference is that it’s basically a real software development experience,” Hykes explained.

content.text:
You're right to challenge it — but the reason he's relevant isn't Docker. That framing document is attributing his credibility to the wrong thing entirely.

Solomon Hykes is currently the co-founder of Dagger, and he shipped Container Use — an open-source MCP server that gives coding agents isolated, containerized environments to work in, solving the problem of agents interfering with each other when running on the same system. That's an active, current, production tool directly relevant to agentic DevOps. The quote isn't "Docker guy talks about AI" — it's "the person actively building the agent isolation infrastructure."

That said, you're right that leading with "co-founder of Docker" frames him wrong for your audience. Frame it as: *founder of Dagger, the agent execution layer* — because that's what actually makes the security quote land.

**On Bret Fisher:** He started the Agentic DevOps podcast in early 2025 specifically to document how AI agents and MCP tools can be used in the real world for DevOps automation, platform engineering, and systems operations, explicitly without "hand-wavy AI hype." He's a Docker Captain and CNCF Ambassador — practitioner credibility, not analyst credibility. Good for "what's being built right now" but not for data-backed claims.

**On "Thomas"** — from the search results, the reference is likely Thomas Ptacek from fly.io, who wrote a piece called "My AI Skeptic Friends are All Nuts." That's a security practitioner perspective, not an agentic DevOps expert. Minor source.

**Bottom line:** Hykes is actually the right voice for the security/isolation point — just attribute him to Dagger and Container Use, not Docker. That makes the quote land with your senior engineer audience instead of sounding like name-dropping a 2014 legacy.

chat_messages.text:
Michael is pushing back on the Container Use / agent isolation concept. Let me think about this carefully before responding.

His argument: agents shouldn't be interfering with each other because good design means one agent per system. He's saying this is a bad design problem, not a real infrastructure problem worth solving.

Let me think about whether he's right or wrong here...

He's partially right but missing a real use case. The interference problem isn't about multiple agents touching the same production system — he's correct that's bad design. The real problem is during the DEVELOPMENT/CI phase where multiple agents are running in the same dev environment simultaneously, like:

1. Claude Code spawning multiple parallel subagents that all try to write to the same filesystem, run the same test suite, install packages simultaneously, etc. 

The actual issue Michael's overlooking is that this happens in local development and CI environments, not production. When multiple agents are working in parallel on the same developer machine or CI runner — like one agent handling auth while another handles the API layer, both trying to run npm install or modify package.json — they create real conflicts. Michael's thinking about production Kubernetes deployments where you'd never do this, but Container Use is solving the developer workstation and CI runner problem where this actually happens.

Now I'm reconsidering what Solomon Hykes is really trying to solve with Container Use... you have a coding agent (like Claude Code) that needs to:
- Run code
- Install dependencies  
- Modify files
- Test things

If you run multiple parallel agents on the same machine without isolation, they'll:
- Conflict on file system writes
- Install incompatible package versions
- Run tests that interfere with each other's state
- Use the same ports

This is genuinely a real problem when you're running multiple coding agents in parallel — like GitHub Copilot Coding Agent running multiple parallel tasks, or when a single agent spawns subagents. It's NOT about production K8s infrastructure. It's about the local execution environment for agents doing coding tasks.

So Michael is thinking about the wrong layer. He's thinking production operations agents. Container Use is solving the development/coding agent layer.

But is Michael right that this is "bad design"? Kind of — you could solve it differently. But Container Use is actually addressing a practical need for agents running in parallel on shared infrastructure like CI runners or developer laptops. The thing is, Michael's focused on production operations where isolation is already handled through IAM, RBAC, and network policies — not containers. So Container Use isn't really solving the problem he cares about for his webinar topic.You're not wrong — but you're thinking about the wrong layer. Let me separate these out.

**Where you're right:** In production operations — incident response agents, deployment agents, infrastructure agents — yes, you design for one agent per system boundary with IAM/RBAC controlling the blast radius. That's your boundary design framework. Agents interfering with each other there is just bad architecture, exactly as you said.

**Where Container Use is actually solving a real problem:** It's not a production ops problem. It's a *coding agent* problem. When you run multiple parallel coding agents — say Claude Code spinning up three subagents simultaneously to work on different parts of a codebase — they all try to run on the same developer machine or CI runner. They conflict on:

- Filesystem writes to the same working directory
- Package installs (`npm install` in one agent conflicts with another)
- Same ports when running local test servers
- Test state contamination

So two agents both trying to run `npm test` on the same machine at the same time will step on each other. Container Use gives each agent its own isolated execution environment on that single machine.

**The honest call for your deck:** This is a coding agent infrastructure problem, not an agentic DevOps operations problem. It doesn't belong in your webinar at all. Your audience are ops and platform engineers, not people building coding agent pipelines. The Phuong doc conflated two different agent categories and presented a coding tooling solution as if it were relevant to your topic.

Drop it. It would confuse your audience more than help them.

- Test things

- Install incompatible package versions

- Use the same ports

chat_messages.text:
Michael is pushing back on Container Use / isolated containers for parallel coding agents, arguing git worktrees solve this already. Let me think about this carefully.

Git worktrees: They solve the filesystem/working directory collision problem — each worktree is a separate checkout of the repo at a different path. So multiple agents can work on different branches simultaneously without conflicting on the same files.

But do they solve everything Container Use claims to solve? Let me think:

1. **Filesystem writes to working directory** → git worktrees handle this ✓
2. **Package installs (npm install conflicts)** → git worktrees give separate node_modules per worktree ✓ (mostly)


3. **Same ports when running local test servers** → git worktrees don't prevent port conflicts when multiple agents start dev servers on the same port ✗
4. **Test state contamination** → depends on whether tests share global state, databases, etc.
5. **System-level dependencies** → if one agent modifies system packages, worktrees don't isolate that
6. **Environment variables / secrets** → worktrees share the same process environment
7. **Database state** → if tests hit a local database, worktrees don't isolate that

So Michael's point is only partially correct — worktrees solve the code-level conflicts but leave runtime isolation problems unsolved.

Git worktrees handle file system isolation effectively, while containers address the broader execution environment (ports, system dependencies, databases, process isolation). For most agent use cases involving integration tests or different dependency versions, containers are the cleaner approach. But Michael's critique has merit — this is fundamentally a developer tooling discussion that doesn't fit the DevOps-focused webinar audience, and his worktrees suggestion is actually valid for a meaningful subset of scenarios. I should acknowledge that both tools solve different parts of the problem, and he's right that this topic doesn't belong in this context.You're right for the majority of the problem. Git worktrees handle the filesystem collision — separate checkouts, separate branches, agents work in parallel without stepping on each other's code changes. That's the core of what Container Use is claiming to solve, and worktrees already do it.

Where containers add something worktrees don't cover:

- **Port conflicts** — if two agents both spin up a local test server, worktrees don't stop them both trying to bind port 3000
- **System-level dependency conflicts** — if agents are installing different versions of system packages, not just npm packages
- **Database state** — if integration tests hit a local database instance, worktrees don't isolate that

But here's the honest assessment of those exceptions: they're edge cases in controlled coding agent pipelines, not common enough to warrant the framing that Container Use is solving some fundamental agentic infrastructure problem. Most teams running parallel coding agents would solve port conflicts with environment config, not a whole new isolation layer.

So your instinct is correct. Git worktrees handle the real use case. Container Use is solving a narrower execution environment problem that most teams will never hit, and Dagger/Hykes has commercial incentive to make it sound more foundational than it is.

The Phuong doc is citing it because it sounds impressive and technical. It doesn't belong in your deck, the Solomon Hykes attribution should be dropped entirely, and the only thing worth keeping from that source is the untrusted text injection security point — which you should re-attribute to someone like the Snyk research you already have.

input.description: Claude Code spec for Agentic DevOps webinar collateral repo
input.file_text:
# Claude Code Spec: Agentic DevOps Webinar Collateral Repo

## Context

This spec builds out the downloadable/shareable collateral for the KodeKloud live webinar:
**"Beyond Copilots: How Agentic AI is Rewriting the DevOps Playbook"**
Live date: April 2026

All output lives under `Agentic_DevOps/` inside the existing KodeKloud repo.
Do not modify anything outside that folder.

---

## Repo Structure to Build

```
Agentic_DevOps/
├── README.md
├── 90-day-playbook/
│   ├── README.md
│   ├── playbook.md
│   └── decision-gate-checklist.md
├── readiness-assessment/
│   ├── README.md
│   └── assessment.md
├── agent-boundary-design/
│   ├── README.md
│   ├── permission-matrix-template.md
│   └── agents-md-template.md
├── eval-framework/
│   ├── README.md
│   └── eval-suite-starter.md
├── stack-reference/
│   ├── README.md
│   └── tool-landscape.md
└── resources.md
```

---

## Authoring Rules (Read Before Writing Anything)

- **Tone:** Written for mid-to-senior DevOps and Platform engineers. No hand-holding, no marketing language. Treat the reader as someone who already knows Kubernetes, CI/CD, and observability.
- **No hype.** Every claim must be either sourced (GitHub, DORA, Opsera, Datadog, Snyk, IDC) or explicitly framed as practitioner opinion.
- **No bullet-point soup.** Use prose for explanations. Use tables and structured lists only where they genuinely aid scanning (permission matrices, checklists, tool comparisons).
- **Every file must be immediately actionable.** If a section can't answer "so what do I do Monday?" it needs to be rewritten or cut.
- **No placeholder filler.** If you don't have enough information to write a section properly, leave a clearly marked `<!-- TODO: [specific question] -->` comment rather than padding.
- **Filenames are kebab-case.** No spaces, no underscores in filenames except the top-level `Agentic_DevOps/` folder name which is fixed.

---

## File-by-File Specifications

---

### `Agentic_DevOps/README.md`

**Purpose:** Entry point. Orients the reader to what's here and why.

**Must include:**
- One-paragraph context: what this webinar covered and why this repo exists
- A table mapping each folder to its use case and who it's for (individual engineer vs. team lead vs. engineering manager)
- A "start here" recommendation based on three reader profiles:
  - "I'm an individual engineer who wants to start with agents"
  - "I'm a team lead trying to assess readiness"
  - "I'm an engineering leader building a business case"
- Links to all sub-folders

**Tone anchor:** Write it like a senior engineer left this for their team on a shared Notion, not like a product landing page.

---

### `Agentic_DevOps/90-day-playbook/README.md`

One paragraph explaining what the playbook is and what it is not. Specifically call out: this is NOT a vendor evaluation guide and NOT a full autonomous deployment plan. It's a governance-first proof of concept path.

---

### `Agentic_DevOps/90-day-playbook/playbook.md`

**Purpose:** The primary collateral asset. The thing people download and bring to their team.

**Structure:**

```
# 90-Day Agentic DevOps Getting Started Playbook

## What This Is
## The One Use Case to Start With
## Days 1–30: Foundation
## Days 31–60: Validation
## Days 60 Decision Gate
## Days 61–90: Controlled Expansion
## What You Will Have Built by Day 90
## What Comes Next (Level 3 preview)
```

**Section-by-section requirements:**

**What This Is**
- 3-4 sentences max. One use case, one team, advisory mode first.
- Explicitly state what this does NOT cover: production write access, multi-agent systems, full pipeline automation.

**The One Use Case to Start With**
- Automated CI build failure analysis in advisory mode.
- Explain the Elastic pattern: build fails → agent analyzes error → recommends fix → human approves commit.
- Three reasons this is the right starting point:
  1. Read-only in most implementations = near-zero blast radius
  2. Measurable ROI from day one (track agent recommendation accuracy vs. manual triage time)
  3. Reproducible — connects to existing CI tooling, no new infrastructure required

**Days 1–30: Foundation**

Cover these three parallel workstreams in prose:

1. **Build your eval suite first.** Not after the agent is deployed. Before. Identify your 10 most common CI failure scenarios. These become your test cases. If you wouldn't deploy code without tests, don't deploy agents without evals. Reference AWS's four-part eval framework: evaluations (LLM judges + pass@k metrics), trajectory visualization (OpenTelemetry), fast feedback loops (run in minutes not hours), production sampling.

2. **Define permissions explicitly.** Create a separate service principal for the agent. Read-only access only. Document the IAM policy. Commit it to AGENTS.md in the repo root. Include a reference to the permission matrix template in `agent-boundary-design/`.

3. **Form your Agentic AI Pod.** 2-3 engineers. Defined ownership: eval suite, observability, governance documentation. Someone must be accountable or it drifts.

**Days 31–60: Validation**

Write this section around the principle: generate data, not enthusiasm.

- Agents remain in advisory mode. They recommend. Humans approve. No exceptions during this phase.
- Add observability before adding anything else. Pick one: Datadog LLM Observability, New Relic AI Monitoring, Grafana (GA Oct 2025), or Langfuse (open source). Reference `eval-framework/` for what to instrument.
- Four metrics to track:
  1. Agent recommendation accuracy (% of time humans agree with recommendation)
  2. False positive rate
  3. Net time saved (time saved by agent recommendation minus time spent reviewing it)
  4. Human override frequency (your leading governance health indicator)

**Days 60 Decision Gate**

Format this as a clear binary:

```
≥70% human agreement rate → proceed to Days 61–90
<70% human agreement rate → tune context, improve eval suite, stay in advisory mode
```

Include a note: if you're below 70%, the fix is almost never "better prompts." It's either bad context (the agent doesn't have access to enough information about your specific codebase/environment) or the wrong use case (the failure modes you're targeting require architectural knowledge the agent can't reason about). Diagnose before you iterate.

Note on the 70% threshold: this is a starting benchmark, not an industry standard. Tune it to your organization's risk tolerance. High-stakes environments (financial services, healthcare) may require 85%+.

**Days 61–90: Controlled Expansion**

Add one second use case: AI-assisted incident triage (read-only data surfacing + hypothesis formation, human confirms before any remediation).

Explicit "not yet" list:
- Write access to production
- Autonomous remediation
- Multi-agent systems
- Full pipeline automation

Share learnings across teams as a story, not a mandate. Document what the agent got right, what it got wrong, what you had to tune. That's what builds organizational trust faster than any pilot report.

**What You Will Have Built by Day 90**

Present this as a concrete inventory, not a summary:
- One agentic workflow in production (advisory mode, CI build failure analysis)
- Eval suite covering your top 10 failure scenarios with pass/fail tracking
- Agent observability dashboards (token cost, recommendation accuracy, override frequency)
- IAM documentation for agent permissions committed to the repo
- One incident triage workflow running in advisory mode
- The data set to make the case for expanding scope

Close with: IDC 2026 estimates the majority of organizations are at Level 1 maturity (individual ad-hoc AI tool use, ungoverned). By day 90 you are at Level 2 (AI in pipeline stages, human approval gates, documented policies). That puts you ahead of 85%+ of the industry — without a breach and without a failed pilot.

**What Comes Next**
- Briefly name Level 3 capabilities without making them sound imminent: write access to staging, agent-to-agent delegation, policy-as-code guardrails
- One sentence: "The teams who define what Level 3 looks like are building it now, on top of the Level 2 foundation you just built."

---

### `Agentic_DevOps/90-day-playbook/decision-gate-checklist.md`

**Purpose:** A printable/shareable checklist for the Day 60 gate review. Designed to be used in an actual team meeting.

**Format:** Checkboxes in markdown. Three sections:

1. **Data Requirements** (must have all of these before the meeting)
   - 30+ agent recommendations logged and reviewed
   - Recommendation accuracy rate calculated
   - False positive rate calculated
   - Human override frequency tracked
   - Net time saved documented with actual numbers

2. **Governance Requirements** (must have all of these)
   - IAM policy for agent service principal documented and reviewed
   - AGENTS.md committed to repo and reviewed by security
   - Eval suite passing on all 10 baseline scenarios
   - Observability dashboard live and showing last 30 days

3. **Go / No-Go Decision**
   - If all data requirements met AND accuracy ≥70%: proceed to Days 61–90
   - If data requirements met but accuracy <70%: diagnosis required before proceeding (see playbook)
   - If data requirements not met: extend Days 31–60 by two weeks, identify the gap

---

### `Agentic_DevOps/readiness-assessment/README.md`

One paragraph. This assessment is for teams, not individuals. Run it in a 45-minute team session, not solo.

---

### `Agentic_DevOps/readiness-assessment/assessment.md`

**Purpose:** The team self-assessment scorecard referenced in the webinar. Maps directly to the 4-layer stack.

**Format:** Four scored sections (0–3 per item, total score at end with interpretation).

**Section 1: Foundation Layer (Your Existing Infrastructure)**

Score each 0 (not in place), 1 (partially), 2 (solid), 3 (strong/documented):

- Kubernetes in production with RBAC configured
- CI/CD pipeline with automated testing (not just build)
- Observability stack (metrics, logs, traces) — not just uptime monitoring
- IaC (Terraform or equivalent) with state management
- Incident response runbooks that are current and actually used
- On-call rotation with documented escalation paths

**Section 2: Platform Readiness**

- Kubernetes Gateway API or service mesh in use
- Prometheus/Grafana or equivalent with alerting rules that fire correctly
- Container image scanning in CI pipeline
- RBAC policies documented and reviewed in the last 6 months
- Secrets management (not hardcoded, not in env vars in plain text)

**Section 3: Agent Readiness**

- Team has read and can explain what MCP is
- At least one engineer has built or run a basic agent workflow (locally is fine)
- Eval/testing discipline exists for current automation (you test your pipelines)
- IAM hygiene: principle of least privilege is actually practiced, not just in policy docs
- Your team has had an explicit conversation about where you would NOT deploy an agent

**Section 4: Organizational Readiness**

- Engineering leadership is aware of and supportive of an agent pilot
- Security team has been briefed (even informally) on agent plans
- You have 2-3 engineers who can own an agent pod for 90 days alongside existing work
- You have at least one CI failure type that is high-volume, well-understood, and currently handled manually

**Scoring interpretation:**

```
0–12:   Foundation gaps need addressing first. Focus on Section 1 items.
13–22:  Solid foundation, agent readiness gaps. Start with eval discipline and MCP basics.
23–32:  Ready to start the 90-day playbook. Begin with Days 1–30.
33–40:  Strong foundation. You may be able to compress Days 1–30 to 2–3 weeks.
```

**Close with:** A note that scoring yourself a 3 on "RBAC configured" because it exists but nobody has reviewed it in a year is how agent pilots fail. Be honest. The assessment is only useful if it reflects reality.

---

### `Agentic_DevOps/agent-boundary-design/README.md`

One paragraph. The IAM role an agent assumes is its blast radius. This folder contains the templates to define that boundary before any agent touches a pipeline.

---

### `Agentic_DevOps/agent-boundary-design/permission-matrix-template.md`

**Purpose:** The per-agent permission definition table. Fill this out for every agent before deployment.

**Format:**

```markdown
# Agent Permission Matrix

## Agent: [Name / Purpose]
## Deployment date:
## Owner (person accountable for this agent):
## Review date (max 90 days from deployment):

---

| Resource | Can READ | Can WRITE | Can EXECUTE | Requires Human Approval |
|----------|----------|-----------|-------------|------------------------|
| Source code repo | | | | |
| CI/CD pipeline config | | | | |
| Container registry | | | | |
| Kubernetes cluster (prod) | | | | |
| Kubernetes cluster (staging) | | | | |
| Infrastructure state (Terraform) | | | | |
| Secrets / credential store | | | | |
| Incident management system | | | | |
| Deployment tooling | | | | |
| Log/metrics store | | | | |

---

## Approval Gates

List every action this agent can take that requires human approval before execution:

1.
2.
3.

## Explicitly Out of Scope

List what this agent is explicitly NOT permitted to do, even if technically possible:

1.
2.
3.

## Retry Budget

- Maximum retries on failure: [number]
- Retry delay: [seconds]
- Circuit breaker behavior: [what happens when budget is exhausted]

## Audit Trail

- Where are agent actions logged: [location]
- Log retention: [duration]
- Who reviews logs and how often: [name/role, cadence]
```

**After the template, include a completed example** for a CI build failure analysis agent (read-only, advisory mode). Use realistic values, not placeholders.

---

### `Agentic_DevOps/agent-boundary-design/agents-md-template.md`

**Purpose:** A starter AGENTS.md file for teams to commit to their repo root. AGENTS.md is the per-repo configuration file that tells agents how to behave in that repository context.

**Must include sections for:**

- Repository context (what this repo is, what it does, what it doesn't do)
- Agent behavior rules (what agents are allowed to do in this repo)
- Off-limits (explicit prohibitions)
- CI/CD context (which pipeline, what triggers mean)
- Escalation (what to do when the agent is uncertain)
- Tool access (which MCP servers/tools are configured for this repo)

**Include a filled-out example** for a mid-sized monorepo with a Node.js service and Terraform infrastructure. Make it realistic enough that an engineer can use it as a starting point immediately.

---

### `Agentic_DevOps/eval-framework/README.md`

One paragraph. Eval-driven development is the same discipline as TDD applied to agents. If you wouldn't deploy code without tests, don't deploy agents without evals.

---

### `Agentic_DevOps/eval-framework/eval-suite-starter.md`

**Purpose:** A starter eval framework for the CI build failure analysis use case. Teams replace the example scenarios with their own.

**Structure:**

```
# Agent Eval Suite: CI Build Failure Analysis

## What This Is
## How to Use This
## Eval Scenario Template
## Example Scenarios (10 filled in)
## Scoring Methodology
## Trajectory Checklist
## Production Sampling Protocol
```

**What This Is:** 2-3 sentences. This is your acceptance criteria for the agent before it goes to advisory mode in CI.

**How to Use This:**
- Run the eval suite against your agent before initial deployment
- Run it again after any context change (new AGENTS.md, new tool access, new model version)
- Sample production runs weekly against these scenarios to detect drift

**Eval Scenario Template:**

```markdown
### Scenario [N]: [Short name]

**Input:** [The exact CI failure log or error message the agent receives]

**Context provided to agent:** [What additional context the agent has access to — repo README, AGENTS.md, recent commit history, etc.]

**Expected output:**
- Root cause identification: [what it should identify]
- Recommended fix: [what it should recommend]
- Confidence signal: [should it express uncertainty or flag for human review?]
- What it should NOT do: [explicit negative criteria]

**Pass criteria:** Human reviewer agrees recommendation is correct or within acceptable range
**Fail criteria:** [specific failure conditions]
**Edge case:** [a variant of this scenario that should trigger a different response]
```

**Example Scenarios:** Write 10 realistic CI failure scenarios for a Node.js/Kubernetes environment. Include a variety:
- Dependency version conflict
- Failed linting (straightforward)
- Failed integration test with ambiguous error (should flag uncertainty)
- Infra drift (Terraform plan fails)
- Flaky test (should recommend investigation, not a code fix)
- Missing secret in environment
- Image pull failure
- OOM kill in test container
- Test timeout (ambiguous — could be code or environment)
- Breaking change in upstream dependency (should flag as needing human architectural decision, not recommend a fix)

Make scenarios 6-10 progressively harder and include the expected uncertainty/escalation behavior, not just the happy path.

**Scoring Methodology:**
- Each scenario: Pass (1) / Fail (0) / Partial (0.5)
- Minimum passing score for deployment: 8/10
- Partial credit criteria: recommendation is directionally correct but missing specificity

**Trajectory Checklist:**
What to verify in OpenTelemetry traces for each eval run:
- Did the agent call the correct tools in a logical sequence?
- Did it terminate when it should have (no runaway loops)?
- Did it express appropriate uncertainty on scenarios 8-10?
- Token usage within expected range?

**Production Sampling Protocol:**
- Sample 10% of production agent runs weekly
- Compare against eval expectations
- Track drift: if accuracy on production samples drops >10% below eval suite score, review context configuration before continuing

---

### `Agentic_DevOps/stack-reference/README.md`

One paragraph. This is a point-in-time reference (March 2026). The landscape is moving fast. Check dates before citing any of this.

---

### `Agentic_DevOps/stack-reference/tool-landscape.md`

**Purpose:** The 4-layer stack reference for practitioners. Not a buying guide. Not ranked by preference.

**Layer 1: Foundation (You Probably Already Have This)**

Write one sentence per tool. No marketing descriptions. What it is, why it matters for agent deployment.

Include: Kubernetes, Docker, Terraform, GitHub Actions, ArgoCD, Prometheus, Grafana

**Layer 2: Platform Layer (The Infrastructure Agents Run On)**

Include: Kubernetes Gateway API, Dapr, KEDA, Service mesh (Istio/Linkerd — note the tradeoffs honestly)

For each: one sentence on what it is, one sentence on why it's relevant to agentic workloads specifically (not generic value).

**Layer 3: Agent Infrastructure (Where Most Teams Need to Build Now)**

Include:
- MCP (Model Context Protocol): the integration standard. How agents connect to tools. Note: no built-in security standards as of March 2026.
- KAgent: deploying and managing AI agents on Kubernetes (KodeKloud course)
- LangGraph 1.0: multi-step agent orchestration, durable execution, human-in-the-loop as first-class feature. 47M+ PyPI downloads, GA. Used by Uber, LinkedIn, Klarna.
- Dapr Agents: CNCF graduated, sidecar pattern, state persists through failures, cloud-agnostic

For each: be honest about maturity. Note where things are GA vs. preview vs. experimental.

**Layer 4: Agent Intelligence (Building and Running the Models)**

Include: Claude Code (Opus 4.6, 14.5-hour task horizon, MCP native), GitHub Copilot Coding Agent (GA Sept 2025), Google ADK (live), AWS Bedrock, OpenAI Codex (relaunched May 2025 as GPT-5.3-Codex)

For each: one concrete production capability, one honest limitation.

**Observability Tools for Agents (Cross-cutting)**

This deserves its own subsection because it's non-negotiable infrastructure before increasing agent autonomy.

Include: Datadog LLM Observability (GA June 2025), New Relic AI Monitoring (expanded Feb 2026), Grafana Assistant (GA Oct 2025), Langfuse (open source), OpenTelemetry GenAI SIG (standardizing semantic conventions — watch this space)

Agent-specific metrics every team needs to instrument (not optional):
- Reasoning loop count
- Tool call success/failure rate
- Token usage and cost per invocation
- Human override frequency
- Loop/stall detection

**The Four Failure Modes (Reference)**

Brief entries — detailed treatment in the playbook. Point to `90-day-playbook/playbook.md` for full context.
1. Hallucination propagation
2. Retry loops without circuit breakers
3. State drift (no audit trail)
4. Deploying everywhere on day one

---

### `Agentic_DevOps/resources.md`

**Purpose:** Curated external reading list. No link dumps.

**Format:** Annotated list. For each resource: title, source, one sentence on why it's worth reading and what question it answers.

**Must include:**

- 2025 DORA Report (delivery throughput vs. stability finding)
- Opsera 2026 research (4.6x PR review time, 48% faster PR creation, 15-18% more vulnerabilities in AI-generated PRs)
- Snyk February 2026 research (13.4% of public agent skills had critical vulnerabilities, 76 confirmed malicious payloads)
- Forrester 2026 prediction on agentic AI breach
- IDC 2026 maturity model research
- AWS DevOps Agent production writeup (eval framework source)
- Datadog Bits AI SRE Agent GA announcement
- Block/Goose PagerDuty MCP integration writeup
- Elastic self-healing CI/CD case study
- Bret Fisher / Agentic DevOps podcast (agenticdevops.fm) — note: practitioner perspective, not research

**Do not include:**
- Generic "AI is changing everything" think pieces
- Vendor whitepapers without cited data
- Anything without a date (undated content is useless in this space)

---

## Quality Gates

Before considering any file complete, verify:

1. **No timing information visible anywhere.** Timing guidance belongs in speaker notes only, not in any collateral asset.
2. **Every statistic has a source.** If you can't source it, cut it or flag it with `<!-- UNSOURCED: verify before publishing -->`.
3. **Every file passes the "Monday test":** Can a reader open this file and know what to do this week? If no, it's not done.
4. **No duplicate content across files.** If two files cover the same point, one should reference the other.
5. **The permission matrix template has a filled example.** Templates without examples don't get used.
6. **The eval suite has 10 complete scenarios.** Not 3 with placeholders for the rest.
7. **Run `markdownlint` or equivalent.** Broken link references and malformed tables are embarrassing in public repos.

---

## What This Repo Is NOT Responsible For

- The slide deck (separate deliverable, already built)
- KodeKloud course links (add placeholders, do not hardcode URLs that may change)
- Certification roadmap details (out of scope, reference KodeKloud learning paths generally)
- Anything requiring live infrastructure to demonstrate

---

## Final Check

When all files are written, generate a `Agentic_DevOps/README.md` last, after you have full visibility into what was actually built. The README should reflect reality, not the spec.

content.message: Claude Code spec for Agentic DevOps webinar collateral repo

display_content.json_block:
{"language": "markdown", "code": "# Claude Code Spec: Agentic DevOps Webinar Collateral Repo

## Context

This spec builds out the downloadable/shareable collateral for the KodeKloud live webinar:
**"Beyond Copilots: How Agentic AI is Rewriting the DevOps Playbook"**
Live date: April 2026

All output lives under `Agentic_DevOps/` inside the existing KodeKloud repo.
Do not modify anything outside that folder.

---

## Repo Structure to Build

```
Agentic_DevOps/
├── README.md
├── 90-day-playbook/
│   ├── README.md
│   ├── playbook.md
│   └── decision-gate-checklist.md
├── readiness-assessment/
│   ├── README.md
│   └── assessment.md
├── agent-boundary-design/
│   ├── README.md
│   ├── permission-matrix-template.md
│   └── agents-md-template.md
├── eval-framework/
│   ├── README.md
│   └── eval-suite-starter.md
├── stack-reference/
│   ├── README.md
│   └── tool-landscape.md
└── resources.md
```

---

## Authoring Rules (Read Before Writing Anything)

- **Tone:** Written for mid-to-senior DevOps and Platform engineers. No hand-holding, no marketing language. Treat the reader as someone who already knows Kubernetes, CI/CD, and observability.
- **No hype.** Every claim must be either sourced (GitHub, DORA, Opsera, Datadog, Snyk, IDC) or explicitly framed as practitioner opinion.
- **No bullet-point soup.** Use prose for explanations. Use tables and structured lists only where they genuinely aid scanning (permission matrices, checklists, tool comparisons).
- **Every file must be immediately actionable.** If a section can't answer "so what do I do Monday?" it needs to be rewritten or cut.
- **No placeholder filler.** If you don't have enough information to write a section properly, leave a clearly marked `<!-- TODO: [specific question] -->` comment rather than padding.
- **Filenames are kebab-case.** No spaces, no underscores in filenames except the top-level `Agentic_DevOps/` folder name which is fixed.

---

## File-by-File Specifications

---

### `Agentic_DevOps/README.md`

**Purpose:** Entry point. Orients the reader to what's here and why.

**Must include:**
- One-paragraph context: what this webinar covered and why this repo exists
- A table mapping each folder to its use case and who it's for (individual engineer vs. team lead vs. engineering manager)
- A "start here" recommendation based on three reader profiles:
  - "I'm an individual engineer who wants to start with agents"
  - "I'm a team lead trying to assess readiness"
  - "I'm an engineering leader building a business case"
- Links to all sub-folders

**Tone anchor:** Write it like a senior engineer left this for their team on a shared Notion, not like a product landing page.

---

### `Agentic_DevOps/90-day-playbook/README.md`

One paragraph explaining what the playbook is and what it is not. Specifically call out: this is NOT a vendor evaluation guide and NOT a full autonomous deployment plan. It's a governance-first proof of concept path.

---

### `Agentic_DevOps/90-day-playbook/playbook.md`

**Purpose:** The primary collateral asset. The thing people download and bring to their team.

**Structure:**

```
# 90-Day Agentic DevOps Getting Started Playbook

## What This Is
## The One Use Case to Start With
## Days 1–30: Foundation
## Days 31–60: Validation
## Days 60 Decision Gate
## Days 61–90: Controlled Expansion
## What You Will Have Built by Day 90
## What Comes Next (Level 3 preview)
```

**Section-by-section requirements:**

**What This Is**
- 3-4 sentences max. One use case, one team, advisory mode first.
- Explicitly state what this does NOT cover: production write access, multi-agent systems, full pipeline automation.

**The One Use Case to Start With**
- Automated CI build failure analysis in advisory mode.
- Explain the Elastic pattern: build fails → agent analyzes error → recommends fix → human approves commit.
- Three reasons this is the right starting point:
  1. Read-only in most implementations = near-zero blast radius
  2. Measurable ROI from day one (track agent recommendation accuracy vs. manual triage time)
  3. Reproducible — connects to existing CI tooling, no new infrastructure required

**Days 1–30: Foundation**

Cover these three parallel workstreams in prose:

1. **Build your eval suite first.** Not after the agent is deployed. Before. Identify your 10 most common CI failure scenarios. These become your test cases. If you wouldn't deploy code without tests, don't deploy agents without evals. Reference AWS's four-part eval framework: evaluations (LLM judges + pass@k metrics), trajectory visualization (OpenTelemetry), fast feedback loops (run in minutes not hours), production sampling.

2. **Define permissions explicitly.** Create a separate service principal for the agent. Read-only access only. Document the IAM policy. Commit it to AGENTS.md in the repo root. Include a reference to the permission matrix template in `agent-boundary-design/`.

3. **Form your Agentic AI Pod.** 2-3 engineers. Defined ownership: eval suite, observability, governance documentation. Someone must be accountable or it drifts.

**Days 31–60: Validation**

Write this section around the principle: generate data, not enthusiasm.

- Agents remain in advisory mode. They recommend. Humans approve. No exceptions during this phase.
- Add observability before adding anything else. Pick one: Datadog LLM Observability, New Relic AI Monitoring, Grafana (GA Oct 2025), or Langfuse (open source). Reference `eval-framework/` for what to instrument.
- Four metrics to track:
  1. Agent recommendation accuracy (% of time humans agree with recommendation)
  2. False positive rate
  3. Net time saved (time saved by agent recommendation minus time spent reviewing it)
  4. Human override frequency (your leading governance health indicator)

**Days 60 Decision Gate**

Format this as a clear binary:

```
≥70% human agreement rate → proceed to Days 61–90
<70% human agreement rate → tune context, improve eval suite, stay in advisory mode
```

Include a note: if you're below 70%, the fix is almost never "better prompts." It's either bad context (the agent doesn't have access to enough information about your specific codebase/environment) or the wrong use case (the failure modes you're targeting require architectural knowledge the agent can't reason about). Diagnose before you iterate.

Note on the 70% threshold: this is a starting benchmark, not an industry standard. Tune it to your organization's risk tolerance. High-stakes environments (financial services, healthcare) may require 85%+.

**Days 61–90: Controlled Expansion**

Add one second use case: AI-assisted incident triage (read-only data surfacing + hypothesis formation, human confirms before any remediation).

Explicit "not yet" list:
- Write access to production
- Autonomous remediation
- Multi-agent systems
- Full pipeline automation

Share learnings across teams as a story, not a mandate. Document what the agent got right, what it got wrong, what you had to tune. That's what builds organizational trust faster than any pilot report.

**What You Will Have Built by Day 90**

Present this as a concrete inventory, not a summary:
- One agentic workflow in production (advisory mode, CI build failure analysis)
- Eval suite covering your top 10 failure scenarios with pass/fail tracking
- Agent observability dashboards (token cost, recommendation accuracy, override frequency)
- IAM documentation for agent permissions committed to the repo
- One incident triage workflow running in advisory mode
- The data set to make the case for expanding scope

Close with: IDC 2026 estimates the majority of organizations are at Level 1 maturity (individual ad-hoc AI tool use, ungoverned). By day 90 you are at Level 2 (AI in pipeline stages, human approval gates, documented policies). That puts you ahead of 85%+ of the industry — without a breach and without a failed pilot.

**What Comes Next**
- Briefly name Level 3 capabilities without making them sound imminent: write access to staging, agent-to-agent delegation, policy-as-code guardrails
- One sentence: "The teams who define what Level 3 looks like are building it now, on top of the Level 2 foundation you just built."

---

### `Agentic_DevOps/90-day-playbook/decision-gate-checklist.md`

**Purpose:** A printable/shareable checklist for the Day 60 gate review. Designed to be used in an actual team meeting.

**Format:** Checkboxes in markdown. Three sections:

1. **Data Requirements** (must have all of these before the meeting)
   - 30+ agent recommendations logged and reviewed
   - Recommendation accuracy rate calculated
   - False positive rate calculated
   - Human override frequency tracked
   - Net time saved documented with actual numbers

2. **Governance Requirements** (must have all of these)
   - IAM policy for agent service principal documented and reviewed
   - AGENTS.md committed to repo and reviewed by security
   - Eval suite passing on all 10 baseline scenarios
   - Observability dashboard live and showing last 30 days

3. **Go / No-Go Decision**
   - If all data requirements met AND accuracy ≥70%: proceed to Days 61–90
   - If data requirements met but accuracy <70%: diagnosis required before proceeding (see playbook)
   - If data requirements not met: extend Days 31–60 by two weeks, identify the gap

---

### `Agentic_DevOps/readiness-assessment/README.md`

One paragraph. This assessment is for teams, not individuals. Run it in a 45-minute team session, not solo.

---

### `Agentic_DevOps/readiness-assessment/assessment.md`

**Purpose:** The team self-assessment scorecard referenced in the webinar. Maps directly to the 4-layer stack.

**Format:** Four scored sections (0–3 per item, total score at end with interpretation).

**Section 1: Foundation Layer (Your Existing Infrastructure)**

Score each 0 (not in place), 1 (partially), 2 (solid), 3 (strong/documented):

- Kubernetes in production with RBAC configured
- CI/CD pipeline with automated testing (not just build)
- Observability stack (metrics, logs, traces) — not just uptime monitoring
- IaC (Terraform or equivalent) with state management
- Incident response runbooks that are current and actually used
- On-call rotation with documented escalation paths

**Section 2: Platform Readiness**

- Kubernetes Gateway API or service mesh in use
- Prometheus/Grafana or equivalent with alerting rules that fire correctly
- Container image scanning in CI pipeline
- RBAC policies documented and reviewed in the last 6 months
- Secrets management (not hardcoded, not in env vars in plain text)

**Section 3: Agent Readiness**

- Team has read and can explain what MCP is
- At least one engineer has built or run a basic agent workflow (locally is fine)
- Eval/testing discipline exists for current automation (you test your pipelines)
- IAM hygiene: principle of least privilege is actually practiced, not just in policy docs
- Your team has had an explicit conversation about where you would NOT deploy an agent

**Section 4: Organizational Readiness**

- Engineering leadership is aware of and supportive of an agent pilot
- Security team has been briefed (even informally) on agent plans
- You have 2-3 engineers who can own an agent pod for 90 days alongside existing work
- You have at least one CI failure type that is high-volume, well-understood, and currently handled manually

**Scoring interpretation:**

```
0–12:   Foundation gaps need addressing first. Focus on Section 1 items.
13–22:  Solid foundation, agent readiness gaps. Start with eval discipline and MCP basics.
23–32:  Ready to start the 90-day playbook. Begin with Days 1–30.
33–40:  Strong foundation. You may be able to compress Days 1–30 to 2–3 weeks.
```

**Close with:** A note that scoring yourself a 3 on "RBAC configured" because it exists but nobody has reviewed it in a year is how agent pilots fail. Be honest. The assessment is only useful if it reflects reality.

---

### `Agentic_DevOps/agent-boundary-design/README.md`

One paragraph. The IAM role an agent assumes is its blast radius. This folder contains the templates to define that boundary before any agent touches a pipeline.

---

### `Agentic_DevOps/agent-boundary-design/permission-matrix-template.md`

**Purpose:** The per-agent permission definition table. Fill this out for every agent before deployment.

**Format:**

```markdown
# Agent Permission Matrix

## Agent: [Name / Purpose]
## Deployment date:
## Owner (person accountable for this agent):
## Review date (max 90 days from deployment):

---

| Resource | Can READ | Can WRITE | Can EXECUTE | Requires Human Approval |
|----------|----------|-----------|-------------|------------------------|
| Source code repo | | | | |
| CI/CD pipeline config | | | | |
| Container registry | | | | |
| Kubernetes cluster (prod) | | | | |
| Kubernetes cluster (staging) | | | | |
| Infrastructure state (Terraform) | | | | |
| Secrets / credential store | | | | |
| Incident management system | | | | |
| Deployment tooling | | | | |
| Log/metrics store | | | | |

---

## Approval Gates

List every action this agent can take that requires human approval before execution:

1.
2.
3.

## Explicitly Out of Scope

List what this agent is explicitly NOT permitted to do, even if technically possible:

1.
2.
3.

## Retry Budget

- Maximum retries on failure: [number]
- Retry delay: [seconds]
- Circuit breaker behavior: [what happens when budget is exhausted]

## Audit Trail

- Where are agent actions logged: [location]
- Log retention: [duration]
- Who reviews logs and how often: [name/role, cadence]
```

**After the template, include a completed example** for a CI build failure analysis agent (read-only, advisory mode). Use realistic values, not placeholders.

---

### `Agentic_DevOps/agent-boundary-design/agents-md-template.md`

**Purpose:** A starter AGENTS.md file for teams to commit to their repo root. AGENTS.md is the per-repo configuration file that tells agents how to behave in that repository context.

**Must include sections for:**

- Repository context (what this repo is, what it does, what it doesn't do)
- Agent behavior rules (what agents are allowed to do in this repo)
- Off-limits (explicit prohibitions)
- CI/CD context (which pipeline, what triggers mean)
- Escalation (what to do when the agent is uncertain)
- Tool access (which MCP servers/tools are configured for this repo)

**Include a filled-out example** for a mid-sized monorepo with a Node.js service and Terraform infrastructure. Make it realistic enough that an engineer can use it as a starting point immediately.

---

### `Agentic_DevOps/eval-framework/README.md`

One paragraph. Eval-driven development is the same discipline as TDD applied to agents. If you wouldn't deploy code without tests, don't deploy agents without evals.

---

### `Agentic_DevOps/eval-framework/eval-suite-starter.md`

**Purpose:** A starter eval framework for the CI build failure analysis use case. Teams replace the example scenarios with their own.

**Structure:**

```
# Agent Eval Suite: CI Build Failure Analysis

## What This Is
## How to Use This
## Eval Scenario Template
## Example Scenarios (10 filled in)
## Scoring Methodology
## Trajectory Checklist
## Production Sampling Protocol
```

- Sample production runs weekly against these scenarios to detect drift

**Eval Scenario Template:**

```markdown
### Scenario [N]: [Short name]

**Input:** [The exact CI failure log or error message the agent receives]

**Context provided to agent:** [What additional context the agent has access to — repo README, AGENTS.md, recent commit history, etc.]

**Expected output:**

**Pass criteria:** Human reviewer agrees recommendation is correct or within acceptable range
**Fail criteria:** [specific failure conditions]
**Edge case:** [a variant of this scenario that should trigger a different response]
```

**Example Scenarios:** Write 10 realistic CI failure scenarios for a Node.js/Kubernetes environment. Include a variety:

- Failed integration test with ambiguous error (should flag uncertainty)
- Infra drift (Terraform plan fails)

Make scenarios 6-10 progressively harder and include the expected uncertainty/escalation behavior, not just the happy path.

**Scoring Methodology:**
- Each scenario: Pass (1) / Fail (0) / Partial (0.5)
- Minimum passing score for deployment: 8/10
- Partial credit criteria: recommendation is directionally correct but missing specificity

**Trajectory Checklist:**
What to verify in OpenTelemetry traces for each eval run:
- Did the agent call the correct tools in a logical sequence?
- Did it terminate when it should have (no runaway loops)?
- Did it express appropriate uncertainty on scenarios 8-10?
- Token usage within expected range?

**Production Sampling Protocol:**

- Compare against eval expectations
- Track drift: if accuracy on production samples drops >10% below eval suite score, review context configuration before continuing

---

### `Agentic_DevOps/stack-reference/README.md`

One paragraph. This is a point-in-time reference (March 2026). The landscape is moving fast. Check dates before citing any of this.

---

### `Agentic_DevOps/stack-reference/tool-landscape.md`

**Purpose:** The 4-layer stack reference for practitioners. Not a buying guide. Not ranked by preference.

**Layer 1: Foundation (You Probably Already Have This)**

Write one sentence per tool. No marketing descriptions. What it is, why it matters for agent deployment.

Include: Kubernetes, Docker, Terraform, GitHub Actions, ArgoCD, Prometheus, Grafana

**Layer 2: Platform Layer (The Infrastructure Agents Run On)**

**Observability Tools for Agents (Cross-cutting)**

Include: Datadog LLM Observability (GA June 2025), New Relic AI Monitoring (expanded Feb 2026), Grafana Assistant (GA Oct 2025), Langfuse (open source), OpenTelemetry GenAI SIG (standardizing semantic conventions — watch this space)

---

### `Agentic_DevOps/resources.md`

**Purpose:** Curated external reading list. No link dumps.

**Format:** Annotated list. For each resource: title, source, one sentence on why it's worth reading and what question it answers.

**Must include:**

- 2025 DORA Report (delivery throughput vs. stability finding)
- Opsera 2026 research (4.6x PR review time, 48% faster PR creation, 15-18% more vulnerabilities in AI-generated PRs)
- Snyk February 2026 research (13.4% of public agent skills had critical vulnerabilities, 76 confirmed malicious payloads)

- AWS DevOps Agent production writeup (eval framework source)

**Do not include:**
- Generic "AI is changing everything" think pieces
- Vendor whitepapers without cited data
- Anything without a date (undated content is useless in this space)

---

## Quality Gates

Before considering any file complete, verify:

1. **No timing information visible anywhere.** Timing guidance belongs in speaker notes only, not in any collateral asset.
2. **Every statistic has a source.** If you can't source it, cut it or flag it with `<!-- UNSOURCED: verify before publishing -->`.
3. **Every file passes the "Monday test":** Can a reader open this file and know what to do this week? If no, it's not done.
4. **No duplicate content across files.** If two files cover the same point, one should reference the other.
5. **The permission matrix template has a filled example.** Templates without examples don't get used.
6. **The eval suite has 10 complete scenarios.** Not 3 with placeholders for the rest.
7. **Run `markdownlint` or equivalent.** Broken link references and malformed tables are embarrassing in public repos.

---

## What This Repo Is NOT Responsible For

- The slide deck (separate deliverable, already built)
- KodeKloud course links (add placeholders, do not hardcode URLs that may change)
- Certification roadmap details (out of scope, reference KodeKloud learning paths generally)
- Anything requiring live infrastructure to demonstrate

---

## Final Check

When all files are written, generate a `Agentic_DevOps/README.md` last, after you have full visibility into what was actually built. The README should reflect reality, not the spec.
", "filename": "/mnt/user-data/outputs/CLAUDE-agentic-devops-collateral-spec.md"}

content.text: File created successfully: /mnt/user-data/outputs/CLAUDE-agentic-devops-collateral-spec.md

Drop that file in the root of your repo as `CLAUDE.md` inside the `Agentic_DevOps/` folder and run `claude` from that directory. It will build the full structure autonomously.

A few decisions I made that you should know about:

**The 4-layer stack is the organizing spine.** Everything in `stack-reference/` maps to it, and the readiness assessment scores against it. That gives the whole repo a consistent mental model.

**The eval suite has 10 real scenarios, not placeholders.** I spec'd them to get progressively harder toward scenarios where the right answer is *express uncertainty and escalate*, not produce a fix. That's the part most teams skip and then wonder why their agent hallucinates confidently in production.

**The permission matrix template requires a completed example.** Templates without examples don't get used. Claude Code will fill in a realistic CI build failure analysis agent as the reference.

**Two things I deliberately left out:** Solomon Hykes/Container Use (per the earlier conversation — it's not relevant to this audience), and the evolution path from the Phuong doc (Scripts → CI/CD → GitOps etc.). Your Three Waves framework in the deck is the right vehicle for that story.

content.text:
Released by OpenAI in August 2025, AGENTS.md is a simple, universal standard that gives AI coding agents a consistent source of project-specific guidance needed to operate reliably across different repositories and toolchains.

Released by OpenAI in August 2025, AGENTS.md is a simple, universal standard that gives AI coding agents a consistent source of project-specific guidance needed to operate reliably across different repositories and toolchains. This markdown-based convention makes agent behavior far more predictable across diverse repositories and build systems. AGENTS.md has already been adopted by more than 60,000 open source projects and agent frameworks including Amp, Codex, Cursor, Devin, Factory, Gemini CLI, GitHub Copilot, Jules and VS Code among others. OpenAI was an early adopter of MCP and has contributed ACP, Codex CLI, and the Agents SDK and Apps SDK to support the open agentic ecosystem, building on shared, interoperable protocols. “For AI agents to reach their full potential, developers and enterprises need trustworthy infrastructure and accessible tools to build on.

KubeCon + CloudNativeCon Europe 2026 · 23-26 March · Amsterdam REGISTER NOW ... With founding contributions from Anthropic, Block, and OpenAI, the AAIF unites cutting-edge technology and open source governance to shape the future of open and accessible AI ... The Linux Foundation announced the formation of the Agentic AI Foundation (AAIF) with founding contributions of leading technical projects including Anthropic’s Model Context Protocol (MCP), Block’s goose, and OpenAI’s AGENTS.md. The AAIF provides a neutral, open foundation to ensure agentic AI evolves transparently and collaboratively.

MCP is the universal standard protocol for connecting AI models to tools, data and applications; goose is an open source, local-first AI agent framework that combines language models, extensible tools, and standardized MCP-based integration; AGENTS.md is a simple, universal standard that gives AI coding agents a consistent source of project-specific guidance needed to operate reliably across different repositories and toolchains. Platinum members include Amazon Web Services, Anthropic, Block, Bloomberg, Cloudflare, Google, Microsoft and OpenAI. SAN FRANCISCO, Dec. 9, 2025 – The Linux Foundation, the nonprofit organization enabling mass innovation through open source, today announced the formation of the Agentic AI Foundation (AAIF), and founding contributions of three leading projects driving innovation in open source AI; Anthropic’s Model Context Protocol (MCP), Block’s goose, and OpenAI’s AGENTS.md.

Released in early 2025, goose is an open source, local-first AI agent framework that combines language models, extensible tools, and standardized MCP-based integration to provide a structured, reliable, and trusted environment for building and executing agentic workflows. Developed and contributed by Block, the company behind Square, Cash App, Afterpay, TIDAL and a growing ecosystem of bitcoin projects, goose provides the practical infrastructure needed to advance agentic AI safely and consistently. “We're at a critical moment for AI. The technology that will define the next decade, that promises to be the biggest engine of economic growth since the Internet, can either remain closed and proprietary for the benefit of few, or be driven by open standards, open protocols, and open access for the benefit of all,” said Manik Surtani, Head of Open Source at Block.

content.title: The Agentic Web Explained: AGENTS.md, MCP vs A2A, and the Rise of Web 4.0 | NxCode
content.text:
AGENTS.md is a convention — a markdown file placed at the root of a code repository that provides instructions to AI agents working on that codebase. Think of it as a README.md for AI.

The Model Context Protocol (MCP) is an open-source standard created by Anthropic for connecting AI applications to external systems. Announced in November 2024 and rapidly adopted throughout 2025-2026, MCP provides a standardized way for AI agents to access data sources, tools, and workflows. Anthropic describes it as "a USB-C port for AI applications." Just as USB-C provides a single universal connector for charging, data transfer, and video output across all devices, MCP provides a single universal protocol for connecting any AI application to any external tool or data source. MCP follows a client-server architecture with three key participants: MCP Host: The AI application (e.g., Claude Code, VS Code, Claude Desktop) that coordinates connections · MCP Client: A component within the host that maintains a dedicated connection to one MCP server ·

AGENTS.md is a convention — a markdown file placed at the root of a code repository that provides instructions to AI agents working on that codebase. Think of it as a README.md for AI. Where README.md tells human developers how to set up and contribute to a project, AGENTS.md tells AI coding agents what coding standards to follow, what architecture decisions have been made, and what rules to respect. The concept emerged organically from the AI-assisted coding community in 2025. Different tools adopted their own variations: Anthropic's Claude Code uses CLAUDE.md, Cursor uses .cursorrules, Windsurf uses .windsurfrules, and the broader community settled on AGENTS.md as a tool-agnostic standard. The adoption numbers are staggering. As of early 2026, over 60,000 GitHub repositories contain some form of agent instruction file — whether AGENTS.md, CLAUDE.md, .cursorrules, or similar variants.

The internet is undergoing its most significant architectural shift since the invention of the web browser. In 2026, we are witnessing the emergence of the Agentic Web — a new paradigm where AI agents do not just assist humans but autonomously browse, transact, negotiate, and collaborate across the internet on our behalf. This is not a distant future. The infrastructure is being built right now. Anthropic has shipped the Model Context Protocol (MCP). Google has released the Agent-to-Agent (A2A) protocol. And a quiet revolution of AGENTS.md files is reshaping how AI agents interact with codebases across tens of thousands of GitHub repositories. This guide provides a comprehensive, developer-focused breakdown of every piece of this emerging ecosystem — what each protocol does, how they fit together, and what it all means for building software in 2026 and beyond.

MCP adoption has been remarkable. As of 2026, the protocol is supported by: Anthropic: Claude Code, Claude Desktop, Claude on the web · Microsoft: Visual Studio Code (via GitHub Copilot) ... The MCP server ecosystem now includes official connectors for Sentry, GitHub, Slack, Google Drive, PostgreSQL, filesystem access, and many more. Any developer can build a custom MCP server using SDKs available in Python, TypeScript, Java, Kotlin, C#, and Swift. Before MCP, every AI tool needed custom integrations with every external service. If you had 10 AI tools and 20 services, you needed 200 integrations (an M x N problem). MCP reduces this to an M + N problem: each AI tool implements MCP client support once, and each service implements an MCP server once. The Agent-to-Agent (A2A) protocol is an open standard created by Google for enabling communication between AI agents built on different frameworks and operated by different organizations.

content.text:
Unified Agent Conventions: With AGENTS.md, coding agents already share a common metadata format. This concept may generalize: we might see analogous standards (e.g. AGENTS.md for other domains, or open schemas for defining agent objectives).

The Agentic AI Foundation (AAIF) is a newly formed initiative hosted by the Linux Foundation (LF) to coordinate the development of open, interoperable infrastructure for agentic AI – systems that autonomously plan and execute tasks with minimal human guidance. Launched in December 2025 by founding members Anthropic, OpenAI, and Block (among others), AAIF consolidates major open-source contributions (Anthropic’s Model Context Protocol (MCP), Block’s Goose agent framework, OpenAI’s AGENTS.md convention) into a neutral consortium. Its mission is to ensure that as AI moves from passive “chatbot” tools to proactive agents, it does so on open standards that avoid fragmentation and vendor lock-in. Large technology companies (Google, Microsoft, AWS, Bloomberg, Cloudflare, etc.) and numerous startups have joined or signaled support, reflecting broad industry commitment.

Rapid Adoption by Developers: OpenAI reports that more than 60,000 open-source projects and agent frameworks had adopted AGENTS.md by late 2025 ([49]). SiliconANGLE similarly cites 40,000+ projects using AGENTS.md and 10,000+ published MCP servers ([39]). This indicates a large pre-existing base for AAIF projects. Enterprise Pilot Rates: A UiPath report (mid-2025) shows ~65% of organizations piloting or deploying agentic systems, with ~90% of executives planning increased investment in 2026 ([14]). However, industry experience tempers expectations: despite 60% expecting agents to reach production, only 30% actually do ([82]). This discrepancy suggests the first-mover enterprises and pilot projects will benefit most immediately, and that improved tools (from AAIF) could help others cross the gap. Performance Gains vs. Challenges: Multi-agent approaches can yield striking improvements: UiPath notes trials with up to 60% fewer errors and 40% faster execution in workflows compared to tradi

With 40% of agentic AI projects facing cancellation, learn why durable orchestration with Temporal.io is vital for reliable multi-agent workflows in production ... Learn about the impact of AI agents on B2B productivity in 2026. This analysis details enterprise adoption trends, case studies, and Anthropic's technical visio ...

Unified Agent Conventions: With AGENTS.md, coding agents already share a common metadata format. This concept may generalize: we might see analogous standards (e.g. AGENTS.md for other domains, or open schemas for defining agent objectives). AAIF’s structure could incubate such standards. For example, one established project in AAIF might define an “Agent Manifest” format to describe an agent’s identity, capabilities, or security properties. This would make building safe, discoverable agents easier. Cross-Platform Tooling: Tools like debugging suites, performance monitors, and agent orchestration dashboards could emerge. Because AAIF encourages open development, third parties may create such tools for any AAIF-compliant agent. For example, Microsoft’s Azure Guards or AWS Agents (hypothetical) might be built to work out-of-the-box with MCP or A2A networks.

content.title: Is that allowed? Authentication and authorization in Model Context Protocol - Stack Overflow
content.text:
How to implement this depends on the authentication and authorization schemes you implement. When using the Streamable HTTP transport, Token Exchange is a good standards-based choice if the resources behind the MCP server expect OAuth tokens. In this case, the MCP server provides the token used to authenticate the MCP client to an authorization server along with information about the downstream resource. The authorization server then can mint a new token which includes information about: ... This token can be provided to the downstream resource which can validate it and allow data access based on all the attributes. There are other solutions out there for ensuring the requests the MCP server makes on the MCP client's behalf are correct, but definitely choose one. If you choose a different approach, the MCP specification prohibits passing through tokens.

If you are reading this more than a few months into 2026, check the latest version of the specification and ensure you understand any changes. What is the current status of authentication and authorization in MCP? These occur at the transport layer. There are currently two supported transport technologies: ... Let’s take a look at each of these, and how authentication happens. This transport runs the MCP server as a local process. These are also called “local” MCP servers. All communication takes place over standard input and standard output. When you pipe a request to curl, you are usually implicitly authenticated. Similarly, with the stdio transport, no authentication between the MCP client and MCP server is required. The MCP server is started as a subprocess of the MCP client. Environment variables configure the MCP server. For example, you can set an environment variable with the value of an endpoint URL to the server.

We won't discuss the stdio transport again, since most of the authentication complexity occurs when you have a remote MCP server using the streamable HTTP transport. Let’s take a look at that transport next. This transport, introduced in the 2025-06-18 specification, uses the OAuth 2.1 protocol and other standards-based solutions to authenticate and authorize MCP clients. MCP servers that use this transport are called remote MCP servers, whether the MCP server lives on localhost, a private URL or a public URL. However, you can have a remote MCP server that requires no authentication or authorization. An example of this is the context7 MCP servers. These are remote MCP servers, but because they surface documentation and are read-only, they don’t require any verification of the MCP client. Such MCP servers are in the minority, though. Most MCP servers allow MCP clients to take action, and therefore need to know who the MCP client is and what it can do.

How to implement this depends on the authentication and authorization schemes you implement. When using the Streamable HTTP transport, Token Exchange is a good standards-based choice if the resources behind the MCP server expect OAuth tokens. In this case, the MCP server provides the token used to authenticate the MCP client to an authorization server along with information about the downstream resource. The authorization server then can mint a new token which includes information about: ... This token can be provided to the downstream resource which can validate it and allow data access based on all the attributes. There are other solutions out there for ensuring the requests the MCP server makes on the MCP client's behalf are correct, but definitely choose one. If you choose a different approach, the MCP specification prohibits passing through tokens.

As mentioned above, with remote servers, MCP authentication uses the OAuth 2.1 Authorization Code grant and several related standards. Any further references to an MCP server in this post refer to remote MCP servers. The flow starts with an unauthenticated MCP client such as Cursor or Claude Code. It ends with the MCP client having possession of an OAuth access token and presenting the token to the MCP server to gain access to data and functionality provided by it. ... Confusingly, the term “client” can refer both to the MCP client and an OAuth client, but in the rest of this doc, I’ll be referring to the OAuth client unless otherwise specified. Let's walk through each of these steps in this diagram in more detail. In step 1, an unauthenticated MCP client attempts to connect to an MCP server. The MCP server doesn't accept this request, and returns a 401 pointing to metadata.

MCP supports different authentication models and authorization patterns. Each model serves specific use cases. Your choice depends on security needs and infrastructure. ... OAuth 2.1 Flow. The main standard for MCP authentication. Uses authorization codes for user login. Includes PKCE for extra security. Works with external identity providers. API Key Authentication. Simple method using static keys. Good for machine-to-machine communication. Keys stored in environment variables. Not recommended for production systems. JWT Token Authentication. Self-contained tokens with user information. Includes signature validation and expiration times. Works well for stateless systems. Supports role-based access control. Device Flow. Special OAuth flow for local applications. User logs in on different device. Good for CLI tools and desktop apps. No browser required on the target device.

The MCP ecosystem is growing fast. It has significant implications for enterprise security. The technology went from concept to 1,000+ servers. This represents tremendous opportunity and serious challenges. ... Standards Are Maturing. The June 2026 update brought comprehensive overhauls. OAuth Resource Server classification became mandatory. This ended the era of delayed security. Enterprise Adoption Wave. Companies like Block, Apollo, and Sourcegraph use MCP. They use it for real business operations. This signals the technology's readiness for production. Security Evolution. MCP is experiencing rapid security maturation. Similar to early web applications. Better tools, clearer guidelines, and fewer incidents emerge. Registry Solution. The planned official MCP registry includes security validation. It will function as a curated marketplace. This helps separate legitimate tools from malicious ones.

The core MCP components include servers that expose capabilities, clients that consume services, and transport layers that handle communication. Resources define what data AI can access. Tools specify what actions AI can perform. Prompts guide AI behavior within defined boundaries. Risk Amplification. When AI can suddenly access everything, security flaws become catastrophic. One compromised token could give attackers access. They get access to your entire digital ecosystem. Enterprise Reality Check. Companies like Block and Apollo are running operations through MCP. This makes enterprise-grade security a basic requirement now. The Standardization Win. Custom integrations are security nightmares where connections become vulnerabilities. MCP standardizes everything completely. It makes security patterns repeatable across systems. Explosive Growth. The ecosystem grew from zero to 1,000+ servers quickly.

Understanding MCP security isn't just nice-to-have anymore. It's critical for anyone building AI systems in 2026. Here's why it matters so much. MCP stands for Model Context Protocol. It's the universal translator for AI systems. You can think of it as a bridge between worlds. Your AI can talk directly to Gmail servers. It accesses databases instantly. It controls Slack channels automatically. It connects to hundreds of business tools seamlessly. MCP eliminates the barriers between AI and applications. Before MCP, AI lived in isolation. It couldn't touch external services. It couldn't modify real data. MCP changes everything by creating secure communication channels. But here's where it gets interesting. Without proper security, this connection becomes your biggest vulnerability. Understanding MCP's role in modern authorization isn't just technical knowledge.
