Verifying Claude model specifications and reconciling positioning discrepancies.
Identified factual inaccuracies and gaps against March 2026 standards.

content.text:
Okay, here's the full audit. There are three factually wrong statements, two gaps that matter, and a few framing issues.

---

**🔴 WRONG — Needs Correction**

**1. "MCP lacks built-in security standards as of March 2026"**

This is flat-out outdated. The June 2025 MCP spec update formally classified MCP servers as OAuth 2.1 resource servers and mandated Resource Indicators per RFC 8707 to prevent token mis-redemption attacks. Modern MCP specifications define that MCP servers act as OAuth 2.1 resource servers, while MCP hosts act as OAuth clients on behalf of the user, and MCP servers should require a valid OAuth access token for any request.

The "no security standards" statement was accurate in late 2024. By March 2026 it's just wrong and will get you called out by anyone who's looked at the current spec. Correct it to: *"MCP's security model matured significantly in 2025 — OAuth 2.1 with PKCE is now the formal standard for remote MCP servers — but implementation quality across the 10,000+ published MCP servers varies wildly. The spec exists; enforcement doesn't."* That's honest and accurate.

**2. OTel GenAI SIG described as just "watch this space"**

The spec undersells the current state. The OTel GenAI conventions now cover agent spans, metrics, and events, with technology-specific conventions for Anthropic, AWS Bedrock, Azure AI Inference, and OpenAI. More importantly, Datadog now natively supports OpenTelemetry GenAI Semantic Conventions v1.37+, allowing teams to instrument once with OTel and analyze GenAI spans directly in LLM Observability without duplicating instrumentation. "Watch this space" implies it's theoretical. It's not — it's implementable today. The accurate framing is: conventions are still in **Development** status (not Stable), but major vendors have already adopted them. Teams should instrument to v1.37+ now.

**3. AGENTS.md described as an ops/DevOps agent convention**

Released by OpenAI in August 2025, AGENTS.md is a universal standard that gives AI *coding* agents a consistent source of project-specific guidance needed to operate reliably across different repositories and toolchains. It's a coding agent convention, not an ops agent convention. Your spec template treating it as a place to document runtime ops agent behavior and permissions is a category error. For ops agent governance you want proper IAM policy docs, RBAC manifests, and runbook entries — not AGENTS.md. The permission matrix template is the right artifact. AGENTS.md should be mentioned only as context/codebase documentation for coding agents if your CI agent needs repo awareness.

---

**🟡 GAPS — Should Be Added**

**4. A2A Protocol is missing entirely**

Google launched the open Agent2Agent (A2A) protocol in April 2025 with support from over 50 technology partners, designed to allow AI agents to communicate with each other, securely exchange information, and coordinate actions across enterprise platforms. It's now under the Linux Foundation. As of late 2025, A2A isn't dead but development has slowed significantly, and most of the AI agent ecosystem has consolidated around MCP — meaning MCP won the "agent-to-tool" layer and A2A is still relevant for "agent-to-agent" orchestration but hasn't achieved the same adoption. 

For your audience: A2A belongs in the stack reference under a one-line note in the multi-agent section — *"A2A exists and is under the Linux Foundation, but MCP has won the broader adoption battle. For this playbook's scope, you won't need A2A until Level 3."* Don't ignore it — senior engineers will know about it and wonder why it's absent.

**5. Agentic AI Foundation (AAIF) not mentioned**

The Linux Foundation announced the Agentic AI Foundation (AAIF) in December 2025, with founding contributions of MCP from Anthropic, goose from Block, and AGENTS.md from OpenAI, with platinum members including AWS, Anthropic, Block, Bloomberg, Cloudflare, Google, Microsoft, and OpenAI. This is the governance body for the standards your entire spec is built around. It should get one sentence in the resources.md and/or the stack reference — it tells your audience where to track how these standards evolve.

---

**🟢 HOLDS UP**

- The 90-day structure, decision gate, and maturity framing are sound.
- The permission matrix approach is correct — IAM-first is the right posture.
- Eval-driven development framing is accurate and aligned with how AWS and Anthropic are approaching this.
- OTel GenAI SIG is actively defining semantic conventions covering key areas and has expanded scope to provide instrumentation coverage for agents in Python and other languages — so recommending OTel as the instrumentation layer is correct.
- The "observability before autonomy" principle is the right framing and nothing in current practice contradicts it.

---

**Bottom line:** Two content corrections are mandatory before this goes to Claude Code — the MCP security statement and the AGENTS.md positioning. The A2A gap won't break the repo but a senior engineer will notice it. Want me to update the spec file with the corrections now?

content.text:
1	# Claude Code Spec: Agentic DevOps Webinar Collateral Repo
     2	
     3	## Context
     4	
     5	This spec builds out the downloadable/shareable collateral for the KodeKloud live webinar:
     6	**"Beyond Copilots: How Agentic AI is Rewriting the DevOps Playbook"**
     7	Live date: April 2026
     8	
     9	All output lives under `Agentic_DevOps/` inside the existing KodeKloud repo.
    10	Do not modify anything outside that folder.
    11	
    12	---
    13	
    14	## Repo Structure to Build
    15	
    16	```
    17	Agentic_DevOps/
    18	├── README.md
    19	├── 90-day-playbook/
    20	│   ├── README.md
    21	│   ├── playbook.md
    22	│   └── decision-gate-checklist.md
    23	├── readiness-assessment/
    24	│   ├── README.md
    25	│   └── assessment.md
    26	├── agent-boundary-design/
    27	│   ├── README.md
    28	│   ├── permission-matrix-template.md
    29	│   └── agents-md-template.md
    30	├── eval-framework/
    31	│   ├── README.md
    32	│   └── eval-suite-starter.md
    33	├── stack-reference/
    34	│   ├── README.md
    35	│   └── tool-landscape.md
    36	└── resources.md
    37	```
    38	
    39	---
    40	
    41	## Authoring Rules (Read Before Writing Anything)
    42	
    43	- **Tone:** Written for mid-to-senior DevOps and Platform engineers. No hand-holding, no marketing language. Treat the reader as someone who already knows Kubernetes, CI/CD, and observability.
    44	- **No hype.** Every claim must be either sourced (GitHub, DORA, Opsera, Datadog, Snyk, IDC) or explicitly framed as practitioner opinion.
    45	- **No bullet-point soup.** Use prose for explanations. Use tables and structured lists only where they genuinely aid scanning (permission matrices, checklists, tool comparisons).
    46	- **Every file must be immediately actionable.** If a section can't answer "so what do I do Monday?" it needs to be rewritten or cut.
    47	- **No placeholder filler.** If you don't have enough information to write a section properly, leave a clearly marked `<!-- TODO: [specific question] -->` comment rather than padding.
    48	- **Filenames are kebab-case.** No spaces, no underscores in filenames except the top-level `Agentic_DevOps/` folder name which is fixed.
    49	
    50	---
    51	
    52	## File-by-File Specifications
    53	
    54	---
    55	
    56	### `Agentic_DevOps/README.md`
    57	
    58	**Purpose:** Entry point. Orients the reader to what's here and why.
    59	
    60	**Must include:**
    61	- One-paragraph context: what this webinar covered and why this repo exists
    62	- A table mapping each folder to its use case and who it's for (individual engineer vs. team lead vs. engineering manager)
    63	- A "start here" recommendation based on three reader profiles:
    64	  - "I'm an individual engineer who wants to start with agents"
    65	  - "I'm a team lead trying to assess readiness"
    66	  - "I'm an engineering leader building a business case"
    67	- Links to all sub-folders
    68	
    69	**Tone anchor:** Write it like a senior engineer left this for their team on a shared Notion, not like a product landing page.
    70	
    71	---
    72	
    73	### `Agentic_DevOps/90-day-playbook/README.md`
    74	
    75	One paragraph explaining what the playbook is and what it is not. Specifically call out: this is NOT a vendor evaluation guide and NOT a full autonomous deployment plan. It's a governance-first proof of concept path.
    76	
    77	---
    78	
    79	### `Agentic_DevOps/90-day-playbook/playbook.md`
    80	
    81	**Purpose:** The primary collateral asset. The thing people download and bring to their team.
    82	
    83	**Structure:**
    84	
    85	```
    86	# 90-Day Agentic DevOps Getting Started Playbook
    87	
    88	## What This Is
    89	## The One Use Case to Start With
    90	## Days 1–30: Foundation
    91	## Days 31–60: Validation
    92	## Days 60 Decision Gate
    93	## Days 61–90: Controlled Expansion
    94	## What You Will Have Built by Day 90
    95	## What Comes Next (Level 3 preview)
    96	```
    97	
    98	**Section-by-section requirements:**
    99	
   100	**What This Is**
   101	- 3-4 sentences max. One use case, one team, advisory mode first.
   102	- Explicitly state what this does NOT cover: production write access, multi-agent systems, full pipeline automation.
   103	
   104	**The One Use Case to Start With**
   105	- Automated CI build failure analysis in advisory mode.
   106	- Explain the Elastic pattern: build fails → agent analyzes error → recommends fix → human approves commit.
   107	- Three reasons this is the right starting point:
   108	  1. Read-only in most implementations = near-zero blast radius
   109	  2. Measurable ROI from day one (track agent recommendation accuracy vs. manual triage time)
   110	  3. Reproducible — connects to existing CI tooling, no new infrastructure required
   111	
   112	**Days 1–30: Foundation**
   113	
   114	Cover these three parallel workstreams in prose:
   115	
   116	1. **Build your eval suite first.** Not after the agent is deployed. Before. Identify your 10 most common CI failure scenarios. These become your test cases. If you wouldn't deploy code without tests, don't deploy agents without evals. Reference AWS's four-part eval framework: evaluations (LLM judges + pass@k metrics), trajectory visualization (OpenTelemetry), fast feedback loops (run in minutes not hours), production sampling.
   117	
   118	2. **Define permissions explicitly.** Create a separate service principal for the agent. Read-only access only. Document the IAM policy. Commit it to AGENTS.md in the repo root. Include a reference to the permission matrix template in `agent-boundary-design/`.
   119	
   120	3. **Form your Agentic AI Pod.** 2-3 engineers. Defined ownership: eval suite, observability, governance documentation. Someone must be accountable or it drifts.
   121	
   122	**Days 31–60: Validation**
   123	
   124	Write this section around the principle: generate data, not enthusiasm.
   125	
   126	- Agents remain in advisory mode. They recommend. Humans approve. No exceptions during this phase.
   127	- Add observability before adding anything else. Pick one: Datadog LLM Observability, New Relic AI Monitoring, Grafana (GA Oct 2025), or Langfuse (open source). Reference `eval-framework/` for what to instrument.
   128	- Four metrics to track:
   129	  1. Agent recommendation accuracy (% of time humans agree with recommendation)
   130	  2. False positive rate
   131	  3. Net time saved (time saved by agent recommendation minus time spent reviewing it)
   132	  4. Human override frequency (your leading governance health indicator)
   133	
   134	**Days 60 Decision Gate**
   135	
   136	Format this as a clear binary:
   137	
   138	```
   139	≥70% human agreement rate → proceed to Days 61–90
   140	<70% human agreement rate → tune context, improve eval suite, stay in advisory mode
   141	```
   142	
   143	Include a note: if you're below 70%, the fix is almost never "better prompts." It's either bad context (the agent doesn't have access to enough information about your specific codebase/environment) or the wrong use case (the failure modes you're targeting require architectural knowledge the agent can't reason about). Diagnose before you iterate.
   144	
   145	Note on the 70% threshold: this is a starting benchmark, not an industry standard. Tune it to your organization's risk tolerance. High-stakes environments (financial services, healthcare) may require 85%+.
   146	
   147	**Days 61–90: Controlled Expansion**
   148	
   149	Add one second use case: AI-assisted incident triage (read-only data surfacing + hypothesis formation, human confirms before any remediation).
   150	
   151	Explicit "not yet" list:
   152	- Write access to production
   153	- Autonomous remediation
   154	- Multi-agent systems
   155	- Full pipeline automation
   156	
   157	Share learnings across teams as a story, not a mandate. Document what the agent got right, what it got wrong, what you had to tune. That's what builds organizational trust faster than any pilot report.
   158	
   159	**What You Will Have Built by Day 90**
   160	
   161	Present this as a concrete inventory, not a summary:
   162	- One agentic workflow in production (advisory mode, CI build failure analysis)
   163	- Eval suite covering your top 10 failure scenarios with pass/fail tracking
   164	- Agent observability dashboards (token cost, recommendation accuracy, override frequency)
   165	- IAM documentation for agent permissions committed to the repo
   166	- One incident triage workflow running in advisory mode
   167	- The data set to make the case for expanding scope
   168	
   169	Close with: IDC 2026 estimates the majority of organizations are at Level 1 maturity (individual ad-hoc AI tool use, ungoverned). By day 90 you are at Level 2 (AI in pipeline stages, human approval gates, documented policies). That puts you ahead of 85%+ of the industry — without a breach and without a failed pilot.
   170	
   171	**What Comes Next**
   172	- Briefly name Level 3 capabilities without making them sound imminent: write access to staging, agent-to-agent delegation, policy-as-code guardrails
	< truncated lines 173-373 >
   374	
   375	**How to Use This:**
   376	- Run the eval suite against your agent before initial deployment
   377	- Run it again after any context change (new AGENTS.md, new tool access, new model version)
   378	- Sample production runs weekly against these scenarios to detect drift
   379	
   380	**Eval Scenario Template:**
   381	
   382	```markdown
   383	### Scenario [N]: [Short name]
   384	
   385	**Input:** [The exact CI failure log or error message the agent receives]
   386	
   387	**Context provided to agent:** [What additional context the agent has access to — repo README, AGENTS.md, recent commit history, etc.]
   388	
   389	**Expected output:**
   390	- Root cause identification: [what it should identify]
   391	- Recommended fix: [what it should recommend]
   392	- Confidence signal: [should it express uncertainty or flag for human review?]
   393	- What it should NOT do: [explicit negative criteria]
   394	
   395	**Pass criteria:** Human reviewer agrees recommendation is correct or within acceptable range
   396	**Fail criteria:** [specific failure conditions]
   397	**Edge case:** [a variant of this scenario that should trigger a different response]
   398	```
   399	
   400	**Example Scenarios:** Write 10 realistic CI failure scenarios for a Node.js/Kubernetes environment. Include a variety:
   401	- Dependency version conflict
   402	- Failed linting (straightforward)
   403	- Failed integration test with ambiguous error (should flag uncertainty)
   404	- Infra drift (Terraform plan fails)
   405	- Flaky test (should recommend investigation, not a code fix)
   406	- Missing secret in environment
   407	- Image pull failure
   408	- OOM kill in test container
   409	- Test timeout (ambiguous — could be code or environment)
   410	- Breaking change in upstream dependency (should flag as needing human architectural decision, not recommend a fix)
   411	
   412	Make scenarios 6-10 progressively harder and include the expected uncertainty/escalation behavior, not just the happy path.
   413	
   414	**Scoring Methodology:**
   415	- Each scenario: Pass (1) / Fail (0) / Partial (0.5)
   416	- Minimum passing score for deployment: 8/10
   417	- Partial credit criteria: recommendation is directionally correct but missing specificity
   418	
   419	**Trajectory Checklist:**
   420	What to verify in OpenTelemetry traces for each eval run:
   421	- Did the agent call the correct tools in a logical sequence?
   422	- Did it terminate when it should have (no runaway loops)?
   423	- Did it express appropriate uncertainty on scenarios 8-10?
   424	- Token usage within expected range?
   425	
   426	**Production Sampling Protocol:**
   427	- Sample 10% of production agent runs weekly
   428	- Compare against eval expectations
   429	- Track drift: if accuracy on production samples drops >10% below eval suite score, review context configuration before continuing
   430	
   431	---
   432	
   433	### `Agentic_DevOps/stack-reference/README.md`
   434	
   435	One paragraph. This is a point-in-time reference (March 2026). The landscape is moving fast. Check dates before citing any of this.
   436	
   437	---
   438	
   439	### `Agentic_DevOps/stack-reference/tool-landscape.md`
   440	
   441	**Purpose:** The 4-layer stack reference for practitioners. Not a buying guide. Not ranked by preference.
   442	
   443	**Layer 1: Foundation (You Probably Already Have This)**
   444	
   445	Write one sentence per tool. No marketing descriptions. What it is, why it matters for agent deployment.
   446	
   447	Include: Kubernetes, Docker, Terraform, GitHub Actions, ArgoCD, Prometheus, Grafana
   448	
   449	**Layer 2: Platform Layer (The Infrastructure Agents Run On)**
   450	
   451	Include: Kubernetes Gateway API, Dapr, KEDA, Service mesh (Istio/Linkerd — note the tradeoffs honestly)
   452	
   453	For each: one sentence on what it is, one sentence on why it's relevant to agentic workloads specifically (not generic value).
   454	
   455	**Layer 3: Agent Infrastructure (Where Most Teams Need to Build Now)**
   456	
   457	Include:
   458	- MCP (Model Context Protocol): the integration standard. How agents connect to tools. Note: no built-in security standards as of March 2026.
   459	- KAgent: deploying and managing AI agents on Kubernetes (KodeKloud course)
   460	- LangGraph 1.0: multi-step agent orchestration, durable execution, human-in-the-loop as first-class feature. 47M+ PyPI downloads, GA. Used by Uber, LinkedIn, Klarna.
   461	- Dapr Agents: CNCF graduated, sidecar pattern, state persists through failures, cloud-agnostic
   462

{"language": "markdown", "code": "# Claude Code Spec: Agentic DevOps Webinar Collateral Repo

## Context

This spec builds out the downloadable/shareable collateral for the KodeKloud live webinar:
**"Beyond Copilots: How Agentic AI is Rewriting the DevOps Playbook"**
Live date: April 2026

All output lives under `Agentic_DevOps/` inside the existing KodeKloud repo.
Do not modify anything outside that folder.

---

## Repo Structure to Build

```
Agentic_DevOps/
├── README.md
├── 90-day-playbook/
│   ├── README.md
│   ├── playbook.md
│   └── decision-gate-checklist.md
├── readiness-assessment/
│   ├── README.md
│   └── assessment.md
├── agent-boundary-design/
│   ├── README.md
│   ├── permission-matrix-template.md
│   └── agents-md-template.md
├── eval-framework/
│   ├── README.md
│   └── eval-suite-starter.md
├── stack-reference/
│   ├── README.md
│   └── tool-landscape.md
└── resources.md
```

---

## Authoring Rules (Read Before Writing Anything)

- **Tone:** Written for mid-to-senior DevOps and Platform engineers. No hand-holding, no marketing language. Treat the reader as someone who already knows Kubernetes, CI/CD, and observability.
- **No hype.** Every claim must be either sourced (GitHub, DORA, Opsera, Datadog, Snyk, IDC) or explicitly framed as practitioner opinion.
- **No bullet-point soup.** Use prose for explanations. Use tables and structured lists only where they genuinely aid scanning (permission matrices, checklists, tool comparisons).
- **Every file must be immediately actionable.** If a section can't answer "so what do I do Monday?" it needs to be rewritten or cut.
- **No placeholder filler.** If you don't have enough information to write a section properly, leave a clearly marked `<!-- TODO: [specific question] -->` comment rather than padding.
- **Filenames are kebab-case.** No spaces, no underscores in filenames except the top-level `Agentic_DevOps/` folder name which is fixed.

---

## File-by-File Specifications

---

### `Agentic_DevOps/README.md`

**Purpose:** Entry point. Orients the reader to what's here and why.

**Must include:**
- One-paragraph context: what this webinar covered and why this repo exists
- A table mapping each folder to its use case and who it's for (individual engineer vs. team lead vs. engineering manager)
- A "start here" recommendation based on three reader profiles:
  - "I'm an individual engineer who wants to start with agents"
  - "I'm a team lead trying to assess readiness"
  - "I'm an engineering leader building a business case"
- Links to all sub-folders

**Tone anchor:** Write it like a senior engineer left this for their team on a shared Notion, not like a product landing page.

---

### `Agentic_DevOps/90-day-playbook/README.md`

One paragraph explaining what the playbook is and what it is not. Specifically call out: this is NOT a vendor evaluation guide and NOT a full autonomous deployment plan. It's a governance-first proof of concept path.

---

### `Agentic_DevOps/90-day-playbook/playbook.md`

**Purpose:** The primary collateral asset. The thing people download and bring to their team.

**Structure:**

```
# 90-Day Agentic DevOps Getting Started Playbook

## What This Is
## The One Use Case to Start With
## Days 1–30: Foundation
## Days 31–60: Validation
## Days 60 Decision Gate
## Days 61–90: Controlled Expansion
## What You Will Have Built by Day 90
## What Comes Next (Level 3 preview)
```

**Section-by-section requirements:**

**What This Is**
- 3-4 sentences max. One use case, one team, advisory mode first.
- Explicitly state what this does NOT cover: production write access, multi-agent systems, full pipeline automation.

**The One Use Case to Start With**
- Automated CI build failure analysis in advisory mode.
- Explain the Elastic pattern: build fails → agent analyzes error → recommends fix → human approves commit.
- Three reasons this is the right starting point:
  1. Read-only in most implementations = near-zero blast radius
  2. Measurable ROI from day one (track agent recommendation accuracy vs. manual triage time)
  3. Reproducible — connects to existing CI tooling, no new infrastructure required

**Days 1–30: Foundation**

Cover these three parallel workstreams in prose:

1. **Build your eval suite first.** Not after the agent is deployed. Before. Identify your 10 most common CI failure scenarios. These become your test cases. If you wouldn't deploy code without tests, don't deploy agents without evals. Reference AWS's four-part eval framework: evaluations (LLM judges + pass@k metrics), trajectory visualization (OpenTelemetry), fast feedback loops (run in minutes not hours), production sampling.

2. **Define permissions explicitly.** Create a separate service principal for the agent. Read-only access only. Document the IAM policy. Commit it to AGENTS.md in the repo root. Include a reference to the permission matrix template in `agent-boundary-design/`.

3. **Form your Agentic AI Pod.** 2-3 engineers. Defined ownership: eval suite, observability, governance documentation. Someone must be accountable or it drifts.

**Days 31–60: Validation**

Write this section around the principle: generate data, not enthusiasm.

- Agents remain in advisory mode. They recommend. Humans approve. No exceptions during this phase.
- Add observability before adding anything else. Pick one: Datadog LLM Observability, New Relic AI Monitoring, Grafana (GA Oct 2025), or Langfuse (open source). Reference `eval-framework/` for what to instrument.
- Four metrics to track:
  1. Agent recommendation accuracy (% of time humans agree with recommendation)
  2. False positive rate
  3. Net time saved (time saved by agent recommendation minus time spent reviewing it)
  4. Human override frequency (your leading governance health indicator)

**Days 60 Decision Gate**

Format this as a clear binary:

```
≥70% human agreement rate → proceed to Days 61–90
<70% human agreement rate → tune context, improve eval suite, stay in advisory mode
```

Include a note: if you're below 70%, the fix is almost never "better prompts." It's either bad context (the agent doesn't have access to enough information about your specific codebase/environment) or the wrong use case (the failure modes you're targeting require architectural knowledge the agent can't reason about). Diagnose before you iterate.

Note on the 70% threshold: this is a starting benchmark, not an industry standard. Tune it to your organization's risk tolerance. High-stakes environments (financial services, healthcare) may require 85%+.

**Days 61–90: Controlled Expansion**

Add one second use case: AI-assisted incident triage (read-only data surfacing + hypothesis formation, human confirms before any remediation).

Explicit "not yet" list:
- Write access to production
- Autonomous remediation
- Multi-agent systems
- Full pipeline automation

Share learnings across teams as a story, not a mandate. Document what the agent got right, what it got wrong, what you had to tune. That's what builds organizational trust faster than any pilot report.

**What You Will Have Built by Day 90**

Present this as a concrete inventory, not a summary:
- One agentic workflow in production (advisory mode, CI build failure analysis)
- Eval suite covering your top 10 failure scenarios with pass/fail tracking
- Agent observability dashboards (token cost, recommendation accuracy, override frequency)
- IAM documentation for agent permissions committed to the repo
- One incident triage workflow running in advisory mode
- The data set to make the case for expanding scope

Close with: IDC 2026 estimates the majority of organizations are at Level 1 maturity (individual ad-hoc AI tool use, ungoverned). By day 90 you are at Level 2 (AI in pipeline stages, human approval gates, documented policies). That puts you ahead of 85%+ of the industry — without a breach and without a failed pilot.

**What Comes Next**
- Briefly name Level 3 capabilities without making them sound imminent: write access to staging, agent-to-agent delegation, policy-as-code guardrails
< truncated lines 173-373 >

**How to Use This:**
- Run the eval suite against your agent before initial deployment
- Run it again after any context change (new AGENTS.md, new tool access, new model version)
- Sample production runs weekly against these scenarios to detect drift

**Eval Scenario Template:**

```markdown
### Scenario [N]: [Short name]

**Input:** [The exact CI failure log or error message the agent receives]

**Context provided to agent:** [What additional context the agent has access to — repo README, AGENTS.md, recent commit history, etc.]

**Expected output:**
- Root cause identification: [what it should identify]
- Recommended fix: [what it should recommend]
- Confidence signal: [should it express uncertainty or flag for human review?]
- What it should NOT do: [explicit negative criteria]

**Pass criteria:** Human reviewer agrees recommendation is correct or within acceptable range
**Fail criteria:** [specific failure conditions]
**Edge case:** [a variant of this scenario that should trigger a different response]
```

**Example Scenarios:** Write 10 realistic CI failure scenarios for a Node.js/Kubernetes environment. Include a variety:
- Dependency version conflict
- Failed linting (straightforward)
- Failed integration test with ambiguous error (should flag uncertainty)
- Infra drift (Terraform plan fails)
- Flaky test (should recommend investigation, not a code fix)
- Missing secret in environment
- Image pull failure
- OOM kill in test container
- Test timeout (ambiguous — could be code or environment)
- Breaking change in upstream dependency (should flag as needing human architectural decision, not recommend a fix)

Make scenarios 6-10 progressively harder and include the expected uncertainty/escalation behavior, not just the happy path.

**Scoring Methodology:**
- Each scenario: Pass (1) / Fail (0) / Partial (0.5)
- Minimum passing score for deployment: 8/10
- Partial credit criteria: recommendation is directionally correct but missing specificity

**Trajectory Checklist:**
What to verify in OpenTelemetry traces for each eval run:
- Did the agent call the correct tools in a logical sequence?
- Did it terminate when it should have (no runaway loops)?
- Did it express appropriate uncertainty on scenarios 8-10?
- Token usage within expected range?

**Production Sampling Protocol:**
- Sample 10% of production agent runs weekly
- Compare against eval expectations
- Track drift: if accuracy on production samples drops >10% below eval suite score, review context configuration before continuing

---

### `Agentic_DevOps/stack-reference/README.md`

One paragraph. This is a point-in-time reference (March 2026). The landscape is moving fast. Check dates before citing any of this.

---

### `Agentic_DevOps/stack-reference/tool-landscape.md`

**Purpose:** The 4-layer stack reference for practitioners. Not a buying guide. Not ranked by preference.

**Layer 1: Foundation (You Probably Already Have This)**

Write one sentence per tool. No marketing descriptions. What it is, why it matters for agent deployment.

Include: Kubernetes, Docker, Terraform, GitHub Actions, ArgoCD, Prometheus, Grafana

**Layer 2: Platform Layer (The Infrastructure Agents Run On)**

Include: Kubernetes Gateway API, Dapr, KEDA, Service mesh (Istio/Linkerd — note the tradeoffs honestly)

For each: one sentence on what it is, one sentence on why it's relevant to agentic workloads specifically (not generic value).

**Layer 3: Agent Infrastructure (Where Most Teams Need to Build Now)**

Include:
- MCP (Model Context Protocol): the integration standard. How agents connect to tools. Note: no built-in security standards as of March 2026.
- KAgent: deploying and managing AI agents on Kubernetes (KodeKloud course)
- LangGraph 1.0: multi-step agent orchestration, durable execution, human-in-the-loop as first-class feature. 47M+ PyPI downloads, GA. Used by Uber, LinkedIn, Klarna.
- Dapr Agents: CNCF graduated, sidecar pattern, state persists through failures, cloud-agnostic

For each: be honest about maturity. Note where things are GA vs. preview vs. experimental.

**Layer 4: Agent Intelligence (Building and Running the Models)**

Include: Claude Code (Opus 4.6, 14.5-hour task horizon, MCP native), GitHub Copilot Coding Agent (GA Sept 2025), Google ADK (live), AWS Bedrock, OpenAI Codex (relaunched May 2025 as GPT-5.3-Codex)

For each: one concrete production capability, one honest limitation.

**Observability Tools for Agents (Cross-cutting)**

This deserves its own subsection because it's non-negotiable infrastructure before increasing agent autonomy.

Include: Datadog LLM Observability (GA June 2025), New Relic AI Monitoring (expanded Feb 2026), Grafana Assistant (GA Oct 2025), Langfuse (open source), OpenTelemetry GenAI SIG (standardizing semantic conventions — watch this space)

Agent-specific metrics every team needs to instrument (not optional):
- Reasoning loop count
- Tool call success/failure rate
- Token usage and cost per invocation
- Human override frequency
- Loop/stall detection

**The Four Failure Modes (Reference)**

Brief entries — detailed treatment in the playbook. Point to `90-day-playbook/playbook.md` for full context.
1. Hallucination propagation
2. Retry loops without circuit breakers
3. State drift (no audit trail)
4. Deploying everywhere on day one

---

### `Agentic_DevOps/resources.md`

**Purpose:** Curated external reading list. No link dumps.

**Format:** Annotated list. For each resource: title, source, one sentence on why it's worth reading and what question it answers.

**Must include:**

- 2025 DORA Report (delivery throughput vs. stability finding)
- Opsera 2026 research (4.6x PR review time, 48% faster PR creation, 15-18% more vulnerabilities in AI-generated PRs)
- Snyk February 2026 research (13.4% of public agent skills had critical vulnerabilities, 76 confirmed malicious payloads)
- Forrester 2026 prediction on agentic AI breach
- IDC 2026 maturity model research
- AWS DevOps Agent production writeup (eval framework source)
- Datadog Bits AI SRE Agent GA announcement
- Block/Goose PagerDuty MCP integration writeup
- Elastic self-healing CI/CD case study
- Bret Fisher / Agentic DevOps podcast (agenticdevops.fm) — note: practitioner perspective, not research

**Do not include:**
- Generic "AI is changing everything" think pieces
- Vendor whitepapers without cited data
- Anything without a date (undated content is useless in this space)

---

## Quality Gates

Before considering any file complete, verify:

1. **No timing information visible anywhere.** Timing guidance belongs in speaker notes only, not in any collateral asset.
2. **Every statistic has a source.** If you can't source it, cut it or flag it with `<!-- UNSOURCED: verify before publishing -->`.
3. **Every file passes the "Monday test":** Can a reader open this file and know what to do this week? If no, it's not done.
4. **No duplicate content across files.** If two files cover the same point, one should reference the other.
5. **The permission matrix template has a filled example.** Templates without examples don't get used.
6. **The eval suite has 10 complete scenarios.** Not 3 with placeholders for the rest.
7. **Run `markdownlint` or equivalent.** Broken link references and malformed tables are embarrassing in public repos.

---

## What This Repo Is NOT Responsible For

- The slide deck (separate deliverable, already built)
- KodeKloud course links (add placeholders, do not hardcode URLs that may change)
- Certification roadmap details (out of scope, reference KodeKloud learning paths generally)
- Anything requiring live infrastructure to demonstrate

---

## Final Check

When all files are written, generate a `Agentic_DevOps/README.md` last, after you have full visibility into what was actually built. The README should reflect reality, not the spec.

173	- One sentence: "The teams who define what Level 3 looks like are building it now, on top of the Level 2 foundation you just built."
   174	
   175	---
   176	
   177	### `Agentic_DevOps/90-day-playbook/decision-gate-checklist.md`
   178	
   179	**Purpose:** A printable/shareable checklist for the Day 60 gate review. Designed to be used in an actual team meeting.
   180	
   181	**Format:** Checkboxes in markdown. Three sections:
   182	
   183	1. **Data Requirements** (must have all of these before the meeting)
   184	   - 30+ agent recommendations logged and reviewed
   185	   - Recommendation accuracy rate calculated
   186	   - False positive rate calculated
   187	   - Human override frequency tracked
   188	   - Net time saved documented with actual numbers
   189	
   190	2. **Governance Requirements** (must have all of these)
   191	   - IAM policy for agent service principal documented and reviewed
   192	   - AGENTS.md committed to repo and reviewed by security
   193	   - Eval suite passing on all 10 baseline scenarios
   194	   - Observability dashboard live and showing last 30 days
   195	
   196	3. **Go / No-Go Decision**
   197	   - If all data requirements met AND accuracy ≥70%: proceed to Days 61–90
   198	   - If data requirements met but accuracy <70%: diagnosis required before proceeding (see playbook)
   199	   - If data requirements not met: extend Days 31–60 by two weeks, identify the gap
   200	
   201	---
   202	
   203	### `Agentic_DevOps/readiness-assessment/README.md`
   204	
   205	One paragraph. This assessment is for teams, not individuals. Run it in a 45-minute team session, not solo.
   206	
   207	---
   208	
   209	### `Agentic_DevOps/readiness-assessment/assessment.md`
   210	
   211	**Purpose:** The team self-assessment scorecard referenced in the webinar. Maps directly to the 4-layer stack.
   212	
   213	**Format:** Four scored sections (0–3 per item, total score at end with interpretation).
   214	
   215	**Section 1: Foundation Layer (Your Existing Infrastructure)**
   216	
   217	Score each 0 (not in place), 1 (partially), 2 (solid), 3 (strong/documented):
   218	
   219	- Kubernetes in production with RBAC configured
   220	- CI/CD pipeline with automated testing (not just build)
   221	- Observability stack (metrics, logs, traces) — not just uptime monitoring
   222	- IaC (Terraform or equivalent) with state management
   223	- Incident response runbooks that are current and actually used
   224	- On-call rotation with documented escalation paths
   225	
   226	**Section 2: Platform Readiness**
   227	
   228	- Kubernetes Gateway API or service mesh in use
   229	- Prometheus/Grafana or equivalent with alerting rules that fire correctly
   230	- Container image scanning in CI pipeline
   231	- RBAC policies documented and reviewed in the last 6 months
   232	- Secrets management (not hardcoded, not in env vars in plain text)
   233	
   234	**Section 3: Agent Readiness**
   235	
   236	- Team has read and can explain what MCP is
   237	- At least one engineer has built or run a basic agent workflow (locally is fine)
   238	- Eval/testing discipline exists for current automation (you test your pipelines)
   239	- IAM hygiene: principle of least privilege is actually practiced, not just in policy docs
   240	- Your team has had an explicit conversation about where you would NOT deploy an agent
   241	
   242	**Section 4: Organizational Readiness**
   243	
   244	- Engineering leadership is aware of and supportive of an agent pilot
   245	- Security team has been briefed (even informally) on agent plans
   246	- You have 2-3 engineers who can own an agent pod for 90 days alongside existing work
   247	- You have at least one CI failure type that is high-volume, well-understood, and currently handled manually
   248	
   249	**Scoring interpretation:**
   250	
   251	```
   252	0–12:   Foundation gaps need addressing first. Focus on Section 1 items.
   253	13–22:  Solid foundation, agent readiness gaps. Start with eval discipline and MCP basics.
   254	23–32:  Ready to start the 90-day playbook. Begin with Days 1–30.
   255	33–40:  Strong foundation. You may be able to compress Days 1–30 to 2–3 weeks.
   256	```
   257	
   258	**Close with:** A note that scoring yourself a 3 on "RBAC configured" because it exists but nobody has reviewed it in a year is how agent pilots fail. Be honest. The assessment is only useful if it reflects reality.
   259	
   260	---
   261	
   262	### `Agentic_DevOps/agent-boundary-design/README.md`
   263	
   264	One paragraph. The IAM role an agent assumes is its blast radius. This folder contains the templates to define that boundary before any agent touches a pipeline.
   265	
   266	---
   267	
   268	### `Agentic_DevOps/agent-boundary-design/permission-matrix-template.md`
   269	
   270	**Purpose:** The per-agent permission definition table. Fill this out for every agent before deployment.
   271	
   272	**Format:**
   273	
   274	```markdown
   275	# Agent Permission Matrix
   276	
   277	## Agent: [Name / Purpose]
   278	## Deployment date:
   279	## Owner (person accountable for this agent):
   280	## Review date (max 90 days from deployment):
   281	
   282	---
   283	
   284	| Resource | Can READ | Can WRITE | Can EXECUTE | Requires Human Approval |
   285	|----------|----------|-----------|-------------|------------------------|
   286	| Source code repo | | | | |
   287	| CI/CD pipeline config | | | | |
   288	| Container registry | | | | |
   289	| Kubernetes cluster (prod) | | | | |
   290	| Kubernetes cluster (staging) | | | | |
   291	| Infrastructure state (Terraform) | | | | |
   292	| Secrets / credential store | | | | |
   293	| Incident management system | | | | |
   294	| Deployment tooling | | | | |
   295	| Log/metrics store | | | | |
   296	
   297	---
   298	
   299	## Approval Gates
   300	
   301	List every action this agent can take that requires human approval before execution:
   302	
   303	1.
   304	2.
   305	3.
   306	
   307	## Explicitly Out of Scope
   308	
   309	List what this agent is explicitly NOT permitted to do, even if technically possible:
   310	
   311	1.
   312	2.
   313	3.
   314	
   315	## Retry Budget
   316	
   317	- Maximum retries on failure: [number]
   318	- Retry delay: [seconds]
   319	- Circuit breaker behavior: [what happens when budget is exhausted]
   320	
   321	## Audit Trail
   322	
   323	- Where are agent actions logged: [location]
   324	- Log retention: [duration]
   325	- Who reviews logs and how often: [name/role, cadence]
   326	```
   327	
   328	**After the template, include a completed example** for a CI build failure analysis agent (read-only, advisory mode). Use realistic values, not placeholders.
   329	
   330	---
   331	
   332	### `Agentic_DevOps/agent-boundary-design/agents-md-template.md`
   333	
   334	**Purpose:** A starter AGENTS.md file for teams to commit to their repo root. AGENTS.md is the per-repo configuration file that tells agents how to behave in that repository context.
   335	
   336	**Must include sections for:**
   337	
   338	- Repository context (what this repo is, what it does, what it doesn't do)
   339	- Agent behavior rules (what agents are allowed to do in this repo)
   340	- Off-limits (explicit prohibitions)
   341	- CI/CD context (which pipeline, what triggers mean)
   342	- Escalation (what to do when the agent is uncertain)
   343	- Tool access (which MCP servers/tools are configured for this repo)
   344	
   345	**Include a filled-out example** for a mid-sized monorepo with a Node.js service and Terraform infrastructure. Make it realistic enough that an engineer can use it as a starting point immediately.
   346	
   347	---
   348	
   349	### `Agentic_DevOps/eval-framework/README.md`
   350	
   351	One paragraph. Eval-driven development is the same discipline as TDD applied to agents. If you wouldn't deploy code without tests, don't deploy agents without evals.
   352	
   353	---
   354	
   355	### `Agentic_DevOps/eval-framework/eval-suite-starter.md`

display_content.json_block:
{"language": "markdown", "code": "- One sentence: "The teams who define what Level 3 looks like are building it now, on top of the Level 2 foundation you just built."

---

### `Agentic_DevOps/90-day-playbook/decision-gate-checklist.md`

**Purpose:** A printable/shareable checklist for the Day 60 gate review. Designed to be used in an actual team meeting.

**Format:** Checkboxes in markdown. Three sections:

1. **Data Requirements** (must have all of these before the meeting)
   - 30+ agent recommendations logged and reviewed
   - Recommendation accuracy rate calculated
   - False positive rate calculated
   - Human override frequency tracked
   - Net time saved documented with actual numbers

2. **Governance Requirements** (must have all of these)
   - IAM policy for agent service principal documented and reviewed
   - AGENTS.md committed to repo and reviewed by security
   - Eval suite passing on all 10 baseline scenarios
   - Observability dashboard live and showing last 30 days

3. **Go / No-Go Decision**
   - If all data requirements met AND accuracy ≥70%: proceed to Days 61–90
   - If data requirements met but accuracy <70%: diagnosis required before proceeding (see playbook)
   - If data requirements not met: extend Days 31–60 by two weeks, identify the gap

---

### `Agentic_DevOps/readiness-assessment/README.md`

One paragraph. This assessment is for teams, not individuals. Run it in a 45-minute team session, not solo.

---

### `Agentic_DevOps/readiness-assessment/assessment.md`

**Purpose:** The team self-assessment scorecard referenced in the webinar. Maps directly to the 4-layer stack.

**Format:** Four scored sections (0–3 per item, total score at end with interpretation).

**Section 1: Foundation Layer (Your Existing Infrastructure)**

Score each 0 (not in place), 1 (partially), 2 (solid), 3 (strong/documented):

- Kubernetes in production with RBAC configured
- CI/CD pipeline with automated testing (not just build)
- Observability stack (metrics, logs, traces) — not just uptime monitoring
- IaC (Terraform or equivalent) with state management
- Incident response runbooks that are current and actually used
- On-call rotation with documented escalation paths

**Section 2: Platform Readiness**

- Kubernetes Gateway API or service mesh in use
- Prometheus/Grafana or equivalent with alerting rules that fire correctly
- Container image scanning in CI pipeline
- RBAC policies documented and reviewed in the last 6 months
- Secrets management (not hardcoded, not in env vars in plain text)

**Section 3: Agent Readiness**

- Team has read and can explain what MCP is
- At least one engineer has built or run a basic agent workflow (locally is fine)
- Eval/testing discipline exists for current automation (you test your pipelines)
- IAM hygiene: principle of least privilege is actually practiced, not just in policy docs
- Your team has had an explicit conversation about where you would NOT deploy an agent

**Section 4: Organizational Readiness**

- Engineering leadership is aware of and supportive of an agent pilot
- Security team has been briefed (even informally) on agent plans
- You have 2-3 engineers who can own an agent pod for 90 days alongside existing work
- You have at least one CI failure type that is high-volume, well-understood, and currently handled manually

**Scoring interpretation:**

```
0–12:   Foundation gaps need addressing first. Focus on Section 1 items.
13–22:  Solid foundation, agent readiness gaps. Start with eval discipline and MCP basics.
23–32:  Ready to start the 90-day playbook. Begin with Days 1–30.
33–40:  Strong foundation. You may be able to compress Days 1–30 to 2–3 weeks.
```

**Close with:** A note that scoring yourself a 3 on "RBAC configured" because it exists but nobody has reviewed it in a year is how agent pilots fail. Be honest. The assessment is only useful if it reflects reality.

---

### `Agentic_DevOps/agent-boundary-design/README.md`

One paragraph. The IAM role an agent assumes is its blast radius. This folder contains the templates to define that boundary before any agent touches a pipeline.

---

### `Agentic_DevOps/agent-boundary-design/permission-matrix-template.md`

**Purpose:** The per-agent permission definition table. Fill this out for every agent before deployment.

**Format:**

```markdown
# Agent Permission Matrix

## Agent: [Name / Purpose]
## Deployment date:
## Owner (person accountable for this agent):
## Review date (max 90 days from deployment):

---

| Resource | Can READ | Can WRITE | Can EXECUTE | Requires Human Approval |
|----------|----------|-----------|-------------|------------------------|
| Source code repo | | | | |
| CI/CD pipeline config | | | | |
| Container registry | | | | |
| Kubernetes cluster (prod) | | | | |
| Kubernetes cluster (staging) | | | | |
| Infrastructure state (Terraform) | | | | |
| Secrets / credential store | | | | |
| Incident management system | | | | |
| Deployment tooling | | | | |
| Log/metrics store | | | | |

---

## Approval Gates

List every action this agent can take that requires human approval before execution:

1.
2.
3.

## Explicitly Out of Scope

List what this agent is explicitly NOT permitted to do, even if technically possible:

1.
2.
3.

## Retry Budget

- Maximum retries on failure: [number]
- Retry delay: [seconds]
- Circuit breaker behavior: [what happens when budget is exhausted]

## Audit Trail

- Where are agent actions logged: [location]
- Log retention: [duration]
- Who reviews logs and how often: [name/role, cadence]
```

**After the template, include a completed example** for a CI build failure analysis agent (read-only, advisory mode). Use realistic values, not placeholders.

---

### `Agentic_DevOps/agent-boundary-design/agents-md-template.md`

**Purpose:** A starter AGENTS.md file for teams to commit to their repo root. AGENTS.md is the per-repo configuration file that tells agents how to behave in that repository context.

**Must include sections for:**

- Repository context (what this repo is, what it does, what it doesn't do)
- Agent behavior rules (what agents are allowed to do in this repo)
- Off-limits (explicit prohibitions)
- CI/CD context (which pipeline, what triggers mean)
- Escalation (what to do when the agent is uncertain)
- Tool access (which MCP servers/tools are configured for this repo)

**Include a filled-out example** for a mid-sized monorepo with a Node.js service and Terraform infrastructure. Make it realistic enough that an engineer can use it as a starting point immediately.

---

### `Agentic_DevOps/eval-framework/README.md`

One paragraph. Eval-driven development is the same discipline as TDD applied to agents. If you wouldn't deploy code without tests, don't deploy agents without evals.

---

### `Agentic_DevOps/eval-framework/eval-suite-starter.md`

**Purpose:** A starter eval framework for the CI build failure analysis use case. Teams replace the example scenarios with their own.

**Structure:**

```
# Agent Eval Suite: CI Build Failure Analysis

## What This Is
## How to Use This
## Eval Scenario Template
## Example Scenarios (10 filled in)
## Scoring Methodology
## Trajectory Checklist
## Production Sampling Protocol
```

**What This Is:** 2-3 sentences. This is your acceptance criteria for the agent before it goes to advisory mode in CI.

input.new_str:
Include:
- MCP (Model Context Protocol): the integration standard. How agents connect to tools. The June 2025 spec update formally defined MCP servers as OAuth 2.1 resource servers with mandatory Resource Indicators (RFC 8707) and PKCE — the security standard exists and is solid. The honest caveat is implementation quality across the 10,000+ published MCP servers varies wildly. The spec being sound doesn't mean every server in the ecosystem follows it. Vet any MCP server you consume; don't assume compliance.
- A2A (Agent2Agent Protocol): Google's open protocol for agent-to-agent communication, launched April 2025 and now under the Linux Foundation. Complements MCP — MCP handles agent-to-tool connections, A2A handles agent-to-agent orchestration. Version 0.3 (July 2025) added gRPC support and signed security cards. Honest status: the broader ecosystem has consolidated around MCP for most use cases; A2A adoption is slower and primarily relevant to multi-agent architectures (Level 3+). You don't need A2A for the 90-day playbook. Note it so senior engineers know you're aware of it.
- KAgent: deploying and managing AI agents on Kubernetes (KodeKloud course)
- LangGraph 1.0: multi-step agent orchestration, durable execution, human-in-the-loop as first-class feature. 47M+ PyPI downloads, GA. Used by Uber, LinkedIn, Klarna.
- Dapr Agents: CNCF graduated, sidecar pattern, state persists through failures, cloud-agnostic

For each: be honest about maturity. Note where things are GA vs. preview vs. experimental.

input.description: Fix AGENTS.md template section - reframe its purpose correctly as coding agent context, not ops agent governance
input.new_str:
### `Agentic_DevOps/agent-boundary-design/agents-md-template.md`

**Purpose:** A starter AGENTS.md file for teams to commit to their repo root. Clarify the category up front: AGENTS.md is an OpenAI-originated open standard (August 2025, now under the Linux Foundation's Agentic AI Foundation) that gives **coding agents** (Codex, Claude Code, Copilot, Cursor, Jules, Devin, etc.) a consistent source of project-specific guidance. It is codebase context documentation, not ops agent governance. Do NOT conflate it with permission matrices or IAM policy.

When is AGENTS.md relevant to this webinar's audience? If your CI build failure analysis agent also needs to understand your repo structure, coding conventions, and architecture to make better recommendations, a well-structured AGENTS.md helps the agent reason about your codebase. That's a legitimate use case. Permissions governance lives in the permission matrix, not here.

**Must include sections for:**

- Repository context (what this repo is, what it does, what it doesn't do — for the agent's understanding)
- Coding conventions and style (so coding agents generate consistent code)
- Build and test commands (exact commands, not prose descriptions)
- Architecture overview (major modules, what lives where)
- Off-limits (files or paths the agent should not modify)
- Escalation signals (patterns that mean the agent should stop and flag for human review, not guess)
- Gotchas (known footguns, legacy patterns, things that look wrong but are intentional)

**What to explicitly exclude from AGENTS.md:**
- IAM policies or permission grants (wrong artifact — use permission-matrix-template.md)
- Runtime ops agent behavior (AGENTS.md is read by coding agents, not production ops agents)
- Sensitive environment details or credentials of any kind

**Include a filled-out example** for a mid-sized monorepo with a Node.js service and Terraform infrastructure. Keep it under 150 lines — long AGENTS.md files degrade agent performance by burying signal in noise. Make it realistic enough that an engineer can use it as a starting point immediately.

- Escalation (what to do when the agent is uncertain)
- Tool access (which MCP servers/tools are configured for this repo)

Commit the policy document to the repo (see `agent-boundary-design/permission-matrix-template.md`). Do NOT use AGENTS.md for ops agent governance — AGENTS.md is a coding agent convention (OpenAI, August 2025) that gives coding agents like Codex or Claude Code repo-specific instructions; it is not the right artifact for runtime ops agent permission documentation. Your IAM policy, RBAC manifests, and permission matrix are the governance artifacts. If your CI build failure analysis agent also needs repo context to understand the codebase it's analyzing, then a well-structured AGENTS.md is appropriate — but that's codebase documentation, not permissions governance.
334:**Purpose:** A starter AGENTS.md file for teams to commit to their repo root. Clarify the category up front: AGENTS.md is an OpenAI-originated open standard (August 2025, now under the Linux Foundation's Agentic AI Foundation) that gives **coding agents** (Codex, Claude Code, Copilot, Cursor, Jules, Devin, etc.) a consistent source of project-specific guidance. It is codebase context documentation, not ops agent governance. Do NOT conflate it with permission matrices or IAM policy.
341:- Coding conventions and style (so coding agents generate consistent code)
350:- Runtime ops agent behavior (AGENTS.md is read by coding agents, not production ops agents)
428:What to verify in OpenTelemetry traces for each eval run. Instrument using OTel GenAI Semantic Conventions v1.37+ (production-usable as of March 2026, natively supported by Datadog, New Relic, Grafana). Key attributes: `gen_ai.operation.name`, `gen_ai.usage.input_tokens`, `gen_ai.usage.output_tokens`, `gen_ai.provider.name`. For agent spans specifically, verify:
443:One paragraph. This is a point-in-time reference (March 2026). The landscape is moving fast — check dates before citing any of this. For tracking how the core standards (MCP, AGENTS.md, A2A) evolve, the authoritative source is the Linux Foundation's Agentic AI Foundation (AAIF), formed December 2025 with founding contributions from Anthropic, OpenAI, and Block, and platinum members including AWS, Google, Microsoft, Bloomberg, and Cloudflare.
466:- MCP (Model Context Protocol): the integration standard. How agents connect to tools. The June 2025 spec update formally defined MCP servers as OAuth 2.1 resource servers with mandatory Resource Indicators (RFC 8707) and PKCE — the security standard exists and is solid. The honest caveat is implementation quality across the 10,000+ published MCP servers varies wildly. The spec being sound doesn't mean every server in the ecosystem follows it. Vet any MCP server you consume; don't assume compliance.
467:- A2A (Agent2Agent Protocol): Google's open protocol for agent-to-agent communication, launched April 2025 and now under the Linux Foundation. Complements MCP — MCP handles agent-to-tool connections, A2A handles agent-to-agent orchestration. Version 0.3 (July 2025) added gRPC support and signed security cards. Honest status: the broader ecosystem has consolidated around MCP for most use cases; A2A adoption is slower and primarily relevant to multi-agent architectures (Level 3+). You don't need A2A for the 90-day playbook. Note it so senior engineers know you're aware of it.
484:Include: Datadog LLM Observability (GA June 2025), New Relic AI Monitoring (expanded Feb 2026), Grafana Assistant (GA Oct 2025), Langfuse (open source), OpenTelemetry GenAI SIG (semantic conventions cover agent spans, metrics, and events as of v1.37+; Datadog, New Relic, and others have natively adopted them — instrument to OTel GenAI SemConv v1.37+ now so your telemetry is vendor-portable. Status is still "Development" not "Stable" but the conventions are production-usable today. This is the recommended default for new agent instrumentation.)
521:- MCP Authorization Specification / modelcontextprotocol.io — the actual spec for OAuth 2.1 + PKCE implementation. Required reading before any team builds or consumes an MCP server.
522:- Linux Foundation Agentic AI Foundation (AAIF) announcement (December 2025) — the governance body for MCP, AGENTS.md, and goose. This is where these standards evolve. Bookmark it so you know when specs change.

content.text: All corrections confirmed. Clean.

Here's a summary of every change made and why:

**3 corrections (things that were wrong):**

1. **MCP security** — removed "no built-in security standards." Replaced with the accurate picture: OAuth 2.1 + PKCE is the formal spec since June 2025, but implementation quality across 10,000+ servers varies. Vet what you consume.

2. **OTel GenAI SIG** — removed "watch this space." Replaced with actionable guidance: instrument to v1.37+ now, it's production-usable today and natively supported by Datadog/New Relic/Grafana. Still "Development" status technically, but that's a spec label, not a deployment blocker.

3. **AGENTS.md** — corrected in three places (Days 1-30, decision gate checklist, template spec). It's a coding agent convention for repo context, not ops agent governance. The permission matrix is the right governance artifact. Also added the 150-line ceiling guidance since long AGENTS.md files degrade agent reasoning.

**2 additions (gaps that mattered):**

4. **A2A Protocol** — added to Layer 3 with honest status: MCP won the broader adoption battle, A2A is still relevant for Level 3 multi-agent architectures, not needed for the 90-day playbook. Senior engineers will know about it.

5. **AAIF (Agentic AI Foundation)** — added to stack reference README and resources.md. It's the governance body for the standards this entire repo is built on. Teams need to know where to track spec changes.

chat_messages.text:
also need to make sure that these corrections are in there.. searching current March 2026 standards against your repo:
Key discrepancies identified:
1. MCP security claim is outdated — The repo says MCP has "no built-in security or authentication standard," but the June 2025 spec added OAuth-based auth (servers as OAuth Resource Servers, RFC 8707 Resource Indicators). MCP was also donated to the Linux Foundation's AAIF in Dec 2025. March 2026 security data is worse than cited: 30 CVEs in 60 days, 38% of servers lack auth.
2. Kubernetes 1.29 is EOL — The AGENTS.md example references K8s 1.29, which is past end-of-life. Current is 1.35.2; a realistic EKS production version would be 1.32+.
3. Gateway API version — Repo says "GA v1.1 (Oct 2024)" but current standard release is v1.4.0.
4. GPT-5.4 launched March 5, 2026 — Repo references GPT-5.3-Codex but doesn't mention GPT-5.4 which incorporates Codex capabilities. Also, Claude Opus 4.1 and GPT-5 were deprecated from GitHub Copilot on Feb 17, 2026.
5. Dapr v1.17 (Feb 27, 2026) adds major agent-relevant features: workflow versioning, LangGraph/Strands extensions, 41% throughput improvement.
6. Observability tools expanded — Datadog AI Agent Monitoring is GA, Grafana added MCP monitoring, New Relic has multi-agent visibility.
What's still accurate: LangGraph 1.0 GA status, Claude Opus 4.6 details, KEDA/Dapr CNCF status, all methodology/process content, all cited research with dates.
The plan covers ~7 targeted edits across 2 files (primarily `tool-landscape.md`). No changes needed to the playbook, eval framework scenarios, or resources file.

440	
   441	### `Agentic_DevOps/stack-reference/README.md`
   442	
   443	One paragraph. This is a point-in-time reference (March 2026). The landscape is moving fast — check dates before citing any of this. For tracking how the core standards (MCP, AGENTS.md, A2A) evolve, the authoritative source is the Linux Foundation's Agentic AI Foundation (AAIF), formed December 2025 with founding contributions from Anthropic, OpenAI, and Block, and platinum members including AWS, Google, Microsoft, Bloomberg, and Cloudflare.
   444	
   445	---
   446	
   447	### `Agentic_DevOps/stack-reference/tool-landscape.md`
   448	
   449	**Purpose:** The 4-layer stack reference for practitioners. Not a buying guide. Not ranked by preference.
   450	
   451	**Layer 1: Foundation (You Probably Already Have This)**
   452	
   453	Write one sentence per tool. No marketing descriptions. What it is, why it matters for agent deployment.
   454	
   455	Include: Kubernetes, Docker, Terraform, GitHub Actions, ArgoCD, Prometheus, Grafana
   456	
   457	**Layer 2: Platform Layer (The Infrastructure Agents Run On)**
   458	
   459	Include: Kubernetes Gateway API, Dapr, KEDA, Service mesh (Istio/Linkerd — note the tradeoffs honestly)
   460	
   461	For each: one sentence on what it is, one sentence on why it's relevant to agentic workloads specifically (not generic value).
   462	
   463	**Layer 3: Agent Infrastructure (Where Most Teams Need to Build Now)**
   464	
   465	Include:
   466	- MCP (Model Context Protocol): the integration standard. How agents connect to tools. The June 2025 spec update formally defined MCP servers as OAuth 2.1 resource servers with mandatory Resource Indicators (RFC 8707) and PKCE — the security standard exists and is solid. The honest caveat is implementation quality across the 10,000+ published MCP servers varies wildly. The spec being sound doesn't mean every server in the ecosystem follows it. Vet any MCP server you consume; don't assume compliance.
   467	- A2A (Agent2Agent Protocol): Google's open protocol for agent-to-agent communication, launched April 2025 and now under the Linux Foundation. Complements MCP — MCP handles agent-to-tool connections, A2A handles agent-to-agent orchestration. Version 0.3 (July 2025) added gRPC support and signed security cards. Honest status: the broader ecosystem has consolidated around MCP for most use cases; A2A adoption is slower and primarily relevant to multi-agent architectures (Level 3+). You don't need A2A for the 90-day playbook. Note it so senior engineers know you're aware of it.
   468	- KAgent: deploying and managing AI agents on Kubernetes (KodeKloud course)
   469	- LangGraph 1.0: multi-step agent orchestration, durable execution, human-in-the-loop as first-class feature. 47M+ PyPI downloads, GA. Used by Uber, LinkedIn, Klarna.
   470	- Dapr Agents: CNCF graduated, sidecar pattern, state persists through failures, cloud-agnostic
   471	
   472	For each: be honest about maturity. Note where things are GA vs. preview vs. experimental.
   473	
   474	**Layer 4: Agent Intelligence (Building and Running the Models)**
   475	
   476	Include: Claude Code (Opus 4.6, 14.5-hour task horizon, MCP native), GitHub Copilot Coding Agent (GA Sept 2025), Google ADK (live), AWS Bedrock, OpenAI Codex (relaunched May 2025 as GPT-5.3-Codex)
   477	
   478	For each: one concrete production capability, one honest limitation.
   479	
   480	**Observability Tools for Agents (Cross-cutting)**
   481	
   482	This deserves its own subsection because it's non-negotiable infrastructure before increasing agent autonomy.
   483	
   484	Include: Datadog LLM Observability (GA June 2025), New Relic AI Monitoring (expanded Feb 2026), Grafana Assistant (GA Oct 2025), Langfuse (open source), OpenTelemetry GenAI SIG (semantic conventions cover agent spans, metrics, and events as of v1.37+; Datadog, New Relic, and others have natively adopted them — instrument to OTel GenAI SemConv v1.37+ now so your telemetry is vendor-portable. Status is still "Development" not "Stable" but the conventions are production-usable today. This is the recommended default for new agent instrumentation.)
   485	
   486	Agent-specific metrics every team needs to instrument (not optional):
   487	- Reasoning loop count
   488	- Tool call success/failure rate
   489	- Token usage and cost per invocation
   490	- Human override frequency
   491	- Loop/stall detection
   492	
   493	**The Four Failure Modes (Reference)**
   494	
   495	Brief entries — detailed treatment in the playbook. Point to `90-day-playbook/playbook.md` for full context.
   496	1. Hallucination propagation
   497	2. Retry loops without circuit breakers
   498	3. State drift (no audit trail)
   499	4. Deploying everywhere on day one
   500	
   501	---
   502	
   503	### `Agentic_DevOps/resources.md`
   504	
   505	**Purpose:** Curated external reading list. No link dumps.
   506	
   507	**Format:** Annotated list. For each resource: title, source, one sentence on why it's worth reading and what question it answers.
   508	
   509	**Must include:**
   510	
   511	- 2025 DORA Report (delivery throughput vs. stability finding)
   512	- Opsera 2026 research (4.6x PR review time, 48% faster PR creation, 15-18% more vulnerabilities in AI-generated PRs)
   513	- Snyk February 2026 research (13.4% of public agent skills had critical vulnerabilities, 76 confirmed malicious payloads)
   514	- Forrester 2026 prediction on agentic AI breach
   515	- IDC 2026 maturity model research
   516	- AWS DevOps Agent production writeup (eval framework source)
   517	- Datadog Bits AI SRE Agent GA announcement
   518	- Block/Goose PagerDuty MCP integration writeup
   519	- Elastic self-healing CI/CD case study
   520	- Bret Fisher / Agentic DevOps podcast (agenticdevops.fm) — note: practitioner perspective, not research
   521	- MCP Authorization Specification / modelcontextprotocol.io — the actual spec for OAuth 2.1 + PKCE implementation. Required reading before any team builds or consumes an MCP server.
   522	- Linux Foundation Agentic AI Foundation (AAIF) announcement (December 2025) — the governance body for MCP, AGENTS.md, and goose. This is where these standards evolve. Bookmark it so you know when specs change.
   523	- Google Agent2Agent (A2A) Protocol — a2aproject.org — relevant context for teams planning multi-agent architectures at Level 3. Not required for the 90-day playbook but senior engineers will ask about it.
   524	
   525	**Do not include:**
   526	- Generic "AI is changing everything" think pieces
   527	- Vendor whitepapers without cited data
   528	- Anything without a date (undated content is useless in this space)
   529	
   530	---
   531	
   532	## Quality Gates
   533	
   534	Before considering any file complete, verify:
   535	
   536	1. **No timing information visible anywhere.** Timing guidance belongs in speaker notes only, not in any collateral asset.
   537	2. **Every statistic has a source.** If you can't source it, cut it or flag it with `<!-- UNSOURCED: verify before publishing -->`.
   538	3. **Every file passes the "Monday test":** Can a reader open this file and know what to do this week? If no, it's not done.
   539	4. **No duplicate content across files.** If two files cover the same point, one should reference the other.
   540	5. **The permission matrix template has a filled example.** Templates without examples don't get used.
   541	6. **The eval suite has 10 complete scenarios.** Not 3 with placeholders for the rest.
   542	7. **Run `markdownlint` or equivalent.** Broken link references and malformed tables are embarrassing in public repos.
   543	
   544	---
   545	
   546	## What This Repo Is NOT Responsible For
   547	
   548	- The slide deck (separate deliverable, already built)
   549	- KodeKloud course links (add placeholders, do not hardcode URLs that may change)
   550	- Certification roadmap details (out of scope, reference KodeKloud learning paths generally)
   551	- Anything requiring live infrastructure to demonstrate
   552	
   553	---
   554	
   555	## Final Check
   556	
   557	When all files are written, generate a `Agentic_DevOps/README.md` last, after you have full visibility into what was actually built. The README should reflect reality, not the spec.

{"language": "markdown", "code": "
### `Agentic_DevOps/stack-reference/README.md`

One paragraph. This is a point-in-time reference (March 2026). The landscape is moving fast — check dates before citing any of this. For tracking how the core standards (MCP, AGENTS.md, A2A) evolve, the authoritative source is the Linux Foundation's Agentic AI Foundation (AAIF), formed December 2025 with founding contributions from Anthropic, OpenAI, and Block, and platinum members including AWS, Google, Microsoft, Bloomberg, and Cloudflare.

---

### `Agentic_DevOps/stack-reference/tool-landscape.md`

**Purpose:** The 4-layer stack reference for practitioners. Not a buying guide. Not ranked by preference.

**Layer 1: Foundation (You Probably Already Have This)**

Write one sentence per tool. No marketing descriptions. What it is, why it matters for agent deployment.

Include: Kubernetes, Docker, Terraform, GitHub Actions, ArgoCD, Prometheus, Grafana

**Layer 2: Platform Layer (The Infrastructure Agents Run On)**

Include: Kubernetes Gateway API, Dapr, KEDA, Service mesh (Istio/Linkerd — note the tradeoffs honestly)

For each: one sentence on what it is, one sentence on why it's relevant to agentic workloads specifically (not generic value).

**Layer 3: Agent Infrastructure (Where Most Teams Need to Build Now)**

Include:
- MCP (Model Context Protocol): the integration standard. How agents connect to tools. The June 2025 spec update formally defined MCP servers as OAuth 2.1 resource servers with mandatory Resource Indicators (RFC 8707) and PKCE — the security standard exists and is solid. The honest caveat is implementation quality across the 10,000+ published MCP servers varies wildly. The spec being sound doesn't mean every server in the ecosystem follows it. Vet any MCP server you consume; don't assume compliance.
- A2A (Agent2Agent Protocol): Google's open protocol for agent-to-agent communication, launched April 2025 and now under the Linux Foundation. Complements MCP — MCP handles agent-to-tool connections, A2A handles agent-to-agent orchestration. Version 0.3 (July 2025) added gRPC support and signed security cards. Honest status: the broader ecosystem has consolidated around MCP for most use cases; A2A adoption is slower and primarily relevant to multi-agent architectures (Level 3+). You don't need A2A for the 90-day playbook. Note it so senior engineers know you're aware of it.
- KAgent: deploying and managing AI agents on Kubernetes (KodeKloud course)
- LangGraph 1.0: multi-step agent orchestration, durable execution, human-in-the-loop as first-class feature. 47M+ PyPI downloads, GA. Used by Uber, LinkedIn, Klarna.
- Dapr Agents: CNCF graduated, sidecar pattern, state persists through failures, cloud-agnostic

For each: be honest about maturity. Note where things are GA vs. preview vs. experimental.

**Layer 4: Agent Intelligence (Building and Running the Models)**

Include: Claude Code (Opus 4.6, 14.5-hour task horizon, MCP native), GitHub Copilot Coding Agent (GA Sept 2025), Google ADK (live), AWS Bedrock, OpenAI Codex (relaunched May 2025 as GPT-5.3-Codex)

For each: one concrete production capability, one honest limitation.

**Observability Tools for Agents (Cross-cutting)**

This deserves its own subsection because it's non-negotiable infrastructure before increasing agent autonomy.

Include: Datadog LLM Observability (GA June 2025), New Relic AI Monitoring (expanded Feb 2026), Grafana Assistant (GA Oct 2025), Langfuse (open source), OpenTelemetry GenAI SIG (semantic conventions cover agent spans, metrics, and events as of v1.37+; Datadog, New Relic, and others have natively adopted them — instrument to OTel GenAI SemConv v1.37+ now so your telemetry is vendor-portable. Status is still "Development" not "Stable" but the conventions are production-usable today. This is the recommended default for new agent instrumentation.)

Agent-specific metrics every team needs to instrument (not optional):
- Reasoning loop count
- Tool call success/failure rate
- Token usage and cost per invocation
- Human override frequency
- Loop/stall detection

**The Four Failure Modes (Reference)**

Brief entries — detailed treatment in the playbook. Point to `90-day-playbook/playbook.md` for full context.
1. Hallucination propagation
2. Retry loops without circuit breakers
3. State drift (no audit trail)
4. Deploying everywhere on day one

---

### `Agentic_DevOps/resources.md`

**Purpose:** Curated external reading list. No link dumps.

**Format:** Annotated list. For each resource: title, source, one sentence on why it's worth reading and what question it answers.

**Must include:**

- 2025 DORA Report (delivery throughput vs. stability finding)
- Opsera 2026 research (4.6x PR review time, 48% faster PR creation, 15-18% more vulnerabilities in AI-generated PRs)
- Snyk February 2026 research (13.4% of public agent skills had critical vulnerabilities, 76 confirmed malicious payloads)
- Forrester 2026 prediction on agentic AI breach
- IDC 2026 maturity model research
- AWS DevOps Agent production writeup (eval framework source)
- Datadog Bits AI SRE Agent GA announcement
- Block/Goose PagerDuty MCP integration writeup
- Elastic self-healing CI/CD case study
- Bret Fisher / Agentic DevOps podcast (agenticdevops.fm) — note: practitioner perspective, not research
- MCP Authorization Specification / modelcontextprotocol.io — the actual spec for OAuth 2.1 + PKCE implementation. Required reading before any team builds or consumes an MCP server.
- Linux Foundation Agentic AI Foundation (AAIF) announcement (December 2025) — the governance body for MCP, AGENTS.md, and goose. This is where these standards evolve. Bookmark it so you know when specs change.
- Google Agent2Agent (A2A) Protocol — a2aproject.org — relevant context for teams planning multi-agent architectures at Level 3. Not required for the 90-day playbook but senior engineers will ask about it.

**Do not include:**
- Generic "AI is changing everything" think pieces
- Vendor whitepapers without cited data
- Anything without a date (undated content is useless in this space)

---

## Quality Gates

Before considering any file complete, verify:

1. **No timing information visible anywhere.** Timing guidance belongs in speaker notes only, not in any collateral asset.
2. **Every statistic has a source.** If you can't source it, cut it or flag it with `<!-- UNSOURCED: verify before publishing -->`.
3. **Every file passes the "Monday test":** Can a reader open this file and know what to do this week? If no, it's not done.
4. **No duplicate content across files.** If two files cover the same point, one should reference the other.
5. **The permission matrix template has a filled example.** Templates without examples don't get used.
6. **The eval suite has 10 complete scenarios.** Not 3 with placeholders for the rest.
7. **Run `markdownlint` or equivalent.** Broken link references and malformed tables are embarrassing in public repos.

---

## What This Repo Is NOT Responsible For

- The slide deck (separate deliverable, already built)
- KodeKloud course links (add placeholders, do not hardcode URLs that may change)
- Certification roadmap details (out of scope, reference KodeKloud learning paths generally)
- Anything requiring live infrastructure to demonstrate

---

## Final Check

When all files are written, generate a `Agentic_DevOps/README.md` last, after you have full visibility into what was actually built. The README should reflect reality, not the spec.

input.description: Fix MCP entry - add real March 2026 CVE/auth stats, they're worse than previously cited
input.old_str: - MCP (Model Context Protocol): the integration standard. How agents connect to tools. The June 2025 spec update formally defined MCP servers as OAuth 2.1 resource servers with mandatory Resource Indicators (RFC 8707) and PKCE — the security standard exists and is solid. The honest caveat is implementation quality across the 10,000+ published MCP servers varies wildly. The spec being sound doesn't mean every server in the ecosystem follows it. Vet any MCP server you consume; don't assume compliance.
input.new_str: - MCP (Model Context Protocol): the integration standard. How agents connect to tools. The June 2025 spec update formally defined MCP servers as OAuth 2.1 resource servers with mandatory Resource Indicators (RFC 8707) and PKCE — the security standard is solid and well-specified. MCP was donated to the Linux Foundation's AAIF in December 2025. The honest caveat is implementation quality is a genuine crisis: as of March 2026, 30 CVEs were disclosed across MCP server implementations in a 60-day window, and 38% of published MCP servers lack authentication entirely. The spec being sound doesn't mean the ecosystem follows it. **Treat every third-party MCP server as untrusted by default.** Vet auth implementation before connecting any MCP server to a production agent. Build or consume only servers you can audit.
