input.description: Add version accuracy check to Quality Gates
input.new_str:
## Quality Gates

Before considering any file complete, verify:

1. **No timing information visible anywhere.** Timing guidance belongs in speaker notes only, not in any collateral asset.
2. **Every statistic has a source.** If you can't source it, cut it or flag it with `<!-- UNSOURCED: verify before publishing -->`.
3. **Every file passes the "Monday test":** Can a reader open this file and know what to do this week? If no, it's not done.
4. **No duplicate content across files.** If two files cover the same point, one should reference the other.
5. **The permission matrix template has a filled example.** Templates without examples don't get used.
6. **The eval suite has 10 complete scenarios.** Not 3 with placeholders for the rest.
7. **Run `markdownlint` or equivalent.** Broken link references and malformed tables are embarrassing in public repos.
8. **Version audit before publish.** Grep every generated file for `1.29`, `v1.1`, `GPT-5.3`, `5.3-Codex`. Any hit is a bug. Correct versions: Kubernetes 1.32+ (EKS production floor), Gateway API v1.4.0, OpenAI Codex = GPT-5.4 (March 2026). Flag any version reference you cannot verify with `<!-- VERSION-CHECK: confirm before publishing -->`.
input.old_str:
## Quality Gates

Before considering any file complete, verify:

1. **No timing information visible anywhere.** Timing guidance belongs in speaker notes only, not in any collateral asset.
2. **Every statistic has a source.** If you can't source it, cut it or flag it with `<!-- UNSOURCED: verify before publishing -->`.
3. **Every file passes the "Monday test":** Can a reader open this file and know what to do this week? If no, it's not done.
4. **No duplicate content across files.** If two files cover the same point, one should reference the other.
5. **The permission matrix template has a filled example.** Templates without examples don't get used.
6. **The eval suite has 10 complete scenarios.** Not 3 with placeholders for the rest.
7. **Run `markdownlint` or equivalent.** Broken link references and malformed tables are embarrassing in public repos.

content.text: The "FOUND STALE" flag is a false positive — both `1.29` hits are in the version guard instructions explicitly saying *"1.29 is EOL, do not use it."* That's the correct behavior. No actual stale values remain.

All 7 corrections are in. Here's exactly what changed and where:

**`tool-landscape.md` spec (6 edits):**

1. **MCP security** — upgraded from "implementation varies" to the actual March 2026 numbers: 30 CVEs in 60 days, 38% of servers unauthenticated. The framing shifted from "caveat emptor" to "treat every third-party MCP server as untrusted by default." That's the right posture for a practitioner repo.

2. **Kubernetes version floor** — added explicit instruction: current stable is 1.35.2, EKS production floor is 1.32+, 1.29 is EOL. Applied in both Layer 2 and the AGENTS.md template section.

3. **Gateway API** — corrected from unversioned to `v1.4.0` with an explicit note that v1.1 is superseded.

4. **OpenAI Codex** — updated from GPT-5.3-Codex to GPT-5.4 (launched March 5, 2026). Added Copilot deprecation of Claude Opus 4.1 and GPT-5 on Feb 17, 2026 — that's a real gotcha for anyone currently relying on those in Copilot workflows.

5. **Dapr** — updated to v1.17 (Feb 27, 2026) with the specific agent-relevant additions: workflow versioning, LangGraph/Strands extensions, 41% throughput gain.

6. **Observability** — disambiguated Datadog LLM Observability from Datadog AI Agent Monitoring (they're distinct products). Added Grafana MCP monitoring and New Relic multi-agent visibility specifics.

**Quality Gates (1 edit):**

7. **Gate #8 added** — explicit version audit instruction with grep targets (`1.29`, `v1.1`, `GPT-5.3`, `5.3-Codex`) so Claude Code self-checks before considering any file done.

chat_messages.text: why is Dapr in there?  What does that have to do with Agentic DevOps?

content.text: why is Dapr in there?  What does that have to do with Agentic DevOps?

chat_messages.text:
The user is challenging why Dapr is in the spec at all. Let me think honestly about this.

Dapr is a distributed application runtime - it provides building blocks like state management, pub/sub, service invocation, etc. It's not specifically an agent framework. The spec included "Dapr Agents" but let me think about whether that's actually relevant to the agentic DevOps use case being covered here.

The webinar is about agentic DevOps for mid-to-senior DevOps/Platform engineers. The 90-day playbook is about CI build failure analysis agents and incident triage - practical, ops-focused agent use cases.

Dapr Agents is a real thing - it's built on top of Dapr's workflow and actor model to support agent patterns. But is it something a DevOps team building their first CI failure analysis agent would actually use? Probably not. They'd more likely use LangGraph, or just Claude Code/Codex directly.
 
Dapr feels like it was included because of CNCF alignment rather than actual relevance to what DevOps engineers would reach for when building CI/CD agents. While it has legitimate agent capabilities, it's not the natural choice for this use case - it belongs in a broader application development context, not a focused 90-day playbook for ops teams.Honest answer: it doesn't, not for this audience and use case.

Dapr is a distributed application runtime — state management, pub/sub, service invocation building blocks for microservices. Dapr Agents is real, built on top of Dapr's workflow and actor model. But the connection to what your audience is actually doing is thin.

A DevOps engineer building a CI build failure analysis agent or an incident triage agent is not reaching for Dapr. They're using LangGraph for orchestration, Claude Code or Codex as the agent runtime, and their existing Kubernetes + Prometheus stack as the platform. Dapr solves distributed systems coordination problems that your 90-day playbook use cases don't have yet.

It got in there because the Phuong doc mentioned it, and the framing "CNCF graduated + has an agent feature" made it sound relevant. That's not the same as being relevant. The v1.17 update with LangGraph extensions is genuinely interesting — for application developers building complex multi-agent systems at Level 3 maturity. That's not your audience right now.

Drop it from Layer 3. If anything, it belongs in a one-line footnote in the Level 3 preview section of the playbook: "if you're building custom multi-agent state machines, look at Dapr v1.17+ and LangGraph." That's the right scope for it.

Want me to pull it out of the spec now?

content.text:
Honest answer: it doesn't, not for this audience and use case.

Dapr is a distributed application runtime — state management, pub/sub, service invocation building blocks for microservices. Dapr Agents is real, built on top of Dapr's workflow and actor model. But the connection to what your audience is actually doing is thin.

A DevOps engineer building a CI build failure analysis agent or an incident triage agent is not reaching for Dapr. They're using LangGraph for orchestration, Claude Code or Codex as the agent runtime, and their existing Kubernetes + Prometheus stack as the platform. Dapr solves distributed systems coordination problems that your 90-day playbook use cases don't have yet.

It got in there because the Phuong doc mentioned it, and the framing "CNCF graduated + has an agent feature" made it sound relevant. That's not the same as being relevant. The v1.17 update with LangGraph extensions is genuinely interesting — for application developers building complex multi-agent systems at Level 3 maturity. That's not your audience right now.

Drop it from Layer 3. If anything, it belongs in a one-line footnote in the Level 3 preview section of the playbook: "if you're building custom multi-agent state machines, look at Dapr v1.17+ and LangGraph." That's the right scope for it.

Want me to pull it out of the spec now?

input.title: Agentic DevOps: State of the Field in Early 2026
input.content:
# Agentic DevOps: what the field actually means in early 2026

**Agentic DevOps is the application of autonomous AI agents — systems that perceive, reason, decide, and act without waiting for human prompts — to the software delivery and operations lifecycle.** The term was popularized at Microsoft Build 2025 by GitHub CEO Thomas Dohmke and has since been adopted industry-wide, though practitioner consensus on scope and boundaries is still crystallizing. What separates it from the AI-assisted DevOps era (Copilot-style tooling) is a single pivot: agents take multi-step actions across systems on their own, with humans overseeing rather than initiating every step. The DORA 2025 report — renamed "State of AI-Assisted Software Development" — found **90% of respondents now use AI at work**, but only **17% use autonomous agent mode daily**, underscoring that while AI in DevOps is ubiquitous, truly agentic adoption remains early. Opsera's data reveals the core problem agentic DevOps aims to solve: AI-generated pull requests wait **4.6× longer** to be reviewed than human-written ones, meaning code creation has outpaced the downstream pipeline. Agentic DevOps targets that gap — automating the orchestration, testing, security, and deployment stages so the entire lifecycle keeps pace.

## The three-tier distinction practitioners actually use

The clearest practitioner taxonomy separates AI involvement in DevOps into three tiers, and confusing these tiers is the root cause of most "agentic washing" in the market.

**Tier 1 — AI-Assisted DevOps** is the Copilot model: code completions, inline suggestions, chat-based Q&A. The human initiates every action, the AI suggests, the human accepts or rejects. GitHub Copilot's original autocomplete, Amazon CodeWhisperer, and Tabnine all live here. Microsoft itself draws this line explicitly: "Unlike the autocomplete and suggestions you might be familiar with in GitHub Copilot, these are autonomous agents."

**Tier 2 — AI-Augmented DevOps** moves from suggestion to execution under supervision. GitHub Copilot's Agent Mode, which creates branches, writes code, runs tests, and proposes PRs — but requires human review before merge — sits at this tier. The AI handles multi-step workflows, but humans gate every consequential action.

**Tier 3 — Agentic DevOps** introduces autonomous decision-making. Agents observe telemetry, correlate signals across systems, form hypotheses, attempt remediation, and escalate only when they cannot resolve an issue. The human moves from "in the loop" (approving every action) to **"on the loop"** (overseeing and intervening only when needed). The Opsera definition captures the core shift: "The important shift isn't 'more automation.' It's fewer moments where a human has to step in just to keep work moving." Bret Fisher, hosting the dedicated *Agentic DevOps Podcast* (agenticdevops.fm), frames it more cautiously: "How can we humans use non-deterministic, often hallucinating LLMs to automate our infrastructure and help us with the job of software lifecycle management — in a secure, tested, and auditable way?"

The key architectural differences are **adaptive workflows** (agents chain decisions dynamically rather than following predefined pipelines), **proactive action** (agents detect and act without being triggered), **cross-system orchestration** (a security scan triggers compliance validation triggers deployment triggers metrics capture), and **continuous learning from outcomes**. Traditional CI/CD — even sophisticated declarative pipelines — remains deterministic and predefined. That is automation, not agency.

## Where agentic AI is genuinely deployed in production

Incident response and SRE is the most mature category by a significant margin, with multiple tools in GA serving hundreds of production customers. CI/CD pipeline management and security are transitioning, while infrastructure automation remains mostly AI-assisted rather than truly agentic.

**Incident response / SRE** — the frontrunner. **Datadog Bits AI SRE** went GA in December 2025, trained on **2,000+ customer environments**, with customers reporting **70–95% MTTR reduction**. It mimics human SRE reasoning: forms hypotheses, tests them against live telemetry, follows evidence chains. Uber Freight is a named production user. **Resolve.ai**, founded by OpenTelemetry co-creators, hit a **$1B valuation** in February 2026 with production deployments at DataStax, Zscaler, and Tubi (one client saw 72% faster critical incident investigation). **PagerDuty's AI Agent Suite** went GA in October 2025 with three agents — SRE, Scribe, and Workflow — with early adopters reporting **50% faster incident resolution** and **250+ customers** adopting its MCP server before full launch. **incident.io** serves 600+ companies including Netflix and Etsy, spawning parallel investigation agents that deliver root-cause hypotheses within 1–2 minutes. **Cleric** earned Gartner Cool Vendor 2025 status for autonomous 24/7 alert investigation. Most of these tools are autonomous for *investigation and correlation* but still require human approval for *remediation actions*, with PagerDuty and Datadog pushing toward pre-approved autonomous remediation for well-scoped scenarios.

**CI/CD pipeline management** is the second most active area. **Harness AI** (GA August 2025) is the standout: 100 of its 1,000 enterprise customers use its DevOps agent for production pipeline building, with a recursive agentic execution flow and multi-agent architecture. **Opsera** runs **50+ specialized DevOps agents** across platform, security, data, and developer workflows, coordinating decisions and enforcing policy inline. **Datadog's Bits AI Dev Agent** (preview) autonomously monitors telemetry, identifies issues in production, and opens production-ready PRs matching team style guides — iterating until tests pass without developer prompting.

**Security (DevSecOps)** is transitioning to agentic. **Snyk Agent Fix** (GA) autonomously generates and validates security fixes in IDEs and PRs. **Snyk Evo** (preview, broader availability early 2026) is a multi-agent orchestration system with specialized agents for discovery, threat modeling, red teaming, and remediation. Datadog's Bits AI Security Analyst (preview) autonomously triages Cloud SIEM signals.

**Infrastructure management** has the widest gap between marketing and reality. **Kubiya** (production, Gartner Cool Vendor, available on AWS Marketplace) is the closest to genuinely agentic: natural language triggers end-to-end infrastructure tasks spanning Terraform, Kubernetes, and CI/CD with RBAC and OPA policy enforcement. **Azure SRE Agent** is promising but still in preview. Notably, **Spacelift's CEO explicitly stated they are pursuing a "conservative approach" to agents** due to infrastructure risk, and **env0** focuses on governance rather than autonomy. Both are AI-assisted, not agentic — an important distinction the market often blurs.

## Core stack versus adjacent tools — and the Dapr question

The Agentic DevOps stack has a clear core and a fuzzy periphery. Understanding the difference matters because vendors are rapidly relabeling existing products.

**Core Agentic DevOps tools** are purpose-built for autonomous DevOps decision-making: Resolve.ai, Datadog Bits AI (SRE/Dev/Security), PagerDuty AI Agent Suite, Harness AI, Opsera, Kubiya, incident.io, Cleric, and Snyk Agent Fix/Evo. These tools were designed from the ground up (or significantly rebuilt) around agent architectures with autonomous reasoning capabilities.

**Adjacent infrastructure** enables agentic DevOps but is not itself an Agentic DevOps tool. **Dapr Agents is firmly in this category.** Dapr is fundamentally a CNCF distributed application runtime for microservices (state management, pub/sub, service invocation). Dapr Agents, announced March 2025, extends Dapr with an AI agent framework — but it is a *general-purpose framework for building agents*, not an agent that performs DevOps tasks. Dapr co-creator Yaron Schneider said candidly: "Agentic systems is just another tech industry term for 'distributed applications with smarts.'" Co-creator Mark Fussell added: "Rather than calling them microservices, you can call them agents now, mostly because you can put large language models amongst them all." Dapr Agents is excellent infrastructure for *running* agents (virtual actor model, scale-to-zero, 50+ data connectors, Kubernetes-native), but its use cases span customer support, data processing, and many domains beyond DevOps. Think of it as Kubernetes is to cloud-native apps: essential underlying infrastructure, but not a DevOps tool itself.

Similarly adjacent: **Kagent** (Solo.io, CNCF Sandbox) is a Kubernetes-native agent framework closer to DevOps but still a building block, not a product. **Azure AI Foundry** is a general-purpose agent platform. **Dynatrace Davis AI** predates the agentic paradigm — it is established causal AIOps, automated but not agentic in the current sense. **Wiz** focuses on *securing* agentic AI systems rather than *being* an agentic DevOps tool.

## How MCP actually works in DevOps pipelines today

MCP (Model Context Protocol) has evolved from "Anthropic's protocol" to an industry standard under the **Linux Foundation's Agentic AI Foundation**, co-governed by Block, OpenAI, Google, Microsoft, and AWS. With **97 million+ monthly SDK downloads** and **5,000+ servers on GitHub**, it is the dominant agent-to-tool protocol. But its role in DevOps specifically splits into two very different patterns.

**Pattern 1: Developer-in-the-loop (80%+ of current DevOps MCP usage).** Developers in Claude Code, Cursor, or Copilot use MCP to reach DevOps tools — querying Kubernetes clusters via kubectl-mcp-server, fetching Terraform docs via HashiCorp's official MCP server, pulling Datadog metrics, or checking PagerDuty on-call schedules. The human developer remains in the loop; MCP serves as a context bridge. This is powerful but not agentic — it is AI-assisted DevOps via MCP.

**Pattern 2: Autonomous agent operations (emerging rapidly).** Azure SRE Agent uses PagerDuty MCP + Azure monitoring to detect, correlate, triage, and resolve incidents approaching autonomy. CloudBees demonstrated agents diagnosing and fixing CI/CD issues end-to-end through MCP. Block's Goose uses MCP to connect PagerDuty for autonomous incident triage. The DevOps MCP server ecosystem is now substantial: official servers exist from **HashiCorp (Terraform), AWS (60+ servers), Pulumi, GitHub, GitLab, Azure DevOps, Argo CD, Jenkins, JFrog, Datadog, Grafana, PagerDuty, Snyk, Portainer, and CloudBees**.

**The honest assessment:** MCP is becoming foundational infrastructure for Agentic DevOps, but most current usage is still developer-facing, not headless-pipeline-native. The transport mechanisms work for autonomous patterns, but true daemon-style pipeline integration — where MCP-connected agents run inside CI/CD without human interaction — is early-stage. A vocal practitioner contingent argues that LLMs already understand CLI tools (kubectl, terraform, aws) from training data, making MCP overhead not always justified. The practical recommendation from experienced engineers: "Use CLI for infrastructure/servers/code. Use MCP for structured, autonomous production systems where schema-driven reliability matters."

The emerging "two-protocol stack" pairs **MCP for tool access** (vertical: agent-to-tool) with **A2A (Google's Agent-to-Agent protocol) for agent orchestration** (horizontal: agent-to-agent). Both are now under Linux Foundation governance, and this combination is the likely infrastructure layer for multi-agent DevOps systems.

## What is not Agentic DevOps

Six categories of things consistently get incorrectly bundled under the label:

- **Code completion and autocomplete** — Microsoft itself explicitly draws this line. Inline suggestions in an IDE are Tier 1 AI-assisted, not agentic. This includes basic GitHub Copilot, Tabnine, and CodeWhisperer in their standard suggestion modes.
- **Deterministic CI/CD pipeline automation** — Even sophisticated declarative pipelines with conditional logic are not agentic because every step is predefined. Jenkins pipelines, GitHub Actions workflows, and ArgoCD sync policies are automation, not agency. The distinction: agentic systems *reason about context and adapt*; pipelines *execute predefined steps*.
- **Dashboards and analytics without action** — AI-powered dashboards that surface insights, detect anomalies, or generate reports but don't autonomously act on findings are observability tools with ML features. An agent doesn't just report problems; it investigates and resolves them.
- **Chatbots and conversational interfaces** — A Slack bot that answers "what's the status of prod?" is reactive Q&A. An agentic system detects the problem at 3 AM, diagnoses root cause, attempts remediation, and files a report without being asked.
- **"AI-washed" existing automation** — Bret Fisher's podcast explicitly warns against "hand-wavy AI hype." Wrapping an LLM around existing scripts or calling your automation "AI-powered" doesn't make it agentic. The differentiator is autonomous reasoning and decision-making, not LLM integration per se.
- **IaC tools with AI-assisted features** — Spacelift (Saturnhead AI explains failures in plain language) and env0 (Cloud Analyst identifies IaC gaps) are explicitly AI-assisted, not agentic. Their own leadership acknowledges this — Spacelift's CEO stated they are deliberately conservative on agent autonomy due to infrastructure risk.

A useful heuristic from Opsera: if a human still has to "step in just to keep work moving" at every decision point, it's not agentic. The transition happens when the human shifts from approving actions to monitoring outcomes.

## The practitioners and communities shaping this space

The **Bret Fisher Agentic DevOps Podcast** (agenticdevops.fm) is the closest thing to a practitioner home base, co-hosted with Nirmal Mehta, featuring guests like Solomon Hykes (Dagger/Docker co-founder) on agentic CI/CD and AWS engineers on the EKS MCP Server. Fisher's framing is deliberately grounded: he centers the tension between non-deterministic LLMs and deterministic infrastructure requirements.

The **DORA 2025 report** doesn't use the term "Agentic DevOps" but provides the empirical foundation: AI amplifies what's already there — strong teams get stronger, struggling teams see problems magnified. Its new **DORA AI Capabilities Model** (7 systemic factors) and finding that platform engineering quality determines AI benefit are essential context. Gene Kim calls the current period "the DORA anomaly" and argues DevOps practices must fundamentally change for the AI era.

**KubeCon EU 2026** (March 23–26, RAI Amsterdam) features the first **Agentics Day** — a dedicated half-day co-located event with 10+ talks on MCP and AI agents in production Kubernetes, organized by CNCF. This signals the cloud-native community formally embracing the agentic paradigm.

The **Linux Foundation Agentic AI Foundation** (AAIF), formed December 2025, houses MCP, Block's Goose, and OpenAI's AGENTS.md — it is the neutral governance home for agentic AI standards. **Rootly AI Labs** runs a fellow-led research community specifically for AI + SRE, and created the SRE-skills-bench benchmark.

Key individual voices include **Angie Jones** (VP Engineering, Block — deployed AI agents to 12,000 employees), **Kumar Chivukula** (CEO, Opsera — category evangelist), **Patrick Debois** (DevOps co-creator, now AI Product Engineer at Tessl), and **Mandi Walls** (author of "The agentic revolution: A new vision for SREs" in The New Stack, January 2026).

## Production case studies with named evidence

**Block/Square** has the strongest independently verified case study. Block built **Goose**, an open-source AI agent framework using MCP, deployed to its **entire 12,000-person workforce in 8 weeks** (presented by Angie Jones at All Things Open 2025). Approximately 5,000 employees use Goose weekly. Senior engineer Will Pfleger confirmed that Block built a PagerDuty MCP extension during a hack week that became a **production tool automating incident triage and root cause analysis**. Goose was contributed to the Linux Foundation AAIF in December 2025.

**Datadog** launched Bits AI SRE to GA in December 2025, tested against 2,000+ customer environments with tens of thousands of investigations completed. **Uber Freight** is a named production user. The tool claims up to 95% decrease in time-to-resolution, with customer testimonials citing 70% MTTR reduction. Datadog also ships an AI Agents Console for meta-observability — monitoring the behavior of all AI agents across an enterprise.

**Opsera** serves named enterprises including **Cohesity, Cisco, Honeywell, Guardant Health, Qualys, Sephora, and Siemens** with 50+ specialized DevOps agents. Their 2026 benchmark report — based on actual enterprise data — documents that AI coding assistants hit 90% enterprise adoption, but AI-generated PRs show **13.5% code duplication** (vs. 10.5% human) and **15–18% more security vulnerabilities**.

**PagerDuty** has 250+ customers on its MCP server with early adopters of its AI Agent Suite reporting 50% faster incident resolution. Block is a confirmed user that built production tooling on PagerDuty's MCP integration.

**Resolve.ai** (founded by OpenTelemetry co-creators, **$1B valuation** February 2026) has production deployments at DataStax, Zscaler, Tubi, and Blueground. **Incident.io** serves 600+ companies including Netflix and Etsy and tripled its customer base in 12 months.

A sobering counterpoint: the **State of Incident Management 2025 report** (Runframe) found that despite AI investment, operational toil actually *rose* to 30% from 25% — the first increase in five years. ServiceNow's Enterprise AI Maturity Index found fewer than **1% of organizations** scored above 50/100 on AI maturity, and the high score fell 12 points year-over-year. The gap between vendor claims and operational reality remains wide.

## Conclusion

Agentic DevOps in March 2026 is a real and accelerating paradigm shift, but one where production maturity varies enormously by lifecycle stage. **Incident response is genuinely agentic** in production at scale, with multiple GA tools autonomously investigating and correlating incidents. **CI/CD and security are transitioning**, with Harness, Opsera, and Snyk shipping real autonomous capabilities. **Infrastructure management remains mostly AI-assisted**, with even leading vendors acknowledging the risk of autonomous action against production infrastructure.

The most important insight from this research: the field's real boundary is not about specific tools or protocols — it's about the **locus of decision-making**. When a human must initiate or approve every action, that's AI-assisted DevOps. When the system observes, reasons, acts, and the human monitors outcomes, that's agentic. MCP is becoming the connective tissue enabling this shift, but most MCP usage today is still developer-facing. The autonomous pipeline pattern — agents running headless inside CI/CD — is where the field is headed but has not yet arrived for most teams. Block's Goose deployment and Datadog's Bits AI SRE represent the current frontier of what's genuinely in production, while KubeCon EU 2026's dedicated Agentics Day marks the moment the cloud-native community formally recognized this as more than a vendor marketing term.

The key distinction the research clarifies is the "on the loop" vs "in the loop" distinction. The deck already has this concept but it needs to be strengthened.

2. **Production case studies need updating:**


I'm reviewing the production evidence across the landscape - Block and Goose have strong confirmation with 12,000 employees and 5,000 weekly users running PagerDuty MCP in production, while Datadog's Bits AI SRE is hitting GA in December 2025 with 2,000+ environments and impressive MTTR improvements. Resolve.ai's $1B valuation and confirmed deployments at DataStax, Zscaler, and Tubi, along with Opsera's 50+ agents and PagerDuty AI, give me a clearer picture of which examples have real production traction versus those that need stronger validation.

Now I'm noticing a gap in the deck - it doesn't clearly articulate what gets mislabeled as agentic DevOps, whereas the research has solid content on deterministic CI/CD, dashboards without action, and AI-washed automation that should be excluded from this category. I'm also seeing that incident response is actually the most mature use case with multiple GA tools in production, even though the deck leads with CI build failure analysis as the entry point - that's a useful distinction to capture since incident response shows more production maturity while CI is still a good starting point for teams.

Looking at the supporting data, I need to verify whether Dapr made it into the slides since it was supposed to be dropped. The DORA 2025 stats are compelling - 90% of organizations use AI at work but only 17% use autonomous agent mode daily - that's a strong stat for positioning. KubeCon EU 2026 is launching an Agentics Day in March, which is a great signal that this is becoming real. The sobering counterpoint is that fewer than 1% of organizations actually score high on AI maturity.

For the definition slide, I could strengthen it with "on the loop" versus "in the loop" language. The Three Waves slide might work better as "Three Tiers" to align with how practitioners actually categorize this - the Microsoft taxonomy of AI-Assisted, AI-Augmented, and Agentic is clearer than the current framing. I'm also looking at a practitioner quote about how humans can work with non-deterministic, hallucinating systems.

MCP deserves an update with the latest stats - 97M+ monthly downloads, 5,000+ servers, and the Linux Foundation governance shift. The Opsera research on code quality is already in the deck with the 4.6x vulnerability finding, but I could add the code duplication metric too. Looking at what actually needs editing versus what's solid, Slide 4 should definitely get the 17% daily agentic usage data added.

For the bigger picture changes, Slide 7's taxonomy needs refinement to include "AI-Augmented" as a middle tier with clearer boundaries, and Slide 9 should incorporate the "on the loop" language into the definition. The production examples section is missing real confirmed case studies like Resolve.ai, Block/Goose, and incident.io that would strengthen credibility. I'm also considering whether to add a dedicated "What Agentic DevOps is NOT" slide since that's important differentiator content, and there's a strong case for highlighting incident response as the most mature category since the research shows it's further along than CI/CD automation.

content.text:
1	---
     2	name: pptx
     3	description: "Use this skill any time a .pptx file is involved in any way — as input, output, or both. This includes: creating slide decks, pitch decks, or presentations; reading, parsing, or extracting text from any .pptx file (even if the extracted content will be used elsewhere, like in an email or summary); editing, modifying, or updating existing presentations; combining or splitting slide files; working with templates, layouts, speaker notes, or comments. Trigger whenever the user mentions \"deck,\" \"slides,\" \"presentation,\" or references a .pptx filename, regardless of what they plan to do with the content afterward. If a .pptx file needs to be opened, created, or touched, use this skill."
     4	license: Proprietary. LICENSE.txt has complete terms
     5	---
     6	
     7	# PPTX Skill
     8	
     9	## Quick Reference
    10	
    11	| Task | Guide |
    12	|------|-------|
    13	| Read/analyze content | `python -m markitdown presentation.pptx` |
    14	| Edit or create from template | Read [editing.md](editing.md) |
    15	| Create from scratch | Read [pptxgenjs.md](pptxgenjs.md) |
    16	
    17	---
    18	
    19	## Reading Content
    20	
    21	```bash
    22	# Text extraction
    23	python -m markitdown presentation.pptx
    24	
    25	# Visual overview
    26	python scripts/thumbnail.py presentation.pptx
    27	
    28	# Raw XML
    29	python scripts/office/unpack.py presentation.pptx unpacked/
    30	```
    31	
    32	---
    33	
    34	## Editing Workflow
    35	
    36	**Read [editing.md](editing.md) for full details.**
    37	
    38	1. Analyze template with `thumbnail.py`
    39	2. Unpack → manipulate slides → edit content → clean → pack
    40	
    41	---
    42	
    43	## Creating from Scratch
    44	
    45	**Read [pptxgenjs.md](pptxgenjs.md) for full details.**
    46	
    47	Use when no template or reference presentation is available.
    48	
    49	---
    50	
    51	## Design Ideas
    52	
    53	**Don't create boring slides.** Plain bullets on a white background won't impress anyone. Consider ideas from this list for each slide.
    54	
    55	### Before Starting
    56	
    57	- **Pick a bold, content-informed color palette**: The palette should feel designed for THIS topic. If swapping your colors into a completely different presentation would still "work," you haven't made specific enough choices.
    58	- **Dominance over equality**: One color should dominate (60-70% visual weight), with 1-2 supporting tones and one sharp accent. Never give all colors equal weight.
    59	- **Dark/light contrast**: Dark backgrounds for title + conclusion slides, light for content ("sandwich" structure). Or commit to dark throughout for a premium feel.
    60	- **Commit to a visual motif**: Pick ONE distinctive element and repeat it — rounded image frames, icons in colored circles, thick single-side borders. Carry it across every slide.
    61	
    62	### Color Palettes
    63	
    64	Choose colors that match your topic — don't default to generic blue. Use these palettes as inspiration:
    65	
    66	| Theme | Primary | Secondary | Accent |
    67	|-------|---------|-----------|--------|
    68	| **Midnight Executive** | `1E2761` (navy) | `CADCFC` (ice blue) | `FFFFFF` (white) |
    69	| **Forest & Moss** | `2C5F2D` (forest) | `97BC62` (moss) | `F5F5F5` (cream) |
    70	| **Coral Energy** | `F96167` (coral) | `F9E795` (gold) | `2F3C7E` (navy) |
    71	| **Warm Terracotta** | `B85042` (terracotta) | `E7E8D1` (sand) | `A7BEAE` (sage) |
    72	| **Ocean Gradient** | `065A82` (deep blue) | `1C7293` (teal) | `21295C` (midnight) |
    73	| **Charcoal Minimal** | `36454F` (charcoal) | `F2F2F2` (off-white) | `212121` (black) |
    74	| **Teal Trust** | `028090` (teal) | `00A896` (seafoam) | `02C39A` (mint) |
    75	| **Berry & Cream** | `6D2E46` (berry) | `A26769` (dusty rose) | `ECE2D0` (cream) |
    76	| **Sage Calm** | `84B59F` (sage) | `69A297` (eucalyptus) | `50808E` (slate) |
    77	| **Cherry Bold** | `990011` (cherry) | `FCF6F5` (off-white) | `2F3C7E` (navy) |
    78	
    79	### For Each Slide
    80	
    81	**Every slide needs a visual element** — image, chart, icon, or shape. Text-only slides are forgettable.
    82	
    83	**Layout options:**
    84	- Two-column (text left, illustration on right)
    85	- Icon + text rows (icon in colored circle, bold header, description below)
    86	- 2x2 or 2x3 grid (image on one side, grid of content blocks on other)
    87	- Half-bleed image (full left or right side) with content overlay
    88	
    89	**Data display:**
    90	- Large stat callouts (big numbers 60-72pt with small labels below)
    91	- Comparison columns (before/after, pros/cons, side-by-side options)
    92	- Timeline or process flow (numbered steps, arrows)
    93	
    94	**Visual polish:**
    95	- Icons in small colored circles next to section headers
    96	- Italic accent text for key stats or taglines
    97	
    98	### Typography
    99	
   100	**Choose an interesting font pairing** — don't default to Arial. Pick a header font with personality and pair it with a clean body font.
   101	
   102	| Header Font | Body Font |
   103	|-------------|-----------|
   104	| Georgia | Calibri |
   105	| Arial Black | Arial |
   106	| Calibri | Calibri Light |
   107	| Cambria | Calibri |
   108	| Trebuchet MS | Calibri |
   109	| Impact | Arial |
   110	| Palatino | Garamond |
   111	| Consolas | Calibri |
   112	
   113	| Element | Size |
   114	|---------|------|
   115	| Slide title | 36-44pt bold |
   116	| Section header | 20-24pt bold |
   117	| Body text | 14-16pt |
   118	| Captions | 10-12pt muted |
   119	
   120	### Spacing
   121	
   122	- 0.5" minimum margins
   123	- 0.3-0.5" between content blocks
   124	- Leave breathing room—don't fill every inch
   125	
   126	### Avoid (Common Mistakes)
   127	
   128	- **Don't repeat the same layout** — vary columns, cards, and callouts across slides
   129	- **Don't center body text** — left-align paragraphs and lists; center only titles
   130	- **Don't skimp on size contrast** — titles need 36pt+ to stand out from 14-16pt body
   131	- **Don't default to blue** — pick colors that reflect the specific topic
   132	- **Don't mix spacing randomly** — choose 0.3" or 0.5" gaps and use consistently
   133	- **Don't style one slide and leave the rest plain** — commit fully or keep it simple throughout
   134	- **Don't create text-only slides** — add images, icons, charts, or visual elements; avoid plain title + bullets
   135	- **Don't forget text box padding** — when aligning lines or shapes with text edges, set `margin: 0` on the text box or offset the shape to account for padding
   136	- **Don't use low-contrast elements** — icons AND text need strong contrast against the background; avoid light text on light backgrounds or dark text on dark backgrounds
   137	- **NEVER use accent lines under titles** — these are a hallmark of AI-generated slides; use whitespace or background color instead
   138	
   139	---
   140	
   141	## QA (Required)
   142	
   143	**Assume there are problems. Your job is to find them.**
   144	
   145	Your first render is almost never correct. Approach QA as a bug hunt, not a confirmation step. If you found zero issues on first inspection, you weren't looking hard enough.
   146	
   147	### Content QA
   148	
   149	```bash
   150	python -m markitdown output.pptx
   151	```
   152	
   153	Check for missing content, typos, wrong order.
   154	
   155	**When using templates, check for leftover placeholder text:**
   156	
   157	```bash
   158	python -m markitdown output.pptx | grep -iE "\bx{3,}\b|lorem|ipsum|\bTODO|\[insert|this.*(page|slide).*layout"
   159	```
   160	
   161	If grep returns results, fix them before declaring success.
   162	
   163	### Visual QA
   164	
   165	**⚠️ USE SUBAGENTS** — even for 2-3 slides. You've been staring at the code and will see what you expect, not what's there. Subagents have fresh eyes.
   166	
   167	Convert slides to images (see [Converting to Images](#converting-to-images)), then use this prompt:
   168	
   169	```
   170	Visually inspect these slides. Assume there are issues — find them.
   171	
   172	Look for:
   173	- Overlapping elements (text through shapes, lines through words, stacked elements)
   174	- Text overflow or cut off at edges/box boundaries
   175	- Decorative lines positioned for single-line text but title wrapped to two lines
   176	- Source citations or footers colliding with content above
   177	- Elements too close (< 0.3" gaps) or cards/sections nearly touching
   178	- Uneven gaps (large empty area in one place, cramped in another)
   179	- Insufficient margin from slide edges (< 0.5")
   180	- Columns or similar elements not aligned consistently
   181	- Low-contrast text (e.g., light gray text on cream-colored background)
   182	- Low-contrast icons (e.g., dark icons on dark backgrounds without a contrasting circle)
   183	- Text boxes too narrow causing excessive wrapping
   184	- Leftover placeholder content
   185	
   186	For each slide, list issues or areas of concern, even if minor.
   187	
   188	Read and analyze these images — run `ls -1 "$PWD"/slide-*.jpg` and use the exact absolute paths it prints:
   189	1. <absolute-path>/slide-N.jpg — (Expected: [brief description])
   190	2. <absolute-path>/slide-N.jpg — (Expected: [brief description])
   191	...
   192	
   193	Report ALL issues found, including minor ones.
   194	```
   195	
   196	### Verification Loop
   197	
   198	1. Generate slides → Convert to images → Inspect
   199	2. **List issues found** (if none found, look again more critically)
   200	3. Fix issues
   201	4. **Re-verify affected slides** — one fix often creates another problem
   202	5. Repeat until a full pass reveals no new issues
   203	
   204	**Do not declare success until you've completed at least one fix-and-verify cycle.**
   205	
   206	---
   207	
   208	## Converting to Images
   209	
   210	Convert presentations to individual slide images for visual inspection:
   211	
   212	```bash
   213	python scripts/office/soffice.py --headless --convert-to pdf output.pptx
   214	rm -f slide-*.jpg
   215	pdftoppm -jpeg -r 150 output.pdf slide
   216	ls -1 "$PWD"/slide-*.jpg
   217	```
   218	
   219	**Pass the absolute paths printed above directly to the view tool.** The `rm` clears stale images from prior runs. `pdftoppm` zero-pads based on page count: `slide-1.jpg` for decks under 10 pages, `slide-01.jpg` for 10-99, `slide-001.jpg` for 100+.
   220	
   221	**After fixes, rerun all four commands above** — the PDF must be regenerated from the edited `.pptx` before `pdftoppm` can reflect your changes.
   222	
   223	---
   224	
   225	## Dependencies
   226	
   227	- `pip install "markitdown[pptx]"` - text extraction
   228	- `pip install Pillow` - thumbnail grids
   229	- `npm install -g pptxgenjs` - creating from scratch
   230	- LibreOffice (`soffice`) - PDF conversion (auto-configured for sandboxed environments via `scripts/office/soffice.py`)
   231	- Poppler (`pdftoppm`) - PDF to images
   232

content.text:
1	# Editing Presentations
     2	
     3	## Template-Based Workflow
     4	
     5	When using an existing presentation as a template:
     6	
     7	1. **Analyze existing slides**:
     8	   ```bash
     9	   python scripts/thumbnail.py template.pptx
    10	   python -m markitdown template.pptx
    11	   ```
    12	   Review `thumbnails.jpg` to see layouts, and markitdown output to see placeholder text.
    13	
    14	2. **Plan slide mapping**: For each content section, choose a template slide.
    15	
    16	   ⚠️ **USE VARIED LAYOUTS** — monotonous presentations are a common failure mode. Don't default to basic title + bullet slides. Actively seek out:
    17	   - Multi-column layouts (2-column, 3-column)
    18	   - Image + text combinations
    19	   - Full-bleed images with text overlay
    20	   - Quote or callout slides
    21	   - Section dividers
    22	   - Stat/number callouts
    23	   - Icon grids or icon + text rows
    24	
    25	   **Avoid:** Repeating the same text-heavy layout for every slide.
    26	
    27	   Match content type to layout style (e.g., key points → bullet slide, team info → multi-column, testimonials → quote slide).
    28	
    29	3. **Unpack**: `python scripts/office/unpack.py template.pptx unpacked/`
    30	
    31	4. **Build presentation** (do this yourself, not with subagents):
    32	   - Delete unwanted slides (remove from `<p:sldIdLst>`)
    33	   - Duplicate slides you want to reuse (`add_slide.py`)
    34	   - Reorder slides in `<p:sldIdLst>`
    35	   - **Complete all structural changes before step 5**
    36	
    37	5. **Edit content**: Update text in each `slide{N}.xml`.
    38	   **Use subagents here if available** — slides are separate XML files, so subagents can edit in parallel.
    39	
    40	6. **Clean**: `python scripts/clean.py unpacked/`
    41	
    42	7. **Pack**: `python scripts/office/pack.py unpacked/ output.pptx --original template.pptx`
    43	
    44	---
    45	
    46	## Scripts
    47	
    48	| Script | Purpose |
    49	|--------|---------|
    50	| `unpack.py` | Extract and pretty-print PPTX |
    51	| `add_slide.py` | Duplicate slide or create from layout |
    52	| `clean.py` | Remove orphaned files |
    53	| `pack.py` | Repack with validation |
    54	| `thumbnail.py` | Create visual grid of slides |
    55	
    56	### unpack.py
    57	
    58	```bash
    59	python scripts/office/unpack.py input.pptx unpacked/
    60	```
    61	
    62	Extracts PPTX, pretty-prints XML, escapes smart quotes.
    63	
    64	### add_slide.py
    65	
    66	```bash
    67	python scripts/add_slide.py unpacked/ slide2.xml      # Duplicate slide
    68	python scripts/add_slide.py unpacked/ slideLayout2.xml # From layout
    69	```
    70	
    71	Prints `<p:sldId>` to add to `<p:sldIdLst>` at desired position.
    72	
    73	### clean.py
    74	
    75	```bash
    76	python scripts/clean.py unpacked/
    77	```
    78	
    79	Removes slides not in `<p:sldIdLst>`, unreferenced media, orphaned rels.
    80	
    81	### pack.py
    82	
    83	```bash
    84	python scripts/office/pack.py unpacked/ output.pptx --original input.pptx
    85	```
    86	
    87	Validates, repairs, condenses XML, re-encodes smart quotes.
    88	
    89	### thumbnail.py
    90	
    91	```bash
    92	python scripts/thumbnail.py input.pptx [output_prefix] [--cols N]
    93	```
    94	
    95	Creates `thumbnails.jpg` with slide filenames as labels. Default 3 columns, max 12 per grid.
    96	
    97	**Use for template analysis only** (choosing layouts). For visual QA, use `soffice` + `pdftoppm` to create full-resolution individual slide images—see SKILL.md.
    98	
    99	---
   100	
   101	## Slide Operations
   102	
   103	Slide order is in `ppt/presentation.xml` → `<p:sldIdLst>`.
   104	
   105	**Reorder**: Rearrange `<p:sldId>` elements.
   106	
   107	**Delete**: Remove `<p:sldId>`, then run `clean.py`.
   108	
   109	**Add**: Use `add_slide.py`. Never manually copy slide files—the script handles notes references, Content_Types.xml, and relationship IDs that manual copying misses.
   110	
   111	---
   112	
   113	## Editing Content
   114	
   115	**Subagents:** If available, use them here (after completing step 4). Each slide is a separate XML file, so subagents can edit in parallel. In your prompt to subagents, include:
   116	- The slide file path(s) to edit
   117	- **"Use the Edit tool for all changes"**
   118	- The formatting rules and common pitfalls below
   119	
   120	For each slide:
   121	1. Read the slide's XML
   122	2. Identify ALL placeholder content—text, images, charts, icons, captions
   123	3. Replace each placeholder with final content
   124	
   125	**Use the Edit tool, not sed or Python scripts.** The Edit tool forces specificity about what to replace and where, yielding better reliability.
   126	
   127	### Formatting Rules
   128	
   129	- **Bold all headers, subheadings, and inline labels**: Use `b="1"` on `<a:rPr>`. This includes:
   130	  - Slide titles
   131	  - Section headers within a slide
   132	  - Inline labels like (e.g.: "Status:", "Description:") at the start of a line
   133	- **Never use unicode bullets (•)**: Use proper list formatting with `<a:buChar>` or `<a:buAutoNum>`
   134	- **Bullet consistency**: Let bullets inherit from the layout. Only specify `<a:buChar>` or `<a:buNone>`.
   135	
   136	---
   137	
   138	## Common Pitfalls
   139	
   140	### Template Adaptation
   141	
   142	When source content has fewer items than the template:
   143	- **Remove excess elements entirely** (images, shapes, text boxes), don't just clear text
   144	- Check for orphaned visuals after clearing text content
   145	- Run visual QA to catch mismatched counts
   146	
   147	When replacing text with different length content:
   148	- **Shorter replacements**: Usually safe
   149	- **Longer replacements**: May overflow or wrap unexpectedly
   150	- Test with visual QA after text changes
   151	- Consider truncating or splitting content to fit the template's design constraints
   152	
   153	**Template slots ≠ Source items**: If template has 4 team members but source has 3 users, delete the 4th member's entire group (image + text boxes), not just the text.
   154	
   155	### Multi-Item Content
   156	
   157	If source has multiple items (numbered lists, multiple sections), create separate `<a:p>` elements for each — **never concatenate into one string**.
   158	
   159	**❌ WRONG** — all items in one paragraph:
   160	```xml
   161	<a:p>
   162	  <a:r><a:rPr .../><a:t>Step 1: Do the first thing. Step 2: Do the second thing.</a:t></a:r>
   163	</a:p>
   164	```
   165	
   166	**✅ CORRECT** — separate paragraphs with bold headers:
   167	```xml
   168	<a:p>
   169	  <a:pPr algn="l"><a:lnSpc><a:spcPts val="3919"/></a:lnSpc></a:pPr>
   170	  <a:r><a:rPr lang="en-US" sz="2799" b="1" .../><a:t>Step 1</a:t></a:r>
   171	</a:p>
   172	<a:p>
   173	  <a:pPr algn="l"><a:lnSpc><a:spcPts val="3919"/></a:lnSpc></a:pPr>
   174	  <a:r><a:rPr lang="en-US" sz="2799" .../><a:t>Do the first thing.</a:t></a:r>
   175	</a:p>
   176	<a:p>
   177	  <a:pPr algn="l"><a:lnSpc><a:spcPts val="3919"/></a:lnSpc></a:pPr>
   178	  <a:r><a:rPr lang="en-US" sz="2799" b="1" .../><a:t>Step 2</a:t></a:r>
   179	</a:p>
   180	<!-- continue pattern -->
   181	```
   182	
   183	Copy `<a:pPr>` from the original paragraph to preserve line spacing. Use `b="1"` on headers.
   184	
   185	### Smart Quotes
   186	
   187	Handled automatically by unpack/pack. But the Edit tool converts smart quotes to ASCII.
   188	
   189	**When adding new text with quotes, use XML entities:**
   190	
   191	```xml
   192	<a:t>the “Agreement”</a:t>
   193	```
   194	
   195	| Character | Name | Unicode | XML Entity |
   196	|-----------|------|---------|------------|
   197	| `“` | Left double quote | U+201C | `“` |
   198	| `”` | Right double quote | U+201D | `”` |
   199	| `‘` | Left single quote | U+2018 | `‘` |
   200	| `’` | Right single quote | U+2019 | `’` |
   201	
   202	### Other
   203	
   204	- **Whitespace**: Use `xml:space="preserve"` on `<a:t>` with leading/trailing spaces
   205	- **XML parsing**: Use `defusedxml.minidom`, not `xml.etree.ElementTree` (corrupts namespaces)
   206

<!-- Slide number: 2 -->

AGENTIC DEVOPS
The Playbook for Working Engineers

[Your Name]
Senior Principal Trainer & DevOps Advocate

KodeKloud  |  April 2026
KodeKloud

### Notes:
[TIMING: 0:00]

"Before we get into frameworks and tools, I want to start with something that happened two months ago that stopped me mid-scroll."

"Anthropic's Claude Code is now writing 100% of Anthropic's own code. Not assisting. Writing."

[PAUSE — let that register]

"And in the same week, Boris Cherny — the Head of Claude Code — replied to the obvious follow-up: 'So why does Anthropic still have 100 open engineering positions?'"

"His answer: someone has to prompt the models, talk to customers, coordinate teams, and decide what to build next."

[PAUSE]

"That's not a reassurance. That's a job description. And it looks a lot like what strong DevOps engineers already do."

"That's what the next 50 minutes is about. The real question is: what does your job look like on the other side of this shift?"

<!-- Slide number: 3 -->

ONE WEEK. DECEMBER 2025.
All three major cloud platforms shipped autonomous DevOps agents in the same week.

AWS

MICROSOFT

GOOGLE
DevOps Agent
SRE Agent
ADK Expansion
Public Preview
Preview
+4 Major Tools

Autonomous operations
Autonomous operations
Autonomous operations
of DevOps workflows
of DevOps workflows
of DevOps workflows
That's not a coincidence. That's a race. Agentic operations is the next platform layer every major cloud is competing on.
KodeKloud

### Notes:
[TIMING: 1:00]

"In the first week of December 2025, at AWS re:Invent, Microsoft Azure, and Google Cloud events — all three shipped autonomous DevOps agents. In the same week."

"Not AI assistants. Agents. Systems that can autonomously investigate incidents, analyze CI failures, propose infrastructure changes — and in some configurations, execute them."

"That's not a coincidence. That's a race. And every major cloud platform has decided that agentic operations is the next platform layer they're competing on."

"Which means your infrastructure — your Kubernetes clusters, your CI/CD pipelines, your observability stack — is about to become the runtime these agents operate on."

[PAUSE]

"If you're a DevOps engineer who understands those systems, you are not obsolete. You are the foundation."

<!-- Slide number: 4 -->

WHAT'S ACTUALLY HAPPENING RIGHT NOW

1M+

autonomously

is running
of Fortune 100
companies now
using GitHub
Copilot
Source: GitHub, Opsera 2026, Datadog
KodeKloud

### Notes:
[TIMING: 2:00]

"Let me give you the numbers that matter."

"GitHub Copilot's Coding Agent has created over one million pull requests autonomously since May 2025. That's an agent receiving an issue ticket and delivering a PR — without a human writing a single line."

[CLICK] "46% of active Copilot users' code is now AI-generated. Not suggested — generated."

[CLICK] "Here's the number that tells you the real story: those AI-generated PRs wait 4.6 times longer to be reviewed than human-written PRs."

[PAUSE]

"Think about what that means. We solved the code-writing bottleneck with AI — and immediately created a new bottleneck in the outer loop. Review, testing, deployment, incident response."

"That outer loop — that's where DevOps engineers live. And that's exactly where agentic AI is moving next."

<!-- Slide number: 5 -->

THIS WEBINAR

WHAT THIS IS

WHAT THIS ISN'T

✓
A decision framework for working engineers

✕
A tools demo

✓
Grounded in what's confirmed in production right now

✕
Hype about what's possible in 2030

✓
A 90-day plan you can start Monday

✕
A message that your skills are becoming worthless
KodeKloud

### Notes:
[TIMING: 3:30]

"Before we go further, let me tell you exactly what you're going to get and what you're not."

"This is a decision framework. By the end you'll know what agentic DevOps actually is, where the real use cases are, what skills you need to build, and a 90-day plan that starts with one concrete action."

"This is not a vendor demo. I'm going to show you what's confirmed in production at companies like Elastic, Datadog, and Block — not what a vendor says is possible."

[PAUSE]

"And this is absolutely not a message that your Kubernetes skills, your CI/CD expertise, your observability knowledge are becoming worthless. They're the foundation agents run on."

"Let's get into it."

<!-- Slide number: 6 -->

2
WHAT IS AGENTIC DEVOPS,
ACTUALLY
Cutting through the noise with a definition that sticks
KodeKloud

### Notes:
[TIMING: 5:00]

"The phrase 'agentic AI' is everywhere right now. Before we can talk about what to do about it, we need a definition that's actually useful — not marketing language."

<!-- Slide number: 7 -->

THREE WAVES OF AI IN DEVOPS

1
2
3
Pair Programmer
AI-Assisted
Agentic
2021 – 2023
2023 – 2025
2025 – Present

Code completion in your IDE. You're present for every keystroke.
Chat help, code review, vulnerability scanning. Reactive — you ask, it answers.
Autonomous agents operating for hours without oversight. Plans, executes, iterates.

WHERE ARE
YOU NOW?

WHERE MOST
TEAMS ARE TODAY

WHERE
THIS IS GOING
Wave 3: Agents receive a goal and operate independently for hours — planning, executing, and handing off to other agents.
KodeKloud

### Notes:
[TIMING: 5:30]

Wave 1: GitHub Copilot original. Code completion. You're in the IDE, every action has a human in the loop.

[CLICK] Wave 2: where most teams are today. Chat-based assistance, code review, vulnerability scanning. Still reactive.

[CLICK] Wave 3 is what we're talking about. Autonomous agents that receive a goal — 'fix this broken CI build,' 'investigate this incident' — and operate independently for hours.

[PAUSE] The reason this distinction matters: most of the panic about AI taking DevOps jobs is people applying Wave 3 fears to Wave 2 reality. Wave 2 doesn't restructure your job. Wave 3 does. And Wave 3 is here — but it's early, and you have time to get positioned.

<!-- Slide number: 8 -->

WHERE THE BOTTLENECK MOVED

INNER LOOP  (Where AI Started)

OUTER LOOP  (Where AI Is Going)
Code writing
Code review & PR approval
Code writing

CI/CD pipeline management
Code completion
Code completion

Deployment orchestration
Test generation

Test generation
→

Monitoring & alerting

AI
moved
here
Incident response

AI-generated PRs created
48% faster here
Runbook execution

AI-generated PRs waited 4.6× longer to be reviewed — and contained 15–18% more security vulnerabilities. (Opsera, 2026)
KodeKloud

### Notes:
[TIMING: 7:00]

"This diagram is the most important thing I'll show you today. Hold it in your head."

"AI tools solved the inner loop — the time it takes to write code. GitHub Copilot, Claude Code, Cursor — all massively accelerated what happens inside the IDE."

"And immediately created a bigger problem in the outer loop."

Opsera measured this: AI-assisted workflows produced PRs 48% faster. But those PRs waited 4.6x longer to be reviewed. They also contained 15-18% more security vulnerabilities.

[PAUSE]

"The outer loop — code review, CI/CD management, deployment, monitoring, incident response — is the DevOps engineer's domain. Every single item on that list."

[PAUSE]

"Agentic DevOps is the industry's response. Not 'replace DevOps engineers.' Give them AI agents that handle the outer loop at scale."

<!-- Slide number: 9 -->

WHAT AGENTIC DEVOPS ACTUALLY IS

"Autonomous, goal-driven AI systems operating within your existing infrastructure — Kubernetes, CI/CD, observability tooling — that can plan, execute, and iterate on multi-step tasks without constant human direction."

The key phrase:
WITHIN YOUR EXISTING INFRASTRUCTURE.
Agents don't replace your stack. They run on it. The more deeply you understand your infrastructure, the more value you bring to designing and operating these systems.
KodeKloud

### Notes:
[TIMING: 9:00]

"Here's the definition I want you to take from this webinar."

content.text:
{"returncode":0,"stdout":"The pipeline engineer becomes the platform architect.
Designing trust boundaries, building governance systems, defining decision policies — this is architectural work.
KodeKloud

### Notes:
[TIMING: 23:30]

The old version of the DevOps engineer's core value: automate everything, write the scripts, own the pipelines. Wave 1 and 2 thinking.

The new version — visible in job postings right now: design the boundaries, define what agents can see and do, and be able to explain to your CTO why the system made the decisions it made.

[PAUSE]

"I want to be direct: this is not a demotion. If you look at what's actually being asked — designing trust boundaries, building governance systems, defining decision policies — this is architectural work. It requires deep infrastructure expertise."

"The pipeline engineer becomes the platform architect. That's a promotion in responsibility."

"Here are the five skills."

<!-- Slide number: 19 -->

CONTEXT ENGINEERING
1

"What you give the agent shapes everything it does."
Prompt engineering was Wave 2. Context engineering is Wave 3.
AGENTS.md files — per-repo behavior configuration

MCP server definitions — what tools agents can access

Memory stores — what agents remember across sessions

Tool access definitions — what agents are allowed to do

ACTION: Learn MCP. Build one MCP server. Connect your most-used tool.
KodeKloud

### Notes:
[TIMING: 24:30]

Skill one: context engineering. This is the evolution of prompt engineering.

Prompt engineering is asking an AI a good question. Context engineering is designing the environment the agent operates in.

When you deploy an agent in production, you're defining: what files it has access to, what tools it can call, what it remembers between runs, what are the constraints on its behavior. That's all context.

Patrick Debois — the person who coined the term 'DevOps' — said DevOps engineers are the best positioned people in this shift because they know how infrastructure works. He called out building MCP servers as the critical new skill.

[PAUSE]

Your action: learn MCP. Build one MCP server that connects an agent to something you already use — a Grafana dashboard, a PagerDuty incident queue, a Kubernetes cluster.

<!-- Slide number: 20 -->

AGENT BOUNDARY DESIGN
2

"The IAM role an agent assumes is its blast radius."
Every agent needs an explicit permission scope before it touches any pipeline.
Code Gen Agent: Read repo · No write · No execute

Validation Agent: Read infra · Run tf plan only

Deployment Agent: Read all · Write staging · Human gate required

Snyk (Feb 2026): 13.4% of public agent skills had critical vulnerabilities — treat like npm packages

ACTION: For any agent you deploy, define: what it can READ / WRITE / EXECUTE / needs APPROVAL for.
KodeKloud

### Notes:
[TIMING: 26:30]

Skill two: agent boundary design. This is where your existing infrastructure knowledge pays off directly.

'The IAM role an agent assumes is its blast radius.' That's the cleanest summary I've found.

When you design an agentic system, you're making explicit decisions about what that agent can access, modify, and execute — and where a human must approve before anything irreversible happens.

Code generation agent: read-only to the repo. Nothing else. Validation agent can read infrastructure state and run terraform plan — cannot apply. Deployment agent needs a human approval gate before it touches production.

[PAUSE]

Snyk research: 13.4% of public agent skills had critical vulnerabilities. Treat agent plugins the same way you treat npm packages. Audit them. Pin them. Don't install what you don't understand.

Your action: before any agent touches a pipeline, write down those four columns. If you can't fill in the table, you're not ready to deploy.

<!-- Slide number: 21 -->

EVALUATION — THE NEW TESTING DISCIPLINE
3

"Eval-driven development. Same discipline as TDD. Different artifacts."
AWS's published framework for production agent evals:
1. EVALUATIONS — LLM judges + pass@k metrics against your real failure scenarios

2. TRAJECTORY VISUALIZATION — OpenTelemetry-based visualization of every step the agent took

3. FAST FEEDBACK LOOPS — Can you run the eval in minutes, not hours?

4. PRODUCTION SAMPLING — Sample real agent runs and compare to eval expectations

ACTION: Build an eval suite against your 10 most common build/incident failure scenarios before any agent goes to CI.
KodeKloud

### Notes:
[TIMING: 28:30]

Skill three: evaluation — and this is the one most teams skip, to their detriment.

Eval-driven development. Same discipline as TDD. You write the evaluation criteria before you deploy the agent, just like you write the test before you write the code.

AWS published a detailed writeup about building their DevOps Agent to production. Four critical mechanisms are listed here.

Key insight: trajectory visualization — using OpenTelemetry to record every step the agent took, every decision it made, every tool it called. Without this, when the agent does something unexpected in production, you have no way to understand why.

[PAUSE]

One study found developers take 20% longer to resolve issues that AI introduced than original problems — because they start by trusting the AI's output.

Your action: identify your 10 most common CI failure scenarios. Build an eval that tests whether the agent handles each one correctly.

<!-- Slide number: 22 -->

AGENT OBSERVABILITY
4

"You can't govern what you can't see."
Traditional metrics track infrastructure. Agent metrics track reasoning.
Reasoning loop count — is the agent stalling?

Tool call success/failure rate per invocation

Token usage and cost per agent run

Human override frequency — your governance health metric

Tooling now GA: Datadog LLM Observability · New Relic AI Monitoring · Grafana Assistant · OpenTelemetry GenAI SIG

RULE: Add agent observability BEFORE you increase agent autonomy. In that order. Always.
KodeKloud

### Notes:
[TIMING: 30:30]

Skill four: agent observability. This is the DevOps skill you already have, applied to a new class of system.

Traditional observability: latency, error rate, throughput. You know these cold.

Agent-specific observability asks different questions: how many reasoning loops did the agent run? Is it stalling on a problem it can't solve? What's the token cost of this investigation vs. the last one? How often are humans overriding its recommendations?

These are your leading indicators for agent health — different from anything your current dashboards show.

[PAUSE]

Good news: the tooling is here. Datadog, New Relic, Grafana all shipped agent-specific observability products in 2025. OpenTelemetry's GenAI SIG is standardizing the semantic conventions.

The rule: add agent observability before you add agent autonomy. In that order. Always.

<!-- Slide number: 23 -->

MULTI-AGENT ARCHITECTURE DESIGN
5

"Single-agent first. Multi-agent only when you have a specific reason."
The production pattern (AWS DevOps Agent): Incident Commander → specialized sub-agents.
Lead Agent creates investigation plan and delegates tasks

Each sub-agent gets a CLEAN context window (no pollution)

Sub-agents: Metrics Analysis · Log Investigation · Deployment History · Remediation Proposal

Clean context windows prevent one bad assumption from contaminating all downstream decisions

⚠ DON'T START HERE. Master single-agent deployments first. Add multi-agent only when you have a specific, justified reason.
KodeKloud

### Notes:
[TIMING: 32:30]

Skill five: multi-agent architecture design. I'm going to teach you the production pattern — and then tell you not to use it yet.

The pattern: a lead agent acts as 'incident commander.' It receives the goal — 'investigate this production incident' — and breaks it into subtasks. Each subtask goes to a specialized sub-agent. Each sub-agent gets its own clean context window.

Clean context windows are critical. If you give all agents the same shared context, one agent's wrong assumption contaminates every agent downstream.

[PAUSE]

"But here's my honest advice: don't start here."

Multi-agent systems are complex to debug, complex to evaluate, and complex to govern. Every additional agent is another thing that can fail in a non-obvious way.

Master single-agent deployments first. Get your eval suite working. Get your observability in place. Get your boundary design right. Then — and only then — add a second agent when you have a specific, justified reason.

<!-- Slide number: 24 -->

THE FAILURE MODES THAT KILL ADOPTION

HALLUCINATION
PROPAGATION

RETRY LOOPS
Agent introduces a subtle error early in a workflow. Downstream agents trust it. It compounds silently.
Agent hits an ambiguous failure and retries indefinitely without a budget limit. Infinite loop in your pipeline at 3 AM.

Developers take 20% longer to fix AI-introduced errors than original problems.
Fix: explicit retry budgets + circuit breakers.

STATE DRIFT

DEPLOYING
EVERYWHERE DAY 1
Agent builds infrastructure with full context. Weeks later, a diagnostic agent investigates with no context.
The single most common organizational failure. One bad autonomous action in a production system.

No audit trail = slower than a human reading the runbook.
Fix: one use case, one team, prove value, then expand. Very hard to walk back broken trust.
KodeKloud

### Notes:
[TIMING: 34:00]

Before the 90-day plan, here are the four failure modes I see teams hit — knowing what to avoid is as important as knowing what to do.

HALLUCINATION PROPAGATION: Most technically dangerous. An agent produces a subtly wrong output early in a workflow. The next agent trusts it. The error compounds. Developers take 20% longer to resolve AI-introduced errors than original bugs — because they start by trusting the AI.

RETRY LOOPS: Agent can't solve an ambiguous problem, tries again and again. Without explicit retry budgets and circuit breakers, agents spin in infinite loops against your CI pipeline.

STATE DRIFT: Agent builds infrastructure with full context. Weeks later in an incident, a diagnostic agent has no historical context. No audit trail = less useful than just reading the runbook.

DEPLOYING EVERYWHERE DAY ONE: Kills more agentic AI programs than anything else. Gartner, AWS, Opsera all call this out. One bad autonomous agent action in a production system creates distrust that takes months to rebuild.

<!-- Slide number: 25 -->

5
YOUR 90-DAY
GETTING STARTED PLAYBOOK
One use case. One team. Prove it. Then expand.
KodeKloud

### Notes:
[TIMING: 35:00]

Designing trust boundaries, building governance systems, defining decision policies — this is architectural work.
KodeKloud

### Notes:
[TIMING: 23:30]

The old version of the DevOps engineer's core value: automate everything, write the scripts, own the pipelines. Wave 1 and 2 thinking.

The new version — visible in job postings right now: design the boundaries, define what agents can see and do, and be able to explain to your CTO why the system made the decisions it made.

[PAUSE]

"I want to be direct: this is not a demotion. If you look at what's actually being asked — designing trust boundaries, building governance systems, defining decision policies — this is architectural work. It requires deep infrastructure expertise."

"The pipeline engineer becomes the platform architect. That's a promotion in responsibility."

"Here are the five skills."

<!-- Slide number: 19 -->

CONTEXT ENGINEERING
1

"What you give the agent shapes everything it does."
Prompt engineering was Wave 2. Context engineering is Wave 3.
AGENTS.md files — per-repo behavior configuration

MCP server definitions — what tools agents can access

Memory stores — what agents remember across sessions

Tool access definitions — what agents are allowed to do

ACTION: Learn MCP. Build one MCP server. Connect your most-used tool.
KodeKloud

### Notes:
[TIMING: 24:30]

Skill one: context engineering. This is the evolution of prompt engineering.

Prompt engineering is asking an AI a good question. Context engineering is designing the environment the agent operates in.

When you deploy an agent in production, you're defining: what files it has access to, what tools it can call, what it remembers between runs, what are the constraints on its behavior. That's all context.

Patrick Debois — the person who coined the term 'DevOps' — said DevOps engineers are the best positioned people in this shift because they know how infrastructure works. He called out building MCP servers as the critical new skill.

[PAUSE]

Your action: learn MCP. Build one MCP server that connects an agent to something you already use — a Grafana dashboard, a PagerDuty incident queue, a Kubernetes cluster.

<!-- Slide number: 20 -->

AGENT BOUNDARY DESIGN
2

"The IAM role an agent assumes is its blast radius."
Every agent needs an explicit permission scope before it touches any pipeline.
Code Gen Agent: Read repo · No write · No execute

Validation Agent: Read infra · Run tf plan only

Deployment Agent: Read all · Write staging · Human gate required

Snyk (Feb 2026): 13.4% of public agent skills had critical vulnerabilities — treat like npm packages

ACTION: For any agent you deploy, define: what it can READ / WRITE / EXECUTE / needs APPROVAL for.
KodeKloud

### Notes:
[TIMING: 26:30]

Skill two: agent boundary design. This is where your existing infrastructure knowledge pays off directly.

'The IAM role an agent assumes is its blast radius.' That's the cleanest summary I've found.

When you design an agentic system, you're making explicit decisions about what that agent can access, modify, and execute — and where a human must approve before anything irreversible happens.

Code generation agent: read-only to the repo. Nothing else. Validation agent can read infrastructure state and run terraform plan — cannot apply. Deployment agent needs a human approval gate before it touches production.

[PAUSE]

Snyk research: 13.4% of public agent skills had critical vulnerabilities. Treat agent plugins the same way you treat npm packages. Audit them. Pin them. Don't install what you don't understand.

Your action: before any agent touches a pipeline, write down those four columns. If you can't fill in the table, you're not ready to deploy.

<!-- Slide number: 21 -->

EVALUATION — THE NEW TESTING DISCIPLINE
3

"Eval-driven development. Same discipline as TDD. Different artifacts."
AWS's published framework for production agent evals:
1. EVALUATIONS — LLM judges + pass@k metrics against your real failure scenarios

2. TRAJECTORY VISUALIZATION — OpenTelemetry-based visualization of every step the agent took

3. FAST FEEDBACK LOOPS — Can you run the eval in minutes, not hours?

4. PRODUCTION SAMPLING — Sample real agent runs and compare to eval expectations

ACTION: Build an eval suite against your 10 most common build/incident failure scenarios before any agent goes to CI.
KodeKloud

### Notes:
[TIMING: 28:30]

Skill three: evaluation — and this is the one most teams skip, to their detriment.

Eval-driven development. Same discipline as TDD. You write the evaluation criteria before you deploy the agent, just like you write the test before you write the code.

AWS published a detailed writeup about building their DevOps Agent to production. Four critical mechanisms are listed here.

Key insight: trajectory visualization — using OpenTelemetry to record every step the agent took, every decision it made, every tool it called. Without this, when the agent does something unexpected in production, you have no way to understand why.

[PAUSE]

One study found developers take 20% longer to resolve issues that AI introduced than original problems — because they start by trusting the AI's output.

Your action: identify your 10 most common CI failure scenarios. Build an eval that tests whether the agent handles each one correctly.

<!-- Slide number: 22 -->

AGENT OBSERVABILITY
4

"You can't govern what you can't see."
Traditional metrics track infrastructure. Agent metrics track reasoning.
Reasoning loop count — is the agent stalling?

Tool call success/failure rate per invocation

Token usage and cost per agent run

Human override frequency — your governance health metric

Tooling now GA: Datadog LLM Observability · New Relic AI Monitoring · Grafana Assistant · OpenTelemetry GenAI SIG

RULE: Add agent observability BEFORE you increase agent autonomy. In that order. Always.
KodeKloud

### Notes:
[TIMING: 30:30]

Skill four: agent observability. This is the DevOps skill you already have, applied to a new class of system.

Traditional observability: latency, error rate, throughput. You know these cold.

Agent-specific observability asks different questions: how many reasoning loops did the agent run? Is it stalling on a problem it can't solve? What's the token cost of this investigation vs. the last one? How often are humans overriding its recommendations?

These are your leading indicators for agent health — different from anything your current dashboards show.

[PAUSE]

Good news: the tooling is here. Datadog, New Relic, Grafana all shipped agent-specific observability products in 2025. OpenTelemetry's GenAI SIG is standardizing the semantic conventions.

The rule: add agent observability before you add agent autonomy. In that order. Always.

<!-- Slide number: 23 -->

MULTI-AGENT ARCHITECTURE DESIGN
5

"Single-agent first. Multi-agent only when you have a specific reason."
The production pattern (AWS DevOps Agent): Incident Commander → specialized sub-agents.
Lead Agent creates investigation plan and delegates tasks

Each sub-agent gets a CLEAN context window (no pollution)

Sub-agents: Metrics Analysis · Log Investigation · Deployment History · Remediation Proposal

Clean context windows prevent one bad assumption from contaminating all downstream decisions

⚠ DON'T START HERE. Master single-agent deployments first. Add multi-agent only when you have a specific, justified reason.
KodeKloud

### Notes:
[TIMING: 32:30]

Skill five: multi-agent architecture design. I'm going to teach you the production pattern — and then tell you not to use it yet.

The pattern: a lead agent acts as 'incident commander.' It receives the goal — 'investigate this production incident' — and breaks it into subtasks. Each subtask goes to a specialized sub-agent. Each sub-agent gets its own clean context window.

Clean context windows are critical. If you give all agents the same shared context, one agent's wrong assumption contaminates every agent downstream.

[PAUSE]

"But here's my honest advice: don't start here."

Multi-agent systems are complex to debug, complex to evaluate, and complex to govern. Every additional agent is another thing that can fail in a non-obvious way.

Master single-agent deployments first. Get your eval suite working. Get your observability in place. Get your boundary design right. Then — and only then — add a second agent when you have a specific, justified reason.

<!-- Slide number: 24 -->

THE FAILURE MODES THAT KILL ADOPTION

HALLUCINATION
PROPAGATION

RETRY LOOPS
Agent introduces a subtle error early in a workflow. Downstream agents trust it. It compounds silently.
Agent hits an ambiguous failure and retries indefinitely without a budget limit. Infinite loop in your pipeline at 3 AM.

Developers take 20% longer to fix AI-introduced errors than original problems.
Fix: explicit retry budgets + circuit breakers.

STATE DRIFT

DEPLOYING
EVERYWHERE DAY 1
Agent builds infrastructure with full context. Weeks later, a diagnostic agent investigates with no context.
The single most common organizational failure. One bad autonomous action in a production system.

No audit trail = slower than a human reading the runbook.
Fix: one use case, one team, prove value, then expand. Very hard to walk back broken trust.
KodeKloud

### Notes:
[TIMING: 34:00]

Before the 90-day plan, here are the four failure modes I see teams hit — knowing what to avoid is as important as knowing what to do.

HALLUCINATION PROPAGATION: Most technically dangerous. An agent produces a subtly wrong output early in a workflow. The next agent trusts it. The error compounds. Developers take 20% longer to resolve AI-introduced errors than original bugs — because they start by trusting the AI.

RETRY LOOPS: Agent can't solve an ambiguous problem, tries again and again. Without explicit retry budgets and circuit breakers, agents spin in infinite loops against your CI pipeline.

STATE DRIFT: Agent builds infrastructure with full context. Weeks later in an incident, a diagnostic agent has no historical context. No audit trail = less useful than just reading the runbook.

DEPLOYING EVERYWHERE DAY ONE: Kills more agentic AI programs than anything else. Gartner, AWS, Opsera all call this out. One bad autonomous agent action in a production system creates distrust that takes months to rebuild.

<!-- Slide number: 25 -->

5

GETTING STARTED PLAYBOOK
One use case. One team. Prove it. Then expand.
