## SECTION 1: What Is Agentic DevOps, Actually? (10 min)

**Goal:** Cut through the noise with a definition that sticks.

### Three Waves of AI in DevOps

**Wave 1 — Pair Programmer (2021–2023):** Code completion in your IDE. You're present for every keystroke. GitHub Copilot original autocomplete lives here.

**Wave 2 — AI-Augmented (2023–2025):** Multi-step workflows under supervision. Copilot Agent Mode: issues → PRs — but humans gate every merge. AI executes; human approves. This is where most teams are today.

**Wave 3 — Agentic (2025–Present):** Autonomous agents operating for hours without oversight. Plans, executes, iterates. The human moves from **"in the loop"** (approving every action) to **"on the loop"** (monitoring outcomes, intervening only when needed).

### Where the Bottleneck Moved

AI started in the **inner loop** — code writing, code completion, test generation — and created PRs 48% faster. But the data shows AI-generated PRs waited 4.6× longer to be reviewed and contained 15–18% more security vulnerabilities (Opsera, 2026). The speed gain moved the bottleneck downstream.

The **outer loop** is where AI is going: code review and PR approval, CI/CD pipeline management, deployment orchestration, monitoring and alerting, incident response, and runbook execution.

### What Agentic DevOps Actually Is

*"Autonomous, goal-driven AI systems operating within your existing infrastructure — Kubernetes, CI/CD, observability tooling — that can plan, execute, and iterate on multi-step tasks without constant human direction."*

The key phrase: **within your existing infrastructure.** Agents run on your stack, not instead of it.

### Opportunity and Risk — Both Are Real

**The opportunity:**

- Self-healing CI/CD pipelines
- Infrastructure changes from natural language

- Forrester predicts a major breach from agentic AI with insufficient governance will occur in 2026

The IDC 2026 maturity model puts most organizations at Levels 1–2. Fewer than 1% have reached Level 4.

| Level | Description |
|---|---|
| 0 | Manual — no AI in DevOps workflows |
| 1 | AI-Assisted — individual ad hoc tool use, ungoverned |
| 2 | AI-Augmented — AI in pipeline stages, human approval gates |
| 3 | Agent-Embedded — agents with policy-as-code guardrails |
| 4 | Autonomous — self-healing, self-optimizing governed systems |

**Your goal: Level 1 → Level 2 in 90 days.**

The 2025 DORA Report finding that anchors all of this: *"AI doesn't fix a team. It amplifies what's already there."* 90% of respondents use AI at work — but only 17% use autonomous agent mode daily.

**Interactive moment:** Raise your hand (emoji reaction) if your team has already had a conversation about MCP security.

---

- A decision framework for working engineers
- Grounded in what's confirmed in production right now
- A 90-day plan you can start Monday

**What it isn't:**
- A tools demo
- Hype about what's possible in 2030
- A message that your skills are becoming worthless

**Interactive moment:** Quick poll — "Which part of your workflow would you most trust an AI agent with today?" (Code review / CI failure triage / Incident investigation / Deployment approvals / None of them yet)

---

## SECTION 1: What Is Agentic DevOps, Actually? (10 min)

**Goal:** Cut through the noise with a definition that sticks.

### Three Waves of AI in DevOps

**Wave 1 — Pair Programmer (2021–2023):** Code completion in your IDE. You're present for every keystroke. GitHub Copilot original autocomplete lives here.

**Wave 2 — AI-Augmented (2023–2025):** Multi-step workflows under supervision. Copilot Agent Mode: issues → PRs — but humans gate every merge. AI executes; human approves. This is where most teams are today.

**Wave 3 — Agentic (2025–Present):** Autonomous agents operating for hours without oversight. Plans, executes, iterates. The human moves from **"in the loop"** (approving every action) to **"on the loop"** (monitoring outcomes, intervening only when needed).

### Where the Bottleneck Moved

AI started in the **inner loop** — code writing, code completion, test generation — and created PRs 48% faster. But the data shows AI-generated PRs waited 4.6× longer to be reviewed and contained 15–18% more security vulnerabilities (Opsera, 2026). The speed gain moved the bottleneck downstream.

The **outer loop** is where AI is going: code review and PR approval, CI/CD pipeline management, deployment orchestration, monitoring and alerting, incident response, and runbook execution.

### What Agentic DevOps Actually Is

*"Autonomous, goal-driven AI systems operating within your existing infrastructure — Kubernetes, CI/CD, observability tooling — that can plan, execute, and iterate on multi-step tasks without constant human direction."*

The key phrase: **within your existing infrastructure.** Agents run on your stack, not instead of it.

### Opportunity and Risk — Both Are Real

**The opportunity:**
- Autonomous incident triage at scale
- Self-healing CI/CD pipelines
- Infrastructure changes from natural language

**The real risk:**
- Forrester predicts a major breach from agentic AI with insufficient governance will occur in 2026
- Snyk (Feb 2026): 13.4% of public agent skills had critical vulnerabilities; 76 confirmed malicious payloads
- 2025 DORA Report: AI still negatively correlates with delivery stability

### Where Are Teams Actually?

IDC 2026: Only 1% of organizations have reached Level 4. The majority are at Levels 1–2. **Your goal: Level 1 → Level 2 in 90 days.**

| Level | Description |
|---|---|
| 0 | Manual — no AI in DevOps workflows |
| 1 | AI-Assisted — individual ad hoc tool use, ungoverned |
| 2 | AI-Augmented — AI in pipeline stages, human approval gates |
| 3 | Agent-Embedded — agents with policy-as-code guardrails |
| 4 | Autonomous — self-healing, self-optimizing governed systems |

The 2025 DORA Report (5,000 respondents): 90% use AI at work — but only 17% use autonomous agent mode daily. AI still negatively correlates with change failure rate. *"AI doesn't fix a team. It amplifies what's already there."*

**Interactive moment:** Raise your hand (emoji reaction) if your team has already had a conversation about MCP security.

---

## SECTION 2: What's Real Right Now (15 min)

**Goal:** Confirmed in production. Not vendor demos. Four real case studies. Then the tools worth knowing.

### Case Study 1: Elastic — Self-Healing CI/CD Pipeline

**The problem:** 500+ actively updated dependencies in a monorepo. Dependency PRs break builds constantly.

**The pattern:** Build Fails → Agent Analyzes → Proposes Code Fix → Commits Change → Pipeline Restarts

*"So successful it started helping our teams before becoming a fully polished internal feature."* — Elastic Engineering

**Why this matters:** Low blast radius · Immediate visible ROI · Reproducible. **This is your recommended Day 1.**

### Case Study 2: Datadog — AI SRE Agent (GA Dec 2025)

- Autonomous incident investigations across 2,000 production environments
- Forms hypotheses → queries logs, metrics, traces → produces root cause analysis
- Up to 95% reduction in time-to-resolution (vendor-reported ceiling)

**Key insight:** Incident response is the most production-mature agentic DevOps category. Resolve.ai ($1B valuation, Feb 2026), PagerDuty AI Agent Suite, and incident.io are all in production now.

### Case Study 3: Block (Square) — Goose + PagerDuty MCP

- Deployed Goose to 12,000 employees in 8 weeks; ~5,000 use it weekly
- Built a PagerDuty MCP extension during hack week — now a production tool for incident triage and root cause analysis
- *"A production tool that automates triage and root cause analysis across our teams."* — Will Pfleger, Sr. Software Engineer, Block

**Key insight:** Platform engineers who can build MCP integrations are defining how agentic systems work at their companies.

### The Tool Landscape (March 2026)

| Tool | Status | What it does |
|---|---|---|
| **Claude Code** | GA | Opus 4.6, 14.5-hour task horizon, MCP native, 72-plugin marketplace |
| **GitHub Copilot Coding Agent** | GA (Sept 2025) | Assign issues → get draft PRs; Agent HQ runs Claude, Codex + Copilot simultaneously; 90% of Fortune 100 |
| **OpenAI Codex** | GA | GPT-5.4 (Mar 2026), 7+ hour task horizon, GitHub Action for CI/CD; 70% of OpenAI engineers use it internally |
| **LangGraph 1.0** | GA | Most-adopted agent framework, 47M+ PyPI downloads, graph-based orchestration, durable execution; Uber, LinkedIn, Klarna |
| **Argo Workflows** | GA | General-purpose K8s workflow engine — no agent-specific primitives built in (honest assessment) |
| **KAgent** | CNCF Sandbox (Solo.io) | K8s-native agent framework, MCP + A2A native, run agents as K8s CRDs, built for K8s operators |

---

## SECTION 3: The Role Shift (15 min)

**Goal:** Five skills that separate engineers who design agentic systems from engineers who just use AI tools.

### The Job Is Evolving, Not Disappearing

**Old job description:** "Automate everything. Write the scripts. Own the pipelines."

**New job description:** "Design the boundaries. Define what agents can see, do, and affect. Explain why the system made the decisions it made."

This is not a demotion. The pipeline engineer becomes the platform architect. Designing trust boundaries, building governance systems, defining decision policies — this is architectural work.

### Skill 1: Context Engineering

*"What you give the agent shapes everything it does."*

Prompt engineering was Wave 2. Context engineering is Wave 3.

- **AGENTS.md files** — per-repo behavior configuration
- **MCP server definitions** — what tools agents can access
- **Memory stores** — what agents remember across sessions
- **Tool access definitions** — what agents are allowed to do

**Action:** Learn MCP. Build one MCP server. Connect your most-used tool.

### Skill 2: Agent Boundary Design

*"The IAM role an agent assumes is its blast radius."*

Every agent needs an explicit permission scope before it touches any pipeline.

| Agent | Read | Write | Execute |
|---|---|---|---|
| Code Gen Agent | Repo | None | None |
| Validation Agent | Infra | None | `tf plan` only |
| Deployment Agent | All | Staging | Human gate required |

Snyk (Feb 2026): 13.4% of public agent skills had critical vulnerabilities — treat them like npm packages.

**Action:** For any agent you deploy, define what it can READ / WRITE / EXECUTE / what needs APPROVAL.

### Skill 3: Evaluation — The New Testing Discipline

*"Eval-driven development. Same discipline as TDD. Different artifacts."*

AWS's published framework for production agent evals:
1. **Evaluations** — LLM judges + pass@k metrics against your real failure scenarios
2. **Trajectory Visualization** — OpenTelemetry-based visualization of every step the agent took
3. **Fast Feedback Loops** — Can you run the eval in minutes, not hours?
4. **Production Sampling** — Sample real agent runs and compare to eval expectations

**Action:** Build an eval suite against your 10 most common build/incident failure scenarios before any agent goes to CI.

### Skill 4: Agent Observability

*"You can't govern what you can't see."*

Traditional metrics track infrastructure. Agent metrics track reasoning.

- **Reasoning loop count** — is the agent stalling?
- **Tool call success/failure rate** per invocation
- **Token usage and cost** per agent run
- **Human override frequency** — your governance health metric

**Rule:** Add agent observability BEFORE you increase agent autonomy. In that order. Always.

### Skill 5: Multi-Agent Architecture Design

*"Single-agent first. Multi-agent only when you have a specific reason."*

The production pattern (AWS DevOps Agent): Incident Commander → specialized sub-agents.

- Lead agent creates investigation plan and delegates tasks
- Each sub-agent gets a clean context window — no pollution from other agents' assumptions
- Sub-agents: Metrics Analysis · Log Investigation · Deployment History · Remediation Proposal

Clean context windows prevent one bad assumption from contaminating all downstream decisions.

⚠ **Don't start here.** Master single-agent deployments first. Add multi-agent only when you have a specific, justified reason.

### The Four Failure Modes That Kill Adoption

**Hallucination Propagation:** Agent introduces a subtle error early in a workflow. Downstream agents trust it. It compounds silently. Developers take 20% longer to fix AI-introduced errors than original problems.

**Retry Loops:** Agent hits an ambiguous failure and retries indefinitely without a budget limit. Infinite loop in your pipeline at 3 AM. Fix: explicit retry budgets + circuit breakers.

**State Drift:** Agent builds infrastructure with full context. Weeks later, a diagnostic agent investigates with no context. No audit trail = slower than a human reading the runbook.

**Deploying Everywhere Day 1:** The single most common organizational failure. One bad autonomous action in a production system. Fix: one use case, one team, prove value, then expand. Broken trust is very hard to walk back.

---

## SECTION 4: Your 90-Day Getting Started Playbook (10 min)

**Goal:** One use case. One team. Prove it. Then expand.

### Days 1–30: Foundation

**The proven entry point: Automated CI Build Failure Analysis**

The Elastic pattern — agent in advisory mode, human approves the commit.
- Read-only in most implementations — low blast radius
- Clear ROI — measurable time saved per build failure
- Reproducible — no special infrastructure required

**Three parallel actions:**

**1. Build your eval suite first.** Before the agent is deployed. Identify your 10 most common CI failure scenarios. These become your test cases. If you wouldn't deploy code without tests, don't deploy agents without evals.

**2. Define permissions explicitly.** Create a separate service principal. Read-only access only. Document the IAM policy and commit it to the repo.

**3. Form your Agentic AI Pod.** 2–3 engineers with defined ownership across evals, observability, and governance documentation. Someone must be accountable or it drifts.

### Days 31–60: Validation

**Agents in advisory mode only. They recommend. Humans approve. No exceptions.**

Add observability before anything else. Pick one: Datadog LLM Observability, New Relic AI Monitoring, Grafana (GA Oct 2025), or Langfuse (open source).

**Track these four metrics:**
- Agent recommendation accuracy
- False positive rate
- Time saved vs. review time cost
- Human override frequency

**Decision Gate at Day 60:**

≥70% human agreement → ready to expand scope cautiously
<70% human agreement → tune context (not prompts), stay in advisory mode

If you're below 70%, the fix is almost never better prompts. It's bad context or the wrong use case. Diagnose before you iterate.

### Days 61–90: Controlled Expansion

**Add:**
- Second use case: AI-assisted incident triage (read-only data surfacing + hypothesis formation, human confirms before any remediation)
- Cross-team documentation sharing — as a story, not a mandate
- A cadence for prompt tuning and workflow updates

**Not yet:**
- Write access to production
- Autonomous remediation
- Multi-agent systems
- Full pipeline automation

**By Day 90 you will have built:**
- One proven agentic workflow in production (advisory mode)
- An eval suite covering your top failure scenarios
- Agent observability dashboards to show leadership
- IAM documentation for agent permissions committed to the repo
- The data to make the case for expanding scope

That's Level 2. That's ahead of 85%+ of organizations right now.

---

## CLOSING & CTA (5 min)

Your Kubernetes knowledge is the runtime agents operate on. Your CI/CD expertise is the pipeline they're embedded in. Your observability skills are the only way to govern them. Your security knowledge is the boundary design language.

The engineers defining what Agentic DevOps looks like are working on it right now — not in 2028 when there's a certification for it.

**One action before you leave:**

Go to your CI pipeline. Find the last 5 build failures. Ask yourself: "Could an agent have analyzed these and recommended a fix — with me approving the commit?" If yes, you have your first use case. That's your Day 1.

**Resources dropped in chat:**
- 90-Day Agentic DevOps Playbook (PDF)
- Webinar recording link (available within 48 hours)
- KodeKloud Agentic DevOps learning path

---

| 12,000 employees in 8 weeks; 5,000 weekly active users | The New Stack — Angie Jones at All Things Open 2025 | https://thenewstack.io/how-block-got-12000-employees-using-ai-agents-in-two-months/ |
| $1B valuation; $125M Series A; February 4, 2026 | Resolve.ai press release | https://resolve.ai/news/resolveai-raises-125-million-series-a |
| KubeCon EU 2026 Agentics Day | CNCF, March 23–26, RAI Amsterdam | https://events.linuxfoundation.org/kubecon-cloudnativecon-europe/ |

| Linux Foundation AAIF formation, December 2025 | LF AAIF announcement | https://lfaidata.foundation/blog/2025/12/10/introducing-the-agentic-ai-foundation/ |

# Claude Code Spec: Agentic DevOps Webinar Collateral Repo

## Context

This spec builds out the downloadable/shareable collateral for the KodeKloud live webinar:
**"Beyond Copilots: How Agentic AI is Rewriting the DevOps Playbook"**
Live date: April 2026

All output lives under `Agentic_DevOps/` inside the existing KodeKloud repo.
Do not modify anything outside that folder.

---

## Repo Structure to Build

```
Agentic_DevOps/
├── README.md
├── 90-day-playbook/
│   ├── README.md
│   ├── playbook.md
│   └── decision-gate-checklist.md
├── readiness-assessment/
│   ├── README.md
│   └── assessment.md
├── agent-boundary-design/
│   ├── README.md
│   ├── permission-matrix-template.md
│   └── agents-md-template.md
├── eval-framework/
│   ├── README.md
│   └── eval-suite-starter.md
├── stack-reference/
│   ├── README.md
│   └── tool-landscape.md
└── resources.md
```

---

## Authoring Rules (Read Before Writing Anything)

- **Tone:** Written for mid-to-senior DevOps and Platform engineers. No hand-holding, no marketing language. Treat the reader as someone who already knows Kubernetes, CI/CD, and observability.
- **No hype.** Every claim must be either sourced (GitHub, DORA, Opsera, Datadog, Snyk, IDC) or explicitly framed as practitioner opinion.
- **No bullet-point soup.** Use prose for explanations. Use tables and structured lists only where they genuinely aid scanning (permission matrices, checklists, tool comparisons).
- **Every file must be immediately actionable.** If a section can't answer "so what do I do Monday?" it needs to be rewritten or cut.
- **No placeholder filler.** If you don't have enough information to write a section properly, leave a clearly marked `<!-- TODO: [specific question] -->` comment rather than padding.
- **Filenames are kebab-case.** No spaces, no underscores in filenames except the top-level `Agentic_DevOps/` folder name which is fixed.

---

## File-by-File Specifications

---

### `Agentic_DevOps/README.md`

**Purpose:** Entry point. Orients the reader to what's here and why.

**Must include:**
- One-paragraph context: what this webinar covered and why this repo exists
- A table mapping each folder to its use case and who it's for (individual engineer vs. team lead vs. engineering manager)
- A "start here" recommendation based on three reader profiles:
  - "I'm an individual engineer who wants to start with agents"
  - "I'm a team lead trying to assess readiness"
  - "I'm an engineering leader building a business case"
- Links to all sub-folders

**Tone anchor:** Write it like a senior engineer left this for their team on a shared Notion, not like a product landing page.

---

### `Agentic_DevOps/90-day-playbook/README.md`

One paragraph explaining what the playbook is and what it is not. Specifically call out: this is NOT a vendor evaluation guide and NOT a full autonomous deployment plan. It's a governance-first proof of concept path.

- Builds logical narrative throughout

**Updated Slide Count:** 33 slides for 60-minute session (1.8 minutes per slide average)

Required reading before any team builds or consumes an MCP server.
- Linux Foundation Agentic AI Foundation (AAIF) announcement (December 2025) — the governance body for MCP, AGENTS.md, and goose. This is where these standards evolve. Bookmark it so you know when specs change.
- Google Agent2Agent (A2A) Protocol — a2aproject.org — relevant context for teams planning multi-agent architectures at Level 3. Not required for the 90-day playbook but senior engineers will ask about it.

**Do not include:**
- Generic "AI is changing everything" think pieces
- Vendor whitepapers without cited data
- Anything without a date (undated content is useless in this space)

---

## Quality Gates

Before considering any file complete, verify:

1. **No timing information visible anywhere.** Timing guidance belongs in speaker notes only, not in any collateral asset.
2. **Every statistic has a source.** If you can't source it, cut it or flag it with `<!-- UNSOURCED: verify before publishing -->`.
3. **Every file passes the "Monday test":** Can a reader open this file and know what to do this week? If no, it's not done.
4. **No duplicate content across files.** If two files cover the same point, one should reference the other.
5. **The permission matrix template has a filled example.** Templates without examples don't get used.
6. **The eval suite has 10 complete scenarios.** Not 3 with placeholders for the rest.
7. **Run `markdownlint` or equivalent.** Broken link references and malformed tables are embarrassing in public repos.
8. **Version audit before publish.** Grep every generated file for `1.29`, `v1.1`, `GPT-5.3`, `5.3-Codex`. Any hit is a bug. Correct versions: Kubernetes 1.32+ (EKS production floor), Gateway API v1.4.0, OpenAI Codex = GPT-5.4 (March 2026). Flag any version reference you cannot verify with `<!-- VERSION-CHECK: confirm before publishing -->`.

---

## What This Repo Is NOT Responsible For

- The slide deck (separate deliverable, already built)
- KodeKloud course links (add placeholders, do not hardcode URLs that may change)
- Certification roadmap details (out of scope, reference KodeKloud learning paths generally)
- Anything requiring live infrastructure to demonstrate

---

## Final Check

When all files are written, generate a `Agentic_DevOps/README.md` last, after you have full visibility into what was actually built. The README should reflect reality, not the spec.

Agentic DevOps: State of the Field in Early 2026.md
Both are AI-assisted, not agentic — an important distinction the market often blurs.

## Core stack versus adjacent tools — and the Dapr question

The Agentic DevOps stack has a clear core and a fuzzy periphery. Understanding the difference matters because vendors are rapidly relabeling existing products.

**Core Agentic DevOps tools** are purpose-built for autonomous DevOps decision-making: Resolve.ai, Datadog Bits AI (SRE/Dev/Security), PagerDuty AI Agent Suite, Harness AI, Opsera, Kubiya, incident.io, Cleric, and Snyk Agent Fix/Evo. These tools were designed from the ground up (or significantly rebuilt) around agent architectures with autonomous reasoning capabilities.

**Adjacent infrastructure** enables agentic DevOps but is not itself an Agentic DevOps tool. **Dapr Agents is firmly in this category.** Dapr is fundamentally a CNCF distributed application runtime for microservices (state management, pub/sub, service invocation). Dapr Agents, announced March 2025, extends Dapr with an AI agent framework — but it is a *general-purpose framework for building agents*, not an agent that performs DevOps tasks. Dapr co-creator Yaron Schneider said candidly: "Agentic systems is just another tech industry term for 'distributed applications with smarts.'" Co-creator Mark Fussell added: "Rather than calling them microservices, you can call them agents now, mostly because you can put large language models amongst them all." Dapr Agents is excellent infrastructure for *running* agents (virtual actor model, scale-to-zero, 50+ data connectors, Kubernetes-native), but its use cases span customer support, data processing, and many domains beyond DevOps. Think of it as Kubernetes is to cloud-native apps: essential underlying infrastructure, but not a DevOps tool itself.

Similarly adjacent: **Kagent** (Solo.io, CNCF Sandbox) is a Kubernetes-native agent framework closer to DevOps but still a building block, not a product. **Azure AI Foundry** is a general-purpose agent platform. **Dynatrace Davis AI** predates the agentic paradigm — it is established causal AIOps, automated but not agentic in the current sense. **Wiz** focuses on *securing* agentic AI systems rather than *being* an agentic DevOps tool.

## How MCP actually works in DevOps pipelines today

MCP (Model Context Protocol) has evolved from "Anthropic's protocol" to an industry standard under the **Linux Foundation's Agentic AI Foundation**, co-governed by Block, OpenAI, Google, Microsoft, and AWS. With **97 million+ monthly SDK downloads** and **5,000+ servers on GitHub**, it is the dominant agent-to-tool protocol. But its role in DevOps specifically splits into two very different patterns.

**Pattern 1: Developer-in-the-loop (80%+ of current DevOps MCP usage).

This course is for aspiring learners looking to integrate ArgoCD and GitOps principles within their CICD pipelines. We will discuss each concept with a demo followed by practical hands-on exercises, provide simple scripts to set up your Kubernetes ...

Deploy applications using ArgoCD's declarative approach, manage synchronization & rollbacks, configure App of Apps/Helm charts, RBAC/secrets integration, and more. ... Fill this form to get a notification when course is released. ... GitOps is a framework where the entire code delivery process is controlled via Git and can fully manage infrastructure and application definition’s as code. It can be considered an extension of Infrastructure as Code (IaC). The fundamental concept is to use Git commits and pull requests to approve changes while managing resources on Kubernetes. GitOps is not limited to Kubernetes. In principle, GitOps can manage any infrastructure defined as code. In this course, we will look into GitOps and ArgoCD. ArgoCD is a declarative GitOps tool designed for Kubernetes application deployment. ArgoCD is one of the world’s most popular open-source GitOps tools today, and it automates the deployment of the desired applications in the specified Kubernetes target environm

This course is for aspiring learners looking to integrate ArgoCD and GitOps principles within their CICD pipelines. We will discuss each concept with a demo followed by practical hands-on exercises, provide simple scripts to set up your Kubernetes cluster, and have a GitHub repository with various code snippets throughout this course. content.title: GitHub Copilot Agents - KodeKloud
content.text:
GitHub Copilot Agents are domain-specific assistants you can summon in the chat interface by typing @ followed by their names. Each agent is optimized to access relevant contexts—such as your workspace files, terminal history, or editor ...

This guide explores GitHub Copilot Agents and how to control context in prompts for precise AI assistance. ... In this guide, we explore GitHub Copilot Agents and demonstrate how to control context in your prompts for more precise AI assistance. GitHub Copilot Agents are domain-specific assistants you can summon in the chat interface by typing @ followed by their names. Each agent is optimized to access relevant contexts—such as your workspace files, terminal history, or editor settings—to deliver tailored suggestions and code snippets. These agents use advanced natural language processing (NLP) to understand your requests and generate contextually accurate responses. Each agent has unique training data and access scopes—choose the right one for your task to get the best results. To view all available agents, type @ in the Copilot chat prompt.

Mention-based Copilot Agents (@workspace, @terminal, @vscode, etc.) scan specific contexts to deliver targeted assistance. Experimental Agent Mode enables Copilot to autonomously generate code, run commands, and modify files.

Leverage GitHub Copilot Agents to streamline your development workflow and boost productivity.

Open Copilot Chat (Ctrl+Shift+P → Copilot: Open Chat) ... The agent will scan your entire project and highlight relevant functions. For instance: ... def draw_traffic_light(surface, direction, state): """ Draws a circular traffic light for a given direction at a fixed offset from the intersection. """ positions = { 'north': (CENTER_X, STOP_LINE['north'] - LIGHT_OFFSET), 'south': (CENTER_X, STOP_LINE['south'] + LIGHT_OFFSET), 'east': (STOP_LINE['east'] - LIGHT_OFFSET, CENTER_Y), 'west': (STOP_LINE['west'] + LIGHT_OFFSET, CENTER_Y) } pos = positions.get(direction) color = LIGHT_COLORS[state] pygame.draw.circle(surface, color, pos, LIGHT_RADIUS) Switch to the Terminal agent to install and run the app: ... pip3 install pygame python3 -m venv testenv source testenv/bin/activate pip install pygame python main2.py ... content.title: GitHub Copilot: Revolutionize Code Generation with AI
content.text:
GitHub Copilot is an AI-powered code auto-completion tool that integrates seamlessly with popular code editors like Visual Studio Code. It leverages state-of-the-art machine learning techniques and deep learning models, trained on a massive ...

GitHub Copilot is a groundbreaking coding assistant that has been making waves in the development community. Powered by advanced artificial intelligence (AI) models and developed by GitHub, Copilot aims to transform the way we write code. In this comprehensive blog, we will explore the inner workings of GitHub Copilot, its remarkable features, and how it can revolutionize your coding experience. GitHub Copilot is an AI-powered code auto-completion tool that integrates seamlessly with popular code editors like Visual Studio Code. It leverages state-of-the-art machine learning techniques and deep learning models, trained on a massive corpus of code, to provide intelligent code suggestions in real-time. Whether you're working on a personal project or collaborating in a team, Copilot assists you by generating relevant code snippets, function implementations, and even suggesting comments, significantly boosting your productivity.

At its core, Copilot utilizes a language modeling approach to understand and predict the code you want to write. By analyzing the context of your code, including variable names, function signatures, and surrounding code patterns, Copilot generates accurate suggestions that align with your intentions. GitHub Copilot seamlessly integrates with various popular code editors and integrated development environments (IDEs). Whether you prefer Visual Studio Code, JetBrains IDEs, or other widely used editors, you can leverage the power of Copilot within your preferred coding environment. This integration ensures a smooth and familiar experience for developers, enhancing their workflow without the need for significant adjustments. It is built on advanced machine-learning models and benefits from continuous learning and improvement. The models are trained on a vast amount of publicly available code repositories, which enables Copilot to capture diverse coding patterns, idioms, and best practices.

With each iteration and update, Copilot's AI models become more refined, accurate, and adept at generating relevant and high-quality code suggestions. The tool is designed to operate locally, meaning that the code you write stays on your local machine and is not sent to any external servers. This approach ensures that your code remains private and protected. Want to polish your coding skills by “working” in a company and performing real tasks on real systems? Check out KodeKloud Engineer, an environment where students gain real hands-on experience by working on real project tasks on real systems! Below are some of the benefits offered by GitHub Copilot: Increased Productivity: GitHub Copilot automates repetitive coding tasks, saving you time and effort. Providing relevant code suggestions eliminates the need to search for documentation or reference external resources, allowing you to focus on solving more complex problems.

Improved Code Quality: Copilot follows established coding conventions and best practices, helping you write cleaner, more maintainable code. It assists in catching potential bugs, suggests optimal code structures, and promotes consistency throughout your codebase. Faster Prototyping: With Copilot's ability to generate code snippets on the fly, you can quickly prototype ideas and explore different approaches. It accelerates the development process, enabling you to iterate and experiment more efficiently. Learning Resource: GitHub Copilot is valuable, especially for novice developers. By observing the suggestions made by Copilot, you can gain insights into coding patterns, idiomatic expressions, and industry-standard practices, helping you improve your coding skills. Eases learning: Another benefit of GitHub Copilot is that it can significantly reduce the barrier to entry for new developers.

content.title: A Quick Look GitHub Copilot - KodeKloud Notes
content.text:
GitHub Copilot helps both beginners and seasoned developers write less repetitive code and focus on solving problems. GitHub Copilot provides intelligent code suggestions and generates boilerplate code to speed up development.

Comprehensive course notes and guides for cloud technologies, DevOps, Kubernetes, Docker, and more

In this article, we explore GitHub Copilot—a powerful extension for Visual Studio Code—that assists you in developing a small CSV Reader application using Python. We cover setting up your project environment, scaffolding the application, reading and processing a CSV file, and even generating unit tests. GitHub Copilot helps both beginners and seasoned developers write less repetitive code and focus on solving problems. GitHub Copilot provides intelligent code suggestions and generates boilerplate code to speed up development. To begin, install the GitHub Copilot extension in Visual Studio Code. Once installed, you will notice a "ready" status in the lower right-hand corner along with an integrated chat interface for code assistance. For example, if you ask, "How do I create a new Python app?" Copilot might suggest these steps: mkdir my_python_app cd my_python_app python3 -m venv venv source venv/bin/activate # app.py def main(): print("Hello, World!") if __name__ == "__main__": main() 

If you encounter errors such as an undefined attribute (e.g., "AttributeError: 'Namespace' object has no attribute 'csv_file'"), GitHub Copilot can help diagnose and fix these issues by suggesting the correct argument definitions. Simply add the missing argument definition, and Copilot will adjust the code accordingly. GitHub Copilot can also assist by generating unit tests for your code.

In this article, we demonstrated how GitHub Copilot can streamline your development process by helping you: • Scaffold a new Python application • Set up and work within a virtual environment • Read and process CSV file data effectively • Troubleshoot errors with contextual code suggestions • Generate boilerplate unit tests automatically · GitHub Copilot enhances productivity for both beginners and experienced developers by saving time on repetitive code tasks. Next, explore Cursor—a fork of Visual Studio Code offering a uniquely enhanced IDE experience.

content.title: Certified Kubernetes Administrator: Logging & Monitoring
content.text:
Just a quick note: Kubernetes does not have an out-of-the-box complete monitoring solution. However, it integrates with production-capable open-source monitoring solutions such as Metrics Server, Elastic Stack, Prometheus, and many more. There are also proprietary solutions such as Datadog and Dynatrace for enterprise-level monitoring capabilities. The Kubelet service receives instructions from the Kube-API Server on how to run a Pod. It also contains an element known as the container advisor (cAdvisor), which receives Pod performance metrics and exposes them to the Metrics Server through Kube-API Server. You can do that by running the command below: git clone https://github.com/kodekloudhub/kubernetes-metrics-server.git ·

content.title: Horizontal Pod Autoscaler HPA 2025 Updates - KodeKloud Notes
content.text:
Kubernetes supports not only the internal metrics-server for collecting CPU or memory metrics but also custom metrics adapters. These adapters can retrieve metrics from other internal sources or external metrics providers like Datadog or Dynatrace through an external adapter. For further details on advanced configurations, please explore our Kubernetes Autoscaling course. This article provided a comprehensive overview of the Horizontal Pod Autoscaler (HPA) in Kubernetes. We discussed the drawbacks of manual scaling and demonstrated how HPA automates scaling based on real-time resource usage. Whether through imperative commands or declarative YAML configurations, HPA ensures that your applications can adapt dynamically to fluctuating workloads. For additional insights and hands-on experience, consider enrolling in our Kubernetes Autoscaling course.

This article explores the Horizontal Pod Autoscaler in Kubernetes and how it automates workload scaling, improving efficiency over manual scaling methods.

content.title: Monitor Cluster Components - KodeKloud Notes
content.text:
Because Kubernetes does not include a comprehensive built-in monitoring solution, you must implement an external tool. Popular open-source monitoring solutions include Metrics Server, Prometheus, and Elastic Stack. In addition, proprietary options like Datadog and Dynatrace are available for more advanced use cases. Historically, Heapster provided monitoring and analysis support for Kubernetes. Although many reference architectures still mention Heapster, it has been deprecated. Its streamlined successor, Metrics Server, is now the standard for monitoring Kubernetes clusters. Metrics Server is designed to be deployed once per Kubernetes cluster. It collects metrics from nodes and pods, aggregates the data, and retains it in memory. Keep in mind that because Metrics Server stores data only in memory, it does not support historical performance data.

Because Kubernetes does not include a comprehensive built-in monitoring solution, you must implement an external tool. Popular open-source monitoring solutions include Metrics Server, Prometheus, and Elastic Stack. In addition, proprietary options like Datadog and Dynatrace are available for more advanced use cases. Historically, Heapster provided monitoring and analysis support for Kubernetes. Although many reference architectures still mention Heapster, it has been deprecated. Its streamlined successor, Metrics Server, is now the standard for monitoring Kubernetes clusters. Metrics Server is designed to be deployed once per Kubernetes cluster. It collects metrics from nodes and pods, aggregates the data, and retains it in memory. Keep in mind that because Metrics Server stores data only in memory, it does not support historical performance data.

Welcome to this lesson on K8sGPT, a CNCF Sandbox project that brings AI-driven diagnostics and remediation to your Kubernetes clusters. Developed by Alex Jones and his team, K8sGPT is fully open source and available as either a Kubernetes operator or a standalone binary. It connects to your live cluster, gathers resource state across Deployments, Services, ReplicaSets, and more, then leverages AI to pinpoint issues and recommend fixes. K8sGPT integrates seamlessly with major AI providers. You can choose from: Before running the analysis, verify your kubeconfig and context by running kubectl get nodes. Ensure you have network access to both your cluster and the configured AI backend. After configuring your preferred AI provider, simply run: ...

Explanation: The Service selector isn’t matching any Pods. Solution: 1. kubectl get pods -l app=k8sgpt-ollama -n k8sgpt 2. Label Pods if missing: kubectl label pod <pod-name> app=k8sgpt-ollama -n k8sgpt 3: ReplicaSet k8sgpt/k8sgpt-ollama-668396898 - Error: pods "k8sgpt-ollama-668396898-xxxxx" is forbidden: serviceaccount "k8sgpt" not found. Explanation: The ReplicaSet can’t create Pods without a valid ServiceAccount. Solution: 1. Create the ServiceAccount: kubectl create serviceaccount k8sgpt -n k8sgpt 2. Reapply or restart the Deployment/ReplicaSet. This output demonstrates how K8sGPT scans your cluster, identifies common issues across Deployments, Services, and ReplicaSets, and provides clear, actionable remediation steps.

olama@bakugo:~/demo$ k8sgpt analyse --explain 100% AI Provider: openai 0: Deployment k8sgpt/k8sgpt-ollama - Error: Deployment k8sgpt/k8sgpt-ollama has 1 replica but 0 are available. Explanation: The Deployment expects one Pod, but none are running. Solution: 1. Check Pod status: kubectl get pods -n k8sgpt 2. Describe the Pod: kubectl describe pod <pod-name> -n k8sgpt 3. Inspect logs: kubectl logs <pod-name> -n k8sgpt 4. Resolve errors in the Pod spec or logs. 1: Service hosting/web2 - Error: Service has no ready endpoints; expected 2 Pods but none are Ready. Explanation: Pods backing this Service are not passing readiness probes. Solution: 1. kubectl get pods -n hosting 2. kubectl logs <pod-name> -n hosting 3. Verify readinessProbe in the Pod spec. 4. Restart or fix the Pods until they become Ready. 2: Service k8sgpt/k8sgpt-ollama - Error: No endpoints found for label app=k8sgpt-ollama.

input.title: KodeKloud Agentic DevOps Course Catalog: April 2026 Comprehensive Analysis
input.content:
# KodeKloud's Agentic DevOps course catalog in April 2026

**KodeKloud has rapidly built one of the most comprehensive Agentic DevOps curricula available**, with at least **15 courses** directly covering AI agents, MCP, agentic workflows, and AI-assisted development tools — nearly all released in 2025–2026. The platform's AI Learning Path now lists 19 courses, and the broader catalog spans AI-assisted coding, agent frameworks, Kubernetes-native AI, and AIOps. Critically, a **KAgent course does exist** — covering Solo.io's Kagent framework for running AI agents as Kubernetes CRDs — though it may still be in pre-release status as of early April 2026.

---

## The KAgent course exists but may still be launching

**KAgent: Host Your AI Agents on Kubernetes** is listed on KodeKloud's recent courses page and has a dedicated course URL. However, the course page contains a "Fill this form to get a notification when course is released" prompt, suggesting it is either **very recently launched or still in final pre-release** as of April 2026.

- **Title:** KAgent: Host Your AI Agents on Kubernetes
- **URL:** `https://kodekloud.com/courses/kagents-host-your-ai-agents-on-kubernetes`
- **Instructor:** Dipin Thomas (Engineering Manager at ShopBack)
- **Content:** Kagent architecture and installation, UI/dashboard walkthrough, **KMCP** (Kubernetes MCP) installation and MCP server deployment on Kubernetes, agent creation via dashboard, YAML manifests, and BYO agents with LangGraph, **A2A (Agent-to-Agent) communication**, prompt engineering for AI agents, monitoring with Jaeger, debugging labs, real-world integrations with Slack and AWS CloudWatch, public MCP server integration, and GitOps workflows for deploying AI agents
- **Status:** Listed on recent courses page; likely coming soon or just released

This course directly covers the **CNCF Sandbox Kagent project by Solo.io** — including KMCP, CRD-based agent definitions, MCP support, and A2A communication. It is the most Kubernetes-native agentic AI course in KodeKloud's catalog.

---

## Core AI Agents and MCP courses form the foundation

KodeKloud offers three dedicated AI Agents courses and two MCP courses, forming the backbone of the agentic curriculum. All appear to be 2025–2026 releases.

**AI Agents** (`https://kodekloud.com/courses/ai-agents`) — Taught by Gav Ridgeway, this is a practical journey into building autonomous AI agents. It covers agent types, development principles, embeddings, databases, and frameworks. Students build chatbots, multi-agent systems, news aggregators, and resume screeners. It integrates Claude API, Poe, Kubernetes MCP, and Manus AI, and includes advanced projects with audio capabilities and speech translation.

**AI Agents Fundamentals** — Listed on the AI Learning Path (`https://kodekloud.com/learning-path/ai`) with comprehensive course notes at `https://notes.kodekloud.com/docs/AI-Agents-Fundamentals/`. Covers the full agent stack: how LLMs work, embeddings, **LangChain**, prompt engineering, vector databases, semantic search, **RAG**, **LangGraph for stateful AI workflows** (StateGraph, nodes, edges, conditional routing), and a **dedicated MCP module**. This is the closest thing to a standalone LangGraph course on the platform — LangGraph is covered as a major module rather than a separate course.

**Building AI Agents with Claude Agent SDK** (`https://kodekloud.com/courses/learn-by-doing-building-ai-agents-with-claude-agent-sdk`) — Taught by Jaya Sai Krishna Yerramsetty (Principal Platform Architect at KodeKloud). Covers Anthropic's official Claude Agent SDK in Python: the agent loop pattern (Think/Action/Observe/Decide), built-in tools (Bash, Glob, Read, Grep, Edit, MultiEdit, Write), MCP tools for CloudFormation operations, multi-agent systems for AWS infrastructure provisioning, and subagent orchestration.

**MCP For Beginners** (`https://kodekloud.com/courses/mcp-for-beginners`) — Taught by Jeremy Morgan. Introduces Model Context Protocol from first principles: MCP basics, significance, tools/resources/prompts components, running MCP tools, building a basic MCP server, AI integration with Claude, and workflow optimization. Duration approximately **2h 31m**.

**Crash Course: MCP For Beginners** (`https://kodekloud.com/courses/crash-course-mcp-for-beginners`) — Taught by Mumshad Mannambeth (KodeKloud founder/CEO). Goes from MCP basics to building, deploying, and troubleshooting MCP servers and clients, with detailed demos and labs mirroring production scenarios.

A browser-based **MCP Playground** is also available at `https://kodekloud.com/playgrounds/playground-mcp` for hands-on experimentation.

---

## Agentic DevOps and AI-powered operations courses

Two courses directly address the intersection of AI agents and DevOps workflows, using the term "agentic" explicitly in their content.

**Crash Course: AI-Powered DevOps** (`https://kodekloud.com/courses/crash-course-ai-powered-devops`) — Taught by Mumshad Mannambeth. This is KodeKloud's most direct "Agentic DevOps" course. It applies AI across real DevOps workflows: LLM basics, MCP, AI agents, AI-powered CI/CD, a production Kubernetes scenario (P1 outage diagnosis and fix), RAG for knowledge and compliance, an **"Agentic DevOps Team"** module using Docker + Terraform agents, and an **AI Incident Commander** that ingests tickets via MCP and dispatches Cloud Architect and Kubernetes Specialist agents. No standalone course titled "Agentic DevOps" exists, but this course embodies the concept most directly.

**Introduction to K8sGPT and AI-Driven Kubernetes Engineering** (`https://kodekloud.com/courses/introduction-to-k8sgpt-and-ai-driven-kubernetes-engineering`) — Taught by Michael Forrester. A **1-hour** introductory course covering traditional vs. AI-enhanced Kubernetes management, live demos of K8sGPT diagnosing and remediating cluster issues, and a section explicitly titled **"Defining the Agentic Future in DevOps"** addressing emerging roles, skills, and career implications. Course notes available at `https://notes.kodekloud.com/docs/Introduction-to-K8sGPT-and-AI-Driven-Kubernetes-Engineering/`. A companion webinar page exists at `https://kodekloud.com/pages/k8sgpt-and-the-rise-of-ai-agents`.

---

## Claude Code and GitHub Copilot are well covered

**Claude Code For Beginners** (`https://kodekloud.com/courses/claude-code-for-beginners`) — An **8h 32m** hands-on course covering Claude Code as a coding partner: prompting, code reviews, security audits, workflow automation, multi-file codebase navigation, external API/service integration, Claude Code Agents with scoped autonomy, test-driven development, CLI tool building, frontend/backend integration, and CI/CD pipelines with quality gates. Course notes reference Claude Code CLI v1.0.98 and v2.0.37. A **Claude Code Playground** is available at `https://kodekloud.com/playgrounds/playground-claude-code`.

**GitHub Copilot Certification** (`https://kodekloud.com/courses/github-copilot-certification`) — Taught by Jeremy Morgan. Covers AI pair programming, prompt engineering (zero/one/few shot), advanced features including **Copilot agents**, context manipulation, CLI integration, plan management, and practical scenarios.

**GitHub Copilot in Action** (`https://kodekloud.com/courses/github-copilot-in-action`) — Comprehensive hands-on course on using Copilot for AI-enhanced coding in VS Code: code completion, comment-driven development, API development, backend integration, documentation, and unit test generation.

**No courses were found for GitHub Copilot Workspace or the Copilot Coding Agent** specifically. These products are not referenced in any KodeKloud content as of April 2026.

---

## Additional AI and agent framework courses round out the catalog

Several more courses extend KodeKloud's agentic and AI coverage:

- **LangChain** (`https://kodekloud.com/courses/langchain`) — Taught by Janakiram MSV. A **5h 10m** standalone course covering prompts, chains, agents, memory, RAG, and LCEL. **No standalone LangGraph course exists** — LangGraph is covered as a module within AI Agents Fundamentals, and KodeKloud's blog notes they are "closely monitoring" LangGraph for future curriculum.

- **Cline** (`https://kodekloud.com/courses/cline`) — Taught by Jeremy Morgan. Covers the AI-powered VS Code toolkit including autonomous task planning, MCP server integration, MCP Marketplace, model selection, and memory banks. **2h 13m**.

- **Google ADK** (`https://kodekloud.com/courses/google-adk`) — Taught by Jeremy Morgan. Covers Google's Agent Development Kit for building, deploying, and scaling intelligent automation agents. Listed on the recent courses page but appears to still be **coming soon** (notification signup form visible).

- **n8n: Zero to Hero** (`https://kodekloud.com/courses/n8n-zero-to-hero`) — Taught by Marconi. Covers open-source n8n workflows from basics to advanced AI agents, RAG systems, multi-agent orchestration, Pinecone integration, MCP patterns, and production deployments with Docker/AWS.

- **AI Assisted Ansible** (`https://kodekloud.com/courses/ai-assisted-ansible`) — Pairs Ansible with ChatGPT, GitHub Copilot, **Claude Code**, and Red Hat Ansible Lightspeed to generate, debug, and optimize playbooks.

- **AI-Assisted Development** (`https://kodekloud.com/courses/ai-assisted-development`) — Broader course covering ChatGPT, GitHub Copilot, BlackboxAI, Tabnine, and Cursor across full-stack workflows.

- **Cursor AI** (`https://kodekloud.com/courses/cursor-ai`) — Covers Cursor AI for intelligent autocompletion, multi-file editing, and advanced debugging workflows. **3h 48m**.

- **MLOps for AIOps: Build, Track, and Orchestrate AI Models with MLflow & Kubeflow** (`https://kodekloud.com/courses/mlops-for-aiops-build-track-and-orchestrate-ai-models-with-mlflow-kubeflow`) — Takes learners from notebooks to production MLOps on Kubernetes with MLflow and Kubeflow Pipelines.

---

## What KodeKloud does not yet offer

A few notable gaps exist in the catalog as of April 2026:

| Topic | Status |
|---|---|
| **GitHub Copilot Workspace** | No course or content found |
| **GitHub Copilot Coding Agent** | Not covered as a standalone topic |
| **Standalone LangGraph course** | Covered as a module within AI Agents Fundamentals only |
| **Standalone "Agentic DevOps" titled course** | The concept is covered across multiple courses but no single course carries this title |
| **Google ADK** | Course page exists but appears to still be "coming soon" |
| **KAgent course** | Listed on recent courses page but may still be finalizing release |

---

## Conclusion

KodeKloud has built an **impressively broad Agentic DevOps curriculum in a short time**, with the AI Learning Path alone housing 19 courses at `https://kodekloud.com/learning-path/ai`. The most significant finding for this research is that the **KAgent course exists** and directly covers Solo.io's Kubernetes-native agent framework with KMCP, A2A, and MCP support — though its exact availability status should be confirmed directly on the platform. The platform's strongest coverage areas are **MCP** (two dedicated courses plus a playground), **Claude-based agent building** (Claude Code + Claude Agent SDK), and **AI-powered DevOps workflows** (the Crash Course with its Agentic DevOps Team and AI Incident Commander modules). The absence of Copilot Workspace/Coding Agent coverage and a standalone LangGraph course represent the most notable gaps. KodeKloud's pace of AI course releases — with multiple new courses appearing on the recent courses page tagged "New" — suggests these gaps may close in the coming months.
